background_cases: A judge says 'I sentence you to five years' in a courtroom, and the defendant is thereby sentenced.
A bride says 'I do' at her wedding, and a marriage is thereby contracted.
A head of state says 'I declare war,' and a state of war thereby obtains.
definition: A performative utterance succeeds if and only if (i) it is spoken by the appropriate person, (ii) it follows the correct procedure, and (iii) it is uttered in the appropriate circumstances.
conditions: spoken by appropriate person
follows correct procedure
uttered in appropriate circumstances
counterexample: A judge, in the appropriate courtroom, following the correct procedure, sentences a defendant to imprisonment. But the defendant has already been acquitted of the charge by a higher court. The three conditions appear to be met, yet the sentence is void.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved performatives issued in a context free of prior legal nullification. The definition failed to include a condition about the absence of overriding countervailing acts that void the procedure.
passage:
Consider three cases of successful performative utterance. A judge says 'I sentence you to five years' in a courtroom, and the defendant is thereby sentenced. A bride says 'I do' at her wedding, and a marriage is thereby contracted. A head of state says 'I declare war,' and a state of war thereby obtains. From these cases one might extract the following definition: a performative utterance succeeds if and only if it is spoken by the appropriate person, follows the correct procedure, and is uttered in the appropriate circumstances.

Yet consider a case in which a judge, sitting in the proper courtroom and following every procedural requirement, sentences a defendant to imprisonment — but it emerges that the defendant was acquitted of the charge by a higher court the previous day. The judge is the appropriate person, the procedure is correct, and the circumstances appear appropriate. The three conditions are met. Yet the sentence is void; it has no legal effect. The background cases all involved performatives issued in contexts free of prior legal nullification. The definition failed to include a condition about the absence of overriding countervailing acts — acquittals, annulments, revocations — that may render the performative void even when all surface conditions are satisfied.

background_cases: A man six feet tall is clearly tall; a man five feet is clearly not tall.
A head with a full covering of hair is clearly not bald; a completely bare scalp is clearly bald.
A pile of one million grains of sand is clearly a heap; a single grain is clearly not a heap.
definition: A predicate is precise if and only if (i) it divides its domain into two exhaustive classes, (ii) every object in the domain determinately belongs to one class, and (iii) the boundary between the classes can be specified exactly.
conditions: divides domain into two classes
every object determinately belongs to one
boundary specifiable exactly
counterexample: The predicate 'tall' divides people into tall and not-tall, and every person determinately falls into one class at the extremes. But for heights near the boundary, no exact specification is possible — the predicate is vague. Yet 'tall' is a perfectly meaningful predicate.
abductive_insight: The background cases involved clear instances at the extremes, where assignment was unproblematic. The definition assumed that what holds at the extremes holds throughout the domain, but vague predicates have borderline cases where the third condition systematically fails.
passage:
Consider the following observations. A man six feet tall is clearly tall; a man five feet tall is clearly not tall. A head with a full covering of hair is clearly not bald; a completely bare scalp is clearly bald. A pile of one million grains of sand is clearly a heap; a single grain is clearly not a heap. In each case the predicate cleanly divides its domain at the extremes. From these observations one might conclude that a predicate is precise if and only if it divides its domain into two exhaustive classes, every object determinately belongs to one class, and the boundary between the classes can be specified exactly.

But this analysis mistakes what holds at the extremes for what holds throughout the domain. Consider a man five feet ten inches tall. Is he tall? The predicate 'tall' does not determinately assign him to either class. No exact boundary can be drawn: there is no height h such that everyone at least h inches tall is tall and everyone below h is not. The third condition fails, and arguably the second as well. Yet 'tall' is a perfectly meaningful and useful predicate. The background cases all involved clear instances — the extremes where assignment is unproblematic — and the definition was abstracted from these easy cases. It failed to accommodate the borderline region where vague predicates resist precise classification.

background_cases: Asked how his student is doing, a professor writes only 'Mr. X has excellent handwriting.' The reader infers that the student is not a capable philosopher.
A guest, asked whether she is enjoying the wine, replies 'It is certainly red.' The host infers that she is not enjoying it.
A man, asked whether his colleague is a good lecturer, says 'He is always punctual.' The hearer infers that the colleague's lectures are poor.
definition: A speaker conversationally implicates a proposition if and only if (i) the literal content of his utterance does not state the proposition, (ii) the hearer can derive the proposition by assuming the speaker is being cooperative, and (iii) the speaker can cancel the implication by adding further words without contradiction.
conditions: literal content does not state the proposition
derivable by assuming cooperativeness
cancellable without contradiction
counterexample: A man says 'Some of the students passed the examination.' He implicates, by conversational norms, that not all of them passed. The literal content does not state this; the hearer derives it by assuming cooperativeness. Yet if the speaker adds 'Indeed, all of them did,' the addition does not contradict the original statement — the implicature is cancelled. Now suppose another speaker says 'He is a fine friend,' with heavy sarcasm, meaning that the person is no friend at all. The literal content does not state this; the hearer can derive the intended meaning by assuming the speaker is being cooperative in a broader sense. But if the speaker adds 'and I mean that sincerely,' he contradicts the intended (sarcastic) meaning, yet the cancellation is perfectly coherent at the level of literal content. The third condition is ambiguous between cancellation of literal and of intended meaning.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved implicatures generated by deliberate understatement or irrelevance, where cancellation was straightforward. The definition failed to distinguish between literal cancellability (which sarcasm shares) and pragmatic cancellability (which sarcasm may resist).
passage:
Consider the following cases. Asked how his student is doing, a professor writes only that the student has excellent handwriting; the reader infers that the student is not a capable philosopher. A guest, asked whether she enjoys the wine, replies that it is certainly red; the host infers that she does not enjoy it. A man, asked about a colleague's lecturing, says he is always punctual; the hearer infers that the lectures are poor. From these cases one might extract a definition of conversational implicature: a speaker implicates a proposition if and only if the literal content of his utterance does not state it, the hearer can derive it by assuming the speaker is being cooperative, and the speaker can cancel the implication by adding further words without contradiction.

Yet this definition encounters difficulty with sarcasm. A man says 'He is a fine friend,' meaning that the person is a treacherous one. The literal content does not state this; the hearer derives the intended meaning by assuming, in some broad sense, that the speaker is being communicatively cooperative. But can the speaker cancel the sarcastic meaning? If he adds 'and I mean that sincerely,' the literal content is coherent — there is no contradiction at the level of what is said. Yet the pragmatic force of the sarcasm is destroyed in a way quite unlike the cancellation of ordinary implicature. The background cases all involved implicatures generated by deliberate irrelevance or understatement, where adding a clarifying remark simply withdrew the inference. The definition treated cancellability as a uniform operation, but sarcastic meaning resists cancellation in a manner that the standard cases do not.

background_cases: 'Have you stopped taking sugar in your tea?' presupposes that the addressee used to take sugar in his tea.
'The king of France is wise' presupposes that there is a king of France.
'John regrets having missed the lecture' presupposes that John missed the lecture.
definition: A sentence S presupposes a proposition P if and only if (i) the truth of S requires the truth of P, (ii) the negation of S also requires the truth of P, and (iii) the falsity of P renders S neither true nor false.
conditions: truth of S requires truth of P
negation of S requires truth of P
falsity of P renders S truth-valueless
counterexample: Consider the conditional sentence 'If the king of France exists, then the king of France is wise.' The antecedent 'the king of France exists' seems to carry the presupposition that there is a king of France into the conditional. Yet when France has no king, the conditional is arguably true (by vacuous truth of the conditional with a false antecedent) rather than truth-valueless. The third condition fails — the sentence has a definite truth-value even when the presupposed proposition is false. The presupposition has been 'filtered' by the conditional.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all simple sentences in which presuppositions could not be neutralised by the surrounding linguistic context. The definition captured presupposition as it appears in simple sentences but failed to account for the way complex sentence structures — conditionals, disjunctions, modal contexts — can filter or block presuppositions.
passage:
Consider three cases of presupposition. The question 'Have you stopped taking sugar in your tea?' presupposes that the addressee used to take sugar. The sentence 'The king of France is wise' presupposes that there is a king of France. And 'John regrets having missed the lecture' presupposes that John did miss the lecture. In each case, both the sentence and its negation appear to require the truth of the presupposed proposition, and the falsity of that proposition renders the sentence problematic. From these cases one might define presupposition: a sentence S presupposes a proposition P if and only if the truth of S requires the truth of P, the negation of S also requires the truth of P, and the falsity of P renders S neither true nor false.

But consider the sentence 'If the king of France exists, then the king of France is wise.' The embedded clause 'the king of France is wise' presupposes, by the definition, that there is a king of France. Yet the conditional as a whole does not seem to presuppose this. When France has no king, the antecedent is false, and by the standard account of conditionals the whole sentence is vacuously true — not truth-valueless. The presupposition has been absorbed or neutralised by the conditional structure. The background cases were all simple, unembedded sentences; the definition captured what holds in those local environments but failed to anticipate how embedding within conditionals, disjunctions, and other complex structures can filter presuppositions, preventing them from projecting to the level of the whole sentence.

background_cases: 'All bachelors are unmarried' is true in virtue of the meanings of 'bachelor' and 'unmarried.'
'No widow has a living husband' is true in virtue of the meaning of 'widow.'
'All vixens are foxes' is true in virtue of the meaning of 'vixen.'
definition: A statement is analytic if and only if (i) it is true, (ii) its truth can be established by reflecting on the meanings of its terms alone, and (iii) it can be transformed into a logical truth by substituting synonyms.
conditions: the statement is true
truth established by meanings alone
transformable into logical truth by synonym substitution
counterexample: Consider 'Every event has a cause.' Many philosophers have held this to be true and knowable by reflection on the concepts of event and cause. Its denial seems, to some, to be inconceivable. Yet it cannot be transformed into a logical truth by any straightforward substitution of synonyms, for 'cause' is not synonymous with any logical expression. The third condition fails, yet the statement has been regarded by many as analytic.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved definitional truths whose key terms admitted of straightforward synonymy relations — 'bachelor' means 'unmarried man,' 'vixen' means 'female fox.' The definition assumed that all analytic truths rest on such synonym pairs, but some apparently analytic truths resist reduction to logical form because their key concepts lack neat synonyms.
passage:
Consider three statements. 'All bachelors are unmarried' is true in virtue of the meaning of 'bachelor.' 'No widow has a living husband' is true in virtue of the meaning of 'widow.' 'All vixens are foxes' is true in virtue of the meaning of 'vixen.' In each case, one can transform the statement into a logical truth by replacing a term with its synonym: 'bachelor' with 'unmarried man,' 'widow' with 'woman whose husband has died,' 'vixen' with 'female fox.' From these cases one might define analyticity: a statement is analytic if and only if it is true, its truth can be established by reflecting on the meanings of its terms, and it can be transformed into a logical truth by substituting synonyms.

Yet consider the statement 'Every event has a cause.' A long tradition in philosophy has held this to be true and indeed necessary — its denial has been thought inconceivable. If one reflects upon the concept of an event, it may seem that being caused is part of what it is to be an event. The first two conditions are, on this view, satisfied. But the third condition fails: one cannot transform 'Every event has a cause' into a logical truth by substituting synonyms, for 'cause' has no synonym that reduces the statement to a tautology. The background cases all turned on simple definitional relations — 'bachelor' is defined as 'unmarried man' — and the definition assumed that all analytic truths rest upon such neat synonym pairs. The concept of analyticity, it appears, is wider than the method of synonym substitution can reach.

background_cases: 'I promise to return the book tomorrow.' By uttering this sentence, the speaker makes a promise.
'I hereby name this ship the Queen Elizabeth.' By uttering this sentence, the speaker names the ship.
'I apologise for my lateness.' By uttering this sentence, the speaker performs the act of apologising.
definition: An utterance is performative if and only if (i) it accomplishes the act it describes, (ii) it employs a first-person performative verb in the present tense, and (iii) its felicity depends upon the satisfaction of certain conventional conditions (such as the speaker's authority or sincerity).
conditions: accomplishes the act it describes
employs first-person performative verb in present tense
felicity depends on conventional conditions
counterexample: A bishop says 'I excommunicate you' to a member of a religion in which the bishop holds no authority. The utterance employs a first-person performative verb in the present tense and is intended to accomplish the act of excommunication. The conventional conditions — the bishop's authority — are not met, and the act is void. Yet now consider a bystander at a wedding who whispers to his neighbour 'I bet you five pounds they divorce within a year.' He accomplishes the act of betting; the felicity depends upon his neighbour's acceptance. But the utterance is informal, spontaneous, and arguably non-performative in the traditional sense — it more closely resembles a declaration of intent than a formal performance.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all formal, institutional performatives in which a recognised authority employed a conventional formula. The definition captured this institutional character but failed to accommodate informal performatives — bets, warnings, compliments — whose performative force arises from conversational practice rather than from institutional convention.
passage:
Consider three cases of performative utterance. 'I promise to return the book tomorrow': by uttering this, the speaker makes a promise. 'I hereby name this ship the Queen Elizabeth': by uttering this, the speaker names the ship. 'I apologise for my lateness': by uttering this, the speaker apologises. From these cases one might define a performative utterance as one that accomplishes the act it describes, employs a first-person performative verb in the present tense, and whose felicity depends upon the satisfaction of certain conventional conditions — the speaker's authority, sincerity, and the appropriateness of the occasion.

Yet this definition, drawn from formal and institutional settings, proves too narrow. Consider a man who, at a race meeting, turns to his companion and says 'I bet you five pounds the grey horse wins.' He accomplishes the act of betting by his words; the felicity of the bet depends upon his companion's acceptance and upon his having the means to pay. But the utterance is informal and spontaneous — there is no institutional framework of the kind that governs promising, naming, or apologising in their fullest ceremonial forms. The performative force arises from conversational practice, from the mutual understanding of what it is to place a wager, rather than from any institutional convention. The background cases were all specimens of formal performativity — promises, namings, apologies — and the definition captured the institutional and conventional character of these acts. It failed, however, to accommodate the large class of informal performatives whose force derives from ordinary conversational exchange rather than from ceremony or authority.

background_cases: 'I am tired' expresses different propositions depending on who utters it.
'It is raining here' expresses different propositions depending on the location of utterance.
'That is a fine painting' expresses different propositions depending on which painting is demonstrated.
definition: An expression is context-dependent if and only if (i) its content varies with the context of utterance, (ii) the contextual parameter it is sensitive to belongs to a fixed and enumerable set (speaker, time, place, demonstrated object), and (iii) the linguistic meaning of the expression provides a function from contexts to contents.
conditions: content varies with context
sensitive to a fixed enumerable parameter
linguistic meaning provides function from contexts to contents
counterexample: Consider the sentence 'John has had enough.' Depending on the conversational context, this may mean that John has had enough food, enough wine, enough argument, or enough provocation. The content varies with context, and the linguistic meaning of 'enough' provides some guidance — it requires a contextually supplied standard and dimension. But the relevant parameter is not drawn from any fixed, enumerable set such as speaker, time, or place; it is the open-ended conversational topic, which cannot be antecedently listed. The second condition fails, yet the expression is plainly context-dependent.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved indexicals and demonstratives whose contextual parameters — speaker, location, demonstrated object — form a closed and well-understood set. The definition assumed that all context-dependence is of this tidy indexical variety, but many expressions are sensitive to open-ended conversational parameters that resist enumeration.
passage:
Consider three cases of context-dependence. 'I am tired' expresses different propositions depending upon who utters it; the relevant contextual parameter is the speaker. 'It is raining here' depends upon the place of utterance. 'That is a fine painting' depends upon which painting is demonstrated. From these cases one might define context-dependence: an expression is context-dependent if and only if its content varies with the context of utterance, the parameter it is sensitive to belongs to a fixed and enumerable set — speaker, time, place, demonstrated object — and the linguistic meaning of the expression provides a function from contexts to contents.

But this definition, drawn from indexicals and demonstratives, proves too restrictive. Consider the sentence 'John has had enough.' In a dinner-party context, this means John has had enough food; in a quarrel, that he has had enough provocation; at the pub, that he has had enough to drink. The content varies with context, and the linguistic meaning of 'enough' provides some structure — it demands a standard and a dimension. But the relevant contextual parameter is the conversational topic, and this is not drawn from any fixed, enumerable set. One cannot antecedently list all the dimensions along which 'enough' may vary, for they are as various as the situations in which the word may be employed. The background cases involved indexicals sensitive to tidy, well-understood parameters. The definition captured this neat variety of context-dependence but could not accommodate the open-ended sensitivity of ordinary expressions like 'enough,' 'ready,' and 'tall,' whose contextual parameters resist prior enumeration.

background_cases: The sentence 'The cat sat on the mat' has a meaning composed from the meanings of 'the cat,' 'sat,' 'on,' and 'the mat' in accordance with their syntactic arrangement.
The sentence 'Every student passed the examination' has a meaning composed from its parts: the quantifier 'every,' the noun 'student,' the verb 'passed,' and the object 'the examination.'
The sentence 'The red ball is on the table' has a meaning derivable from the meanings of its constituent words and their syntactic combination.
definition: The meaning of a complex expression is compositional if and only if (i) it is a function of the meanings of its immediate constituents, (ii) the function is determined by the syntactic rule used to combine them, and (iii) the meaning of each constituent is itself context-independent.
conditions: meaning is function of immediate constituent meanings
function determined by syntactic combination rule
each constituent meaning is context-independent
counterexample: The word 'bank' in 'The bank was steep' means a river bank, while in 'The bank was closed' it means a financial institution. The meaning of the constituent 'bank' depends on the context provided by the surrounding words. The third condition fails: the meaning of the constituent is not context-independent but is determined in part by the sentential context in which it occurs.
abductive_insight: The background cases all used words with single, unambiguous meanings. The definition assumed that constituent meanings are fixed independently of context, but lexical ambiguity is pervasive in natural language, and the resolution of ambiguity depends upon the very sentential context that compositionality is meant to explain.
passage:
Consider three compositional sentences. 'The cat sat on the mat' derives its meaning from the meanings of its parts and their arrangement. 'Every student passed the examination' is understood by combining the quantifier, noun, verb, and object in accordance with English syntax. 'The red ball is on the table' has a meaning that follows from its constituent words and their combination. From these cases one might hold that compositionality requires that the meaning of a complex expression be a function of the meanings of its immediate constituents, the function be determined by the syntactic rule of combination, and the meaning of each constituent be itself context-independent.

But the third condition encounters a pervasive difficulty. Consider the word 'bank.' In the sentence 'The bank was steep,' it means the sloping side of a river. In the sentence 'The bank was closed,' it means a financial institution. The meaning of the constituent 'bank' is not fixed independently of the sentence in which it occurs; it is disambiguated by the surrounding context — 'steep' points towards the river sense, 'closed' towards the financial sense. The third condition fails. The background cases all used words with single, unambiguous meanings, and the definition assumed that this was the general case. But lexical ambiguity is pervasive in natural language, and the resolution of ambiguity depends upon the sentential context, introducing a circularity that the simple compositional picture does not acknowledge: the meaning of the whole depends on the meanings of the parts, but the meanings of the parts may depend on the meaning of the whole.

background_cases: The name 'Aristotle' denotes the ancient philosopher; we use the name to refer to a man who exists in history.
The name 'London' denotes the capital of England; the name picks out a city that exists in geography.
The description 'the author of Hamlet' denotes Shakespeare; the description identifies a man who exists and who wrote the play.
definition: A singular term successfully denotes if and only if (i) there exists an object to which the term refers, (ii) the object satisfies the descriptive content of the term, and (iii) the term fails to denote if the object does not exist.
conditions: object to which term refers exists
object satisfies descriptive content
term fails to denote if object does not exist
counterexample: The name 'Vulcan' was introduced by the astronomer Le Verrier to refer to a hypothetical planet between Mercury and the Sun. No such planet exists. The first condition fails. Yet the name 'Vulcan' was used meaningfully in astronomical discourse for decades, and sentences containing it — 'Vulcan has not been observed' — appear to be meaningful and even true. The term does not denote any existing object, yet it functions semantically.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved terms that successfully referred to existing objects. The definition assumed that meaningful reference requires existence, but empty names — names that refer to nothing — are used meaningfully in scientific hypotheses, fiction, and everyday discourse, and a theory of denotation must account for them.
passage:
Consider three cases of denotation. 'Aristotle' denotes the ancient philosopher. 'London' denotes the capital of England. 'The author of Hamlet' denotes Shakespeare. In each case, the name or description picks out an object that exists. From these cases one might define successful denotation: there exists an object to which the term refers, the object satisfies the descriptive content of the term, and the term fails to denote if the object does not exist.

But this definition, drawn from cases where the referent exists, encounters difficulty with empty names. Consider 'Vulcan,' the name introduced by Le Verrier in the nineteenth century for a hypothetical planet between Mercury and the Sun, postulated to explain perturbations in Mercury's orbit. No such planet exists; the perturbations were later explained by general relativity. The first condition fails: there is no object to which 'Vulcan' refers. Yet 'Vulcan' was used meaningfully in astronomical discourse for decades. Sentences such as 'Vulcan has not been observed' appear to be meaningful and true. If the term fails to denote, as the third condition states, then it is unclear how sentences containing it can be meaningful at all. The background cases all involved existing referents; the definition assumed that reference requires existence. But empty names present a genuine puzzle: they seem to refer and yet there is nothing for them to refer to, and any adequate account of denotation must explain how this is possible.

background_cases: The sentence 'Snow is white' has a clear literal meaning: it says that the substance snow has the colour white.
The sentence 'Water boils at 100 degrees Celsius' has a clear literal meaning: it states a physical fact about water.
The sentence 'Paris is the capital of France' has a clear literal meaning: it identifies a city as the seat of government.
definition: A sentence has a determinate literal meaning if and only if (i) the meaning is fixed by its words and grammar, (ii) the meaning is the same for every competent speaker, and (iii) the literal meaning is always the primary meaning the speaker intends.
conditions: meaning fixed by words and grammar
same for every competent speaker
literal meaning always primary intended meaning
counterexample: When a host at a dinner party says 'It is cold in here,' the literal meaning — a statement about the temperature — is fixed by the words and grammar, and every competent speaker would assign the same literal content. But the primary meaning the speaker intends may well be the request that someone close the window. The third condition fails: the literal meaning is not the intended meaning but a vehicle for a pragmatic implication.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all declarative statements where the speaker's intended meaning coincided with the literal content. The definition treated this coincidence as universal, but in conversational practice the literal meaning frequently serves as a stepping-stone to a non-literal intended meaning, and the primary communicative force may reside in what is implied rather than in what is said.
passage:
Consider three sentences with clear literal meanings. 'Snow is white' literally says that snow has the colour white. 'Water boils at 100 degrees Celsius' literally states a physical fact. 'Paris is the capital of France' literally identifies a city as a seat of government. In each case, the speaker's intended meaning coincides with the literal content. From these cases one might hold that a sentence has a determinate literal meaning if and only if the meaning is fixed by words and grammar, it is the same for every competent speaker, and the literal meaning is always the primary meaning the speaker intends.

But this definition encounters a pervasive difficulty in conversational practice. Consider a host at a dinner party who says 'It is cold in here.' The literal meaning is fixed by the words and grammar: the sentence describes the temperature of the room. Every competent speaker of English would assign the same literal content. But the primary meaning the speaker intends is not the literal one; it is the implicit request that someone close the window. The guests understand this perfectly and act accordingly. The third condition fails: the literal meaning is present but subordinate; the communicative work is done by the pragmatic implication. The background cases were all instances where literal and intended meaning coincided; the definition took this coincidence to be universal. But in everyday conversation, literal meaning often serves merely as a vehicle for conveying what the speaker really intends, and the primary communicative force lies not in what is said but in what is meant.

background_cases: The sentence 'Socrates is mortal' has the logical form Fa: a predicate applied to an individual.
The sentence 'All men are mortal' has the logical form of a universally quantified conditional: for all x, if x is a man then x is mortal.
The sentence 'Some dogs are brown' has the logical form of an existentially quantified conjunction: there exists an x such that x is a dog and x is brown.
definition: A sentence's logical form is adequately captured if and only if (i) the form can be expressed in the notation of first-order predicate logic, (ii) the translation preserves the sentence's inferential role, and (iii) every meaningful sentence has a translation into first-order logic.
conditions: form expressible in first-order predicate logic
translation preserves inferential role
every meaningful sentence has first-order translation
counterexample: The sentence 'There are more cats than dogs' is meaningful and has a determinate inferential role. But it cannot be expressed in first-order predicate logic without recourse to counting or cardinality predicates that are not part of the standard first-order apparatus. The sentence requires second-order resources or special extensions. The third condition fails, yet the sentence is perfectly meaningful.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all sentences whose logical forms are straightforwardly expressible in first-order logic. The definition treated first-order logic as the universal medium of logical form, but certain meaningful sentences — those involving cardinality comparisons, generics, or adverbial modification — require expressive resources that exceed first-order logic.

Consider three sentences and their logical forms. 'Socrates is mortal' has the form of a predicate applied to an individual. 'All men are mortal' has the form of a universally quantified conditional. 'Some dogs are brown' has the form of an existentially quantified conjunction. Each is readily translated into first-order predicate logic. From these cases one might hold that a sentence's logical form is adequately captured if and only if the form can be expressed in first-order notation, the translation preserves the sentence's inferential role, and every meaningful sentence has a first-order translation.

But consider the sentence 'There are more cats than dogs.' It is perfectly meaningful: every competent speaker understands it, and it has a determinate inferential role — from it, together with 'There are at least ten dogs,' one can infer 'There are at least eleven cats.' Yet the sentence cannot be expressed in standard first-order predicate logic. First-order logic lacks the resources to compare the cardinalities of two sets without recourse to counting predicates or set-theoretic apparatus that are not part of the basic first-order framework. The third condition fails. The background cases were all sentences whose logical forms fit comfortably within first-order logic; the definition assumed this was universally the case. But first-order logic, for all its power, does not exhaust the forms of meaningful assertion, and certain sentences require second-order or higher-order resources for the adequate representation of their logical structure.

background_cases: 'Socrates is wise' ascribes the property of wisdom to an individual who possesses or lacks the property in a definite way.
'The table is brown' ascribes a colour to a physical object that either has or lacks that colour.
'Seven is prime' ascribes a mathematical property to a number that either satisfies or fails to satisfy the predicate.
definition: A predication is well-formed if and only if (i) a property is ascribed to a subject, (ii) the subject either determinately possesses or determinately lacks the property, and (iii) the predication is therefore either true or false.
conditions: property ascribed to subject
subject determinately possesses or lacks property
predication either true or false
counterexample: Consider the predication 'The man with exactly 500 hairs on his head is bald.' Is a man with 500 hairs bald? He is not clearly bald and not clearly not bald; the predicate 'bald' admits of borderline cases. The second condition fails: the subject does not determinately possess or determinately lack the property. And if the subject is a borderline case, the third condition — bivalence — is also in jeopardy.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved predicates with sharp boundaries — wisdom in its conventional ascription, colour, primality. The definition assumed that all predicates yield determinate verdicts, but vague predicates like 'bald,' 'tall,' and 'rich' admit of borderline cases where it is indeterminate whether the property is possessed.
passage:
Consider three predications. 'Socrates is wise' ascribes wisdom to an individual. 'The table is brown' ascribes a colour to an object. 'Seven is prime' ascribes a mathematical property to a number. In each case, the subject either determinately possesses or determinately lacks the property, and the predication is straightforwardly true or false. From these cases one might hold that a predication is well-formed if and only if a property is ascribed to a subject, the subject determinately possesses or lacks the property, and the predication is therefore either true or false.

But this definition, drawn from cases with sharp boundaries, encounters the phenomenon of vagueness. Consider a man with exactly five hundred hairs on his head. Is he bald? He is not clearly bald — a man with five hundred hairs has some hair. But he is not clearly not bald either — five hundred hairs do not constitute a full head of hair. The predicate 'bald' admits of borderline cases, and this man is one of them. The second condition fails: the subject does not determinately possess or determinately lack the property. If the property-ascription is indeterminate, then the third condition — that the predication is either true or false — is also in question. The background cases all involved predicates with sharp boundaries; the definition assumed that this was the universal condition. But vague predicates are pervasive in natural language, and they reveal that predication does not always yield a determinate verdict.

background_cases: A scientist at a conference says 'The boiling point of water at standard pressure is 100 degrees Celsius'; she asserts a proposition she believes and is prepared to defend.
A historian writes 'The Battle of Hastings took place in 1066'; he asserts a proposition on the basis of evidence he has carefully weighed.
A witness in court says 'I saw the defendant enter the building at nine o'clock'; she asserts a proposition she takes to be true on the basis of her observation.
definition: A speech act is a genuine assertion if and only if (i) the speaker believes the proposition expressed, (ii) the speaker has adequate grounds for the proposition, and (iii) the speaker takes responsibility for the truth of the proposition.
conditions: speaker believes proposition expressed
speaker has adequate grounds
speaker takes responsibility for truth
counterexample: A mathematics professor writes on the blackboard 'Assume for contradiction that the square root of 2 is rational.' She does not believe this proposition — she believes the opposite, that the square root of 2 is irrational. She has no grounds for the proposition, for she knows it to be false. Yet her utterance functions as an assertion within the proof: it enters the chain of reasoning and is treated as an asserted premise until the contradiction is derived. The first and second conditions fail, yet the speech act has the force of an assertion within its logical context.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all straightforward assertions of propositions the speaker believed to be true. The definition treated belief and adequate grounds as necessary conditions, but the practice of reductio ad absurdum requires the assertion of propositions the speaker knows to be false, as a strategic move within a proof.
passage:
Consider three assertions. A scientist says 'The boiling point of water at standard pressure is 100 degrees Celsius'; she believes it and is prepared to defend it. A historian writes 'The Battle of Hastings took place in 1066'; he asserts it on the basis of evidence. A witness in court says 'I saw the defendant enter the building'; she takes responsibility for its truth. From these cases one might define genuine assertion: the speaker believes the proposition expressed, has adequate grounds for it, and takes responsibility for its truth.

But this definition, drawn from cases of sincere factual assertion, fails to accommodate a form of assertion that is essential to mathematical and philosophical reasoning. Consider a professor conducting a proof by reductio ad absurdum. She writes on the blackboard: 'Assume for contradiction that the square root of 2 is rational.' She does not believe this proposition; she knows it to be false. She has no evidential grounds for it; her grounds point the other way. Yet within the structure of the proof, her utterance functions as an assertion — it is entered as a premise, submitted to the rules of inference, and used to derive a contradiction. The first and second conditions fail. The background cases were all straightforward sincere assertions; the definition treated sincerity and adequate grounds as essential. But the reductio strategy requires the deliberate assertion of propositions the speaker disbelieves, and this form of assertion is neither insincere nor defective but is a disciplined logical technique essential to rigorous reasoning.

background_cases: The sentence 'The cat is on the mat' is meaningful because 'cat,' 'mat,' and 'on' each have specifiable senses, and these senses combine compositionally to yield a proposition with determinate truth-conditions.

The sentence 'Iron is denser than aluminium' is meaningful because 'iron,' 'aluminium,' 'denser,' and 'than' each contribute a specifiable semantic value, and the compositional rules of English yield a proposition that is determinately true or false.

The sentence 'No bachelor is married' is meaningful because the senses of its component words combine to yield a proposition whose truth-conditions are perfectly determinate — indeed, it is true in all possible circumstances.
definition: A sentence S is meaningful if and only if (i) each word in S has an independently specifiable semantic value, (ii) these values combine according to the compositional rules of the language to yield a determinate proposition, and (iii) the proposition has definite truth-conditions.
conditions: each word in S has an independently specifiable semantic value
these values combine according to the compositional rules of the language to yield a determinate proposition
the proposition has definite truth-conditions
counterexample: Consider the sentence 'Colourless green ideas sleep furiously.' Each word has an independently specifiable semantic value: 'colourless,' 'green,' 'ideas,' 'sleep,' and 'furiously' are all legitimate English words with clear dictionary entries. The sentence is grammatically well-formed; the compositional rules of the language yield what appears to be a proposition. Yet the resulting proposition, if it is one, does not have definite truth-conditions in any ordinary sense: what would it be for ideas to sleep, or for sleeping to be done furiously? The second and third conditions are satisfied only in a formal, syntactic sense — the sentence is well-formed — but the semantic values clash in such a way that no coherent proposition emerges. Nevertheless, the sentence is not wholly devoid of meaning; it has been the subject of extensive discussion, it can be interpreted poetically or metaphorically, and speakers have strong intuitions about what it does not mean.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all sentences whose words combined smoothly and without semantic clash — ordinary declarative sentences about cats, iron, and bachelors. The definition abstracted compositionality and determinate truth-conditions as essential to meaningfulness because these features were present in every case examined. But the cases were drawn from a narrow range of semantically harmonious sentences, and the definition mistook the smooth functioning of compositionality in unproblematic cases for a necessary condition of meaningfulness as such. The Chomsky sentence reveals that meaningfulness does not require the successful production of a determinate proposition with clear truth-conditions; a sentence may be meaningful — interpretable, discussable, semantically provocative — even when its component semantic values resist coherent combination. What is at work is a broader notion of linguistic meaning that encompasses not only well-behaved compositional meaning but also the semantic tension generated by category violations.
passage:
Consider three sentences that are uncontroversially meaningful. 'The cat is on the mat' derives its meaning from the independently specifiable senses of 'cat,' 'mat,' and 'on,' which combine compositionally to yield a proposition with perfectly determinate truth-conditions. 'Iron is denser than aluminium' likewise derives its meaning from the semantic values of its components, which combine to yield a proposition that is determinately true. 'No bachelor is married' is meaningful for the same reason: the senses of the words combine to yield a proposition true in all possible circumstances. From these cases one might extract a definition: a sentence is meaningful if and only if each of its words has an independently specifiable semantic value, these values combine compositionally to yield a determinate proposition, and the proposition has definite truth-conditions.

But one must consider the sentence 'Colourless green ideas sleep furiously.' Each word in this sentence has a clear and independently specifiable semantic value; one may look up 'colourless,' 'green,' 'ideas,' 'sleep,' and 'furiously' in any competent dictionary. The sentence is grammatically impeccable — a noun phrase followed by a verb phrase, with adjectives modifying a noun and an adverb modifying a verb. The compositional machinery of English processes the sentence without syntactic complaint. Yet the result is deeply puzzling. What would it be for ideas to be simultaneously colourless and green? What would it be for ideas to sleep, and for this sleeping to be done furiously? The semantic values of the words, though individually clear, clash when combined: adjectives that require a coloured subject are applied to abstract entities, a verb denoting a biological process is predicated of non-biological things. The proposition that emerges, if it is a proposition at all, does not have truth-conditions in any ordinary sense. And yet the sentence is not meaningless. It has been discussed by linguists, philosophers, and poets; it can be interpreted metaphorically or surreally; speakers have firm intuitions that it differs profoundly from a string of random words. The background cases were all semantically harmonious sentences in which compositionality operated without friction, and the definition mistook this frictionless operation for a necessary condition of meaningfulness. The Chomsky sentence reveals that a sentence may be meaningful — semantically charged, interpretable, worthy of discussion — even when the compositional combination of its parts yields not a coherent proposition but a productive semantic tension.

background_cases: The name 'Bertrand Russell' refers to Bertrand Russell because it was bestowed upon him at birth, and each subsequent user of the name acquired it through a chain of communication tracing back to that initial naming.

The name 'Queen Victoria' refers to Queen Victoria because the name was officially bestowed upon her and has been transmitted through an unbroken chain of usage in historical, legal, and ordinary discourse.
The name 'Napoleon' refers to Napoleon Bonaparte because the name traces back, through a causal-historical chain, to the original bestowal of the name upon the child born in Corsica in 1769.
definition: A proper name N refers to an individual x if and only if (i) N was originally bestowed upon x in an act of naming, (ii) there exists an unbroken causal-historical chain of communicative uses linking the current speaker's use of N to that original act, and (iii) x exists or once existed as a concrete individual.
conditions: N was originally bestowed upon x in an act of naming
there exists an unbroken causal-historical chain of communicative uses linking the current speaker's use of N to that original act
x exists or once existed as a concrete individual
counterexample: Consider the name 'Vulcan,' introduced by the astronomer Le Verrier to refer to a hypothesised planet between Mercury and the Sun. The name was bestowed in an act of naming; there is an unbroken chain of communicative uses linking current speakers to Le Verrier's original introduction of the name. Yet Vulcan does not exist and never existed; the hypothesised planet was never found, and subsequent work in physics explained the perturbations of Mercury's orbit without it. The third condition fails. Yet 'Vulcan' functions as a referring name in discourse: astronomers and historians of science use it to discuss the hypothesis, to explain what Le Verrier was seeking, and to describe the failure of the search. The name refers, in some meaningful sense, to the entity Le Verrier intended to name, even though that entity does not and never did exist.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all names of individuals who existed or once existed — Russell, Victoria, Napoleon. The definition abstracted the existence of the named individual as a necessary condition of reference because every name in the sample referred to an actual person. But the sample was biased toward successful reference to real individuals, and the definition mistook a feature of these cases for a general requirement. The case of 'Vulcan' reveals that names can be introduced with referential intent, transmitted through causal chains, and used in meaningful discourse even when the intended referent does not and never did exist. What the definition fails to accommodate is the possibility of empty names — names that function referentially in the linguistic community despite the non-existence of their intended bearers.
passage:
Consider three names whose reference is uncontroversial. 'Bertrand Russell' refers to the philosopher born in 1872, the name tracing back through a causal chain to the original bestowal upon the infant. 'Queen Victoria' refers to the monarch who reigned from 1837 to 1901, the name transmitted through generations of usage. 'Napoleon' refers to the Corsican general and emperor, the name connected to him through an unbroken history of communicative use. From these cases one might extract a definition: a proper name refers to an individual if and only if it was originally bestowed upon that individual, there is an unbroken causal-historical chain of uses linking the current speaker to the original bestowal, and the individual exists or once existed as a concrete entity. The three background cases satisfy all three conditions admirably.

But one must consider the name 'Vulcan.' In the nineteenth century, the astronomer Le Verrier, seeking to explain certain irregularities in the orbit of Mercury, hypothesised the existence of a small planet orbiting between Mercury and the Sun, and bestowed upon this hypothesised body the name 'Vulcan.' The name was introduced in an act of naming; it entered scientific discourse and has been transmitted through an unbroken chain of communicative uses from Le Verrier's time to the present. Astronomers, historians of science, and philosophers of language all use the name 'Vulcan' to discuss the hypothesis, to describe the search for the planet, and to explain why the search failed. Yet Vulcan does not exist and never did. The perturbations of Mercury's orbit were eventually explained by the general theory of relativity, and no intra-Mercurial planet was ever observed. The third condition of the definition fails entirely. The background cases were all names of actual individuals, and the definition abstracted existence as a necessary condition of naming because it was present in every case examined. The case of 'Vulcan' reveals that a name may be bestowed, transmitted through a causal chain, and used referentially in meaningful discourse even though the intended bearer does not exist. The concept of naming is broader than the definition allows: it encompasses empty names — names that are introduced with referential intent and that function as referring expressions in the linguistic community despite having no actual referent.

background_cases: A host says to a guest, 'Please sit down,' and the guest recognises this as an invitation and sits. The utterance is a directive speech act whose force is immediately transparent to both parties.
A friend says, 'Could you pass the salt?' and the hearer passes the salt without hesitation. The request is understood as a directive, and compliance follows at once.
A sign at the entrance to a park reads 'Keep off the grass,' and visitors comply, recognising the utterance as a prohibition. The speech act is performed impersonally but effectively.
definition: An utterance U has the illocutionary force of a directive if and only if (i) U is addressed by a speaker S to a hearer H, (ii) S intends H to perform (or refrain from performing) some action A, and (iii) H recognises S's communicative intention and responds accordingly.
conditions: U is addressed by a speaker S to a hearer H
S intends H to perform or refrain from performing some action A
H recognises S's communicative intention and responds accordingly
counterexample: Consider the case of a letter placed in a bottle and cast into the sea. The writer inscribes: 'If you find this, please send word to my family at the address below.' The utterance has the force of a request — a directive. Yet at the time of writing, there is no determinate hearer; the writer addresses no particular individual. The bottle may never be found, in which case no one will recognise any communicative intention or respond accordingly. The first and third conditions fail: there is no hearer at the time of utterance, and there may never be one. Yet the utterance has directive force; if found, it will be recognised as a request, and the finder may comply. The speech act is performed even though the conditions of addressee-specificity and uptake are not satisfied at the moment of utterance.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all face-to-face or proximally mediated communicative exchanges in which a speaker addressed a determinate hearer who was present and responsive — a host and guest, friends at table, visitors reading a sign. The definition abstracted addressee-specificity and immediate uptake as essential to directive force because these features were shared by all the cases examined. But the cases were drawn exclusively from situations of co-present, synchronous communication. The message in the bottle reveals that a directive speech act may be performed in the absence of a determinate addressee, without any guarantee of uptake, and across an indefinite temporal gap. The definition mistook the features of situated, face-to-face communication for the necessary conditions of illocutionary force as such. What the bottle reveals is that the force of a directive can be inscribed in language and await its recipient, and that speech acts are not confined to the moment and context of utterance.
passage:
Consider three cases of directive speech acts. A host says to a guest, 'Please sit down,' and the guest, recognising the invitation, takes a seat. A friend at dinner asks, 'Could you pass the salt?' and the hearer complies without a moment's hesitation. A sign at the entrance to a public park reads 'Keep off the grass,' and visitors, recognising the prohibition, avoid the lawn. From these cases one might extract a definition: an utterance has the illocutionary force of a directive if and only if it is addressed by a speaker to a hearer, the speaker intends the hearer to perform or refrain from some action, and the hearer recognises the intention and responds accordingly. The background cases all satisfy these conditions: in each case there is a determinate addressee, a clear communicative intention, and successful uptake.

But one must consider the message in the bottle. A sailor, shipwrecked on a remote island, writes upon a scrap of paper: 'If you find this, please send word to my family at the address below.' The sailor seals the paper in a bottle and casts it into the sea. The utterance has the force of a request — a directive. The sailor intends whoever finds the bottle to perform an action: to contact the family. But at the time of writing there is no determinate hearer. The bottle drifts upon the ocean; it may be found in a week, a year, a century, or never. The sailor addresses no particular individual; the directive is cast into the void, aimed at whoever chance may bring to the bottle's resting place. If the bottle is never found, the third condition is never satisfied — no one recognises the communicative intention, no one responds. Yet the directive force of the utterance is not thereby nullified; the request exists, inscribed in language, awaiting a reader. And if the bottle is found, the finder will recognise the utterance as a request and may comply, even though the conditions of addressee-specificity and immediate uptake were not satisfied at the moment the speech act was performed. The background cases were all instances of synchronous, co-present communication, and the definition mistook the features of such communication for the necessary conditions of directive illocutionary force. The message in the bottle shows that speech acts may be deferred, addressee-indeterminate, and temporally unbounded, and that their force inheres in the language itself, not merely in the circumstances of its production.

background_cases: The name 'London' refers to a single, determinate city on the Thames, and speakers use it without ambiguity to pick out that city.
The name 'Aristotle' refers to a single, determinate philosopher born in Stagira, and scholarly discourse employs the name to pick out that individual without confusion.
The name 'Mount Everest' refers to a single, determinate mountain on the border of Nepal and Tibet, and geographers use the name to pick out that peak exclusively.
definition: A proper name N functions correctly if and only if (i) N picks out exactly one individual or entity, (ii) every competent speaker who uses N uses it to pick out the same individual or entity, and (iii) the referent of N is fixed and does not shift across contexts of use.
conditions: N picks out exactly one individual or entity
every competent speaker who uses N uses it to pick out the same individual or entity
the referent of N is fixed and does not shift across contexts of use
counterexample: Consider the name 'John Smith.' This is among the most common names in the English-speaking world; there are thousands of individuals who bear it. The name does not pick out exactly one individual; it is shared by a multitude. Different speakers using 'John Smith' may use it to pick out different persons — one speaker's 'John Smith' is a neighbour in Bristol, another's is a colleague in Melbourne. And the referent shifts across contexts: in one conversation 'John Smith' refers to the plumber, in another to the barrister. All three conditions fail. Yet 'John Smith' is a perfectly good proper name; it functions correctly in each particular context, and speakers manage to communicate successfully by relying on contextual cues to disambiguate.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all names with unique, famous, and unambiguous referents — 'London,' 'Aristotle,' 'Mount Everest.' The definition abstracted uniqueness, inter-speaker agreement, and context-invariance as essential to the correct functioning of proper names because these features were present in every case examined. But the cases were drawn from a sample of celebrated names with single, culturally salient bearers. The name 'John Smith' reveals that a name may function correctly — may serve its communicative purpose — even when it is shared by thousands of individuals, when different speakers use it to refer to different people, and when its referent shifts from one conversational context to another. The definition confused the features of uniquely borne, culturally prominent names with the necessary conditions for the correct functioning of names as such. What is required is not uniqueness and invariance but the capacity, in a given context, to enable the hearer to identify the intended referent.
passage:
Consider three proper names whose functioning seems unproblematic. 'London' picks out a single, determinate city; every speaker uses it to refer to the same metropolis on the Thames; its referent does not shift across contexts. 'Aristotle' picks out a single philosopher; scholarly discourse employs the name without confusion. 'Mount Everest' picks out a single mountain; geographers and mountaineers use it univocally. From these cases one might extract a definition: a proper name functions correctly if and only if it picks out exactly one individual, every competent speaker uses it for the same individual, and the referent is fixed across all contexts of use. The background cases all satisfy these conditions, and the definition appears to identify the essential features of properly functioning names.

But one must consider the name 'John Smith.' This name is among the most common in the English-speaking world. There are, at any given time, thousands of living individuals who bear it. The name does not pick out exactly one individual; it is shared by a vast multitude. Different speakers who use 'John Smith' may refer to entirely different persons — one speaker's John Smith is a neighbour in a village in Somerset, another's is a colleague in an office in Sydney, a third's is a character in a novel. The referent shifts from one conversational context to another: in one exchange, 'John Smith' denotes the plumber who came on Tuesday; in the next, it denotes the barrister who handled the case. All three conditions of the definition fail. Yet 'John Smith' is a perfectly good proper name. It appears on birth certificates, on passports, on legal documents. In each particular context of use, speakers succeed in communicating by means of it — relying upon shared conversational background, situational cues, and mutual understanding to identify the intended referent. The definition was drawn from a sample of names with unique, culturally celebrated bearers, and it mistook the features of such names for the necessary conditions of correct naming. What the name 'John Smith' reveals is that the communicative function of a name does not require uniqueness or context-invariance but the capacity, within a given conversational setting, to enable the hearer to identify whom or what the speaker means.

background_cases: The metaphor 'Juliet is the sun' invites the hearer to see Juliet in terms of the sun — as radiant, warm, life-giving — by mapping properties of the sun onto Juliet.

The metaphor 'Life is a journey' invites the hearer to see life in terms of a journey — with beginnings, destinations, crossroads, and detours — by mapping the structure of journeys onto the structure of lives.

The metaphor 'The mind is a computer' invites the hearer to see the mind in terms of computation — with inputs, processing, storage, and outputs — by mapping the architecture of computers onto cognitive processes.
definition: A metaphor M is cognitively valuable if and only if (i) M maps the structure of a source domain onto a target domain, (ii) the mapping illuminates previously unnoticed features of the target domain, and (iii) the illumination can, in principle, be cashed out as a set of literal propositions about the target domain.
conditions: M maps the structure of a source domain onto a target domain
the mapping illuminates previously unnoticed features of the target domain
the illumination can, in principle, be cashed out as a set of literal propositions about the target domain
counterexample: Consider the metaphor 'Music is liquid architecture.' This metaphor maps the structure of architecture — with its spatial forms, proportions, and structural tensions — onto music. It illuminates something about music: that musical compositions have form, proportion, and structural integrity analogous to those of buildings. But the illumination resists being cashed out as a set of literal propositions. One cannot specify, in non-metaphorical terms, precisely which features of architecture correspond to which features of music; the metaphor gestures at a likeness that is real and cognitively valuable but that dissolves when one attempts to state it literally. The third condition fails, yet the metaphor is genuinely illuminating — indeed, musicians and critics have found it indispensable.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all metaphors whose cognitive content could be at least partially paraphrased in literal terms — Juliet is radiant, life has stages and destinations, the mind processes information. The definition abstracted the possibility of literal paraphrase as a necessary condition of cognitive value because in each case a rough literal equivalent could be supplied. But the cases were drawn from a class of relatively transparent metaphors, and the definition mistook the paraphrasability of these particular metaphors for a general requirement. The metaphor 'Music is liquid architecture' reveals that a metaphor may be deeply and genuinely illuminating — may disclose a real structural analogy that enriches understanding — even when no adequate literal paraphrase is available. The cognitive value of the metaphor consists precisely in its capacity to make something visible that literal language cannot express, and the demand for literal cashability mistakes a feature of some metaphors for a feature of all.
passage:
Consider three metaphors acknowledged to be cognitively valuable. 'Juliet is the sun' maps properties of the sun — radiance, warmth, centrality — onto Juliet, illuminating her beauty and importance. 'Life is a journey' maps the structure of a journey — beginnings, paths, crossroads, destinations — onto a human life, revealing the purposive and directional character of lived experience. 'The mind is a computer' maps computational architecture onto cognition, highlighting the processing, storage, and retrieval of information. From these cases one might extract a definition: a metaphor is cognitively valuable if and only if it maps the structure of a source domain onto a target domain, the mapping illuminates previously unnoticed features of the target, and the illumination can in principle be cashed out as a set of literal propositions about the target domain. The background cases all appear to satisfy these conditions: one can, at least approximately, say in literal terms what each metaphor conveys.

But one must consider the metaphor 'Music is liquid architecture.' This metaphor, attributed in various forms to several writers and musicians, maps the structure of architecture — spatial form, proportion, the play of tension and resolution, the balance of parts within a whole — onto music. It illuminates something real and important about music: that musical compositions possess a kind of formal integrity, a structural logic, analogous to that of great buildings. Musicians and critics have found the metaphor indispensable; it captures an aspect of musical experience that resists expression in other terms. Yet when one attempts to cash out the illumination as a set of literal propositions, the enterprise falters. One cannot specify, in non-metaphorical language, precisely which architectural properties correspond to which musical properties, or in what the alleged liquidity of musical architecture consists. The analogy is real — one feels its force upon hearing a Bach fugue unfold with the inevitability of a Gothic vault — but it is an analogy that exists, as it were, only within the metaphor. The third condition fails. The background cases were metaphors whose cognitive content could be roughly paraphrased, and the definition mistook this feature for a necessary condition of metaphorical value. What the architecture metaphor reveals is that a metaphor may be cognitively indispensable precisely because it discloses a likeness that literal language cannot capture — that the value of the metaphor may lie in its irreducibility to literal paraphrase.

background_cases: The predicate 'is tall' is vague: as one considers persons of incrementally increasing height, there is no determinate point at which the predicate begins to apply.
The predicate 'is bald' is vague: as one considers heads with incrementally fewer hairs, there is no sharp boundary between the bald and the non-bald.
The predicate 'is a heap' is vague: removing grains of sand one by one from a heap, there is no determinate point at which the heap ceases to be a heap.
definition: A predicate F is vague if and only if (i) F applies to objects arranged along a single linear dimension, (ii) small incremental changes along that dimension never appear sufficient to alter the applicability of F, and (iii) the vagueness of F is exhausted by this one-dimensional sorites susceptibility.
conditions: F applies to objects arranged along a single linear dimension
small incremental changes along that dimension never appear sufficient to alter the applicability of F
the vagueness of F is exhausted by this one-dimensional sorites susceptibility
counterexample: Consider the predicate 'is a city.' It is vague — there are settlements that are borderline cases, not clearly cities and not clearly not-cities. But the vagueness of 'city' does not arise from a single linear dimension. A settlement's claim to being a city depends upon its population, its geographical extent, its density, the complexity of its infrastructure, its administrative status, its cultural and economic role, and a host of further considerations. The vagueness is multidimensional: a settlement may be large in population but diffuse in density, or compact and dense but administratively a town. There is no single dimension along which small incremental changes generate the sorites susceptibility. All three conditions fail, yet the predicate is genuinely vague.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all predicates whose vagueness arises along a single, easily identifiable dimension — height for 'tall,' number of hairs for 'bald,' number of grains for 'heap.' The definition abstracted one-dimensional sorites susceptibility as the essential feature of vagueness because it was present in every case examined. But the cases were drawn from a particularly simple class of vague predicates, and the definition mistook the structure of this class for the structure of vagueness in general. The predicate 'is a city' reveals that vagueness may be multidimensional — arising from the interaction of several independent variables, none of which individually generates a sorites series. The definition captured the mechanism of simple, scalar vagueness and mistook it for the whole of vagueness, when in truth many of the most important vague predicates in natural language are vague in ways that no single dimension can exhaust.
passage:
Consider three predicates whose vagueness is widely acknowledged. 'Is tall' is vague because, as one considers persons of incrementally increasing height, there is no determinate point at which the predicate begins to apply. 'Is bald' is vague because, as hairs are removed one by one, there is no sharp transition from non-bald to bald. 'Is a heap' is vague because, as grains are subtracted one by one, the heap never determinately ceases to be a heap at any single step. From these cases one might extract a definition: a predicate is vague if and only if it applies to objects arranged along a single linear dimension, small incremental changes along that dimension never appear sufficient to alter applicability, and the vagueness is exhausted by this one-dimensional sorites susceptibility.

But one must consider the predicate 'is a city.' This predicate is vague — there exist settlements of which it is genuinely uncertain whether they are cities or merely large towns — and its vagueness has been the occasion of philosophical and administrative perplexity. Yet the vagueness of 'city' does not arise along a single linear dimension. Whether a settlement is a city depends upon a multiplicity of factors: its population, certainly, but also its geographical extent, the density of its habitation, the complexity of its infrastructure, its administrative and legal status, its economic and cultural role, the character of its public institutions, and doubtless further considerations besides. A settlement with a large population but dispersed over a vast rural area may not be a city; a compact and densely populated settlement may have the character of a city despite a modest population. The vagueness is multidimensional, arising from the interaction of these many variables rather than from incremental changes along any one of them. There is no single dimension along which a sorites series can be constructed. The background cases were all predicates whose vagueness is grounded in a single, easily isolated scalar dimension, and the definition abstracted this unidimensional character as essential. The predicate 'is a city' reveals that vagueness may be far more complex — that it may arise from the confluence of multiple independent considerations, none of which is individually decisive, and that the concept of vagueness must be broad enough to encompass this multidimensional structure.

background_cases: A French scientific paper on optics is translated into English, and the translation preserves every proposition, every logical relation, and every empirical claim. The original and the translation are regarded as conveying the same content.
A German legal contract is translated into English, and every clause, condition, and obligation is faithfully rendered. The translation is regarded as legally equivalent to the original.

A Spanish instruction manual for a machine is translated into English, and every procedural step is accurately reproduced. The translation enables an English-speaking reader to operate the machine exactly as the Spanish original does.
definition: A translation T of a text X is adequate if and only if (i) T preserves the propositional content of X, (ii) T preserves the logical and inferential structure of X, and (iii) the translated text serves the same practical purpose as the original.
conditions: T preserves the propositional content of X
T preserves the logical and inferential structure of X
the translated text serves the same practical purpose as the original
counterexample: Consider the translation of a sonnet by Petrarch from Italian into English. A translation that preserved every proposition, every logical relation, and every element of inferential structure — that rendered the semantic content with perfect fidelity — might nonetheless fail to be an adequate translation of the sonnet if it lost the rhyme scheme, the metre, the musicality, the compression and elegance of the Italian. The practical purpose of the original is not merely to convey propositions but to achieve a specific aesthetic effect — and this effect depends upon features of the language that are not propositional: sound, rhythm, the resonance of particular Italian words, the interplay of syntax and line. All three conditions may be satisfied, and yet the translation may be deeply inadequate as a translation of the poem.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all texts whose primary function was informational or instrumental — a scientific paper, a legal contract, an instruction manual. The definition abstracted propositional fidelity, structural preservation, and practical equivalence as essential to translation adequacy because these features sufficed for all the cases examined. But the cases were drawn from a narrow range of text types in which the aesthetic and formal dimensions of language are subordinate to informational content. The Petrarch sonnet reveals that for literary texts — and especially for poetry — the adequacy of a translation depends upon features that the definition does not mention: sound, rhythm, formal structure, tone, and the untranslatable resonances of the original language. The definition captured what is essential to the translation of informational texts and mistook it for what is essential to translation as such.
passage:
Consider three cases of translation universally regarded as adequate. A French scientific paper on optics is rendered into English with every proposition, every logical relation, and every empirical claim faithfully preserved; the translation conveys the same scientific content as the original. A German legal contract is rendered into English with every clause and condition accurately reproduced; the translation is accepted as legally equivalent. A Spanish instruction manual is rendered into English with every procedural step precisely replicated; an English-speaking reader can operate the machine on the basis of the translation alone. From these cases one might extract a definition: a translation is adequate if and only if it preserves the propositional content, preserves the logical and inferential structure, and serves the same practical purpose as the original.

But one must consider the translation of poetry — and not merely incidental verse but a sonnet by Petrarch, one of the supreme achievements of the Italian lyric tradition. A translator might render every proposition in the sonnet with scrupulous fidelity: every image, every comparison, every logical turn. The propositional content would be preserved; the inferential structure would be intact; the practical purpose — if one speaks of poetry's purpose in such terms — would be served in the sense that the reader would learn what the poem says. Yet the translation might be an utter failure as a translation of the sonnet. For what makes the Petrarch sonnet what it is — what constitutes its identity as a work of art — is not exhausted by its propositional content. It is the rhyme scheme, the hendecasyllabic metre, the musicality of the Italian vowels, the compression of meaning into fourteen lines, the way syntax and line-ending interact to produce effects of suspense and resolution. These features are not propositional; they cannot be captured by preserving truth-conditions and inferential relations. And they are, in many cases, untranslatable in any straightforward sense, for they depend upon the phonological, morphological, and syntactic resources of Italian in ways that have no direct equivalent in English. The background cases were all informational or instrumental texts, and the definition abstracted propositional and practical equivalence as sufficient for adequacy. The sonnet reveals that for an entire domain of texts — literary, poetic, rhetorical — the adequacy of a translation depends upon dimensions of language that the definition entirely omits.

background_cases: The sentence 'Snow is white' means that snow is white, and its meaning can be stated by giving its truth-conditions: it is true if and only if snow is white.

The sentence 'Paris is the capital of France' means that Paris is the capital of France, and its meaning can be stated by giving its truth-conditions: it is true if and only if Paris is the capital of France.
