The sentence 'Water boils at 100 degrees Celsius' means that water boils at 100 degrees Celsius, and its meaning is given by its truth-conditions.
definition: The meaning of a declarative sentence S is given by its truth-conditions — that is, a specification of the circumstances under which S is true and the circumstances under which S is false. Two sentences mean the same thing if and only if they have the same truth-conditions.
conditions: the meaning of S is given by its truth-conditions
a specification of when S is true and when S is false exhausts the meaning of S
two sentences mean the same thing if and only if they have the same truth-conditions
counterexample: Consider the sentences 'There are infinitely many prime numbers' and 'The sum of the reciprocals of the primes diverges.' Both are true — indeed, necessarily true. They have the same truth-conditions: both are true in every possible state of affairs in which the natural numbers exist with their standard properties. On the proposed account, they mean the same thing. Yet they plainly do not. The first asserts something about the cardinality of the primes; the second asserts something about the behaviour of a particular infinite series. A mathematician who understands the first may not understand the second, and proving the one is a very different enterprise from proving the other. The third condition fails: sameness of truth-conditions does not suffice for sameness of meaning.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all simple empirical sentences whose meanings seemed fully captured by their truth-conditions — 'Snow is white,' 'Paris is the capital of France,' 'Water boils at 100 degrees Celsius.' The definition abstracted truth-conditional equivalence as sufficient for sameness of meaning because, for sentences of this sort, knowing the truth-conditions does seem to exhaust understanding. But the cases were drawn from a narrow class of straightforward empirical claims. The mathematical sentences reveal that two sentences can share truth-conditions — can be true in all the same circumstances — whilst differing profoundly in what they say, in what one must understand to grasp them, and in the reasoning required to establish them. The definition mistook a feature of simple empirical sentences — that their truth-conditions capture their content — for a general account of meaning, when in fact meaning involves a dimension of cognitive significance that truth-conditions alone do not determine.
passage:
Consider three sentences whose meanings appear to be given by their truth-conditions. 'Snow is white' is true if and only if snow is white; knowing this seems to exhaust one's grasp of the sentence's meaning. 'Paris is the capital of France' is true if and only if Paris is the capital of France; again, the truth-conditions seem to capture the content. 'Water boils at 100 degrees Celsius' is true if and only if water boils at 100 degrees Celsius. From these cases one might extract a general account: the meaning of a declarative sentence is given by its truth-conditions, and two sentences mean the same thing if and only if they share truth-conditions.

But one must consider a pair of mathematical sentences. 'There are infinitely many prime numbers' and 'The sum of the reciprocals of the primes diverges' are both true — indeed, both are necessarily true. They are true in every possible state of affairs in which the natural numbers exist with their standard properties; their truth-conditions are identical, for neither can be false. On the proposed account, the two sentences mean the same thing. Yet they manifestly do not. The first sentence asserts something about the cardinality of the set of primes — that it is infinite. The second asserts something about the behaviour of a particular infinite series — that the series one-half plus one-third plus one-fifth plus one-seventh and so on without end grows beyond all bounds. A mathematician who understands the first may require considerable further effort to understand the second; the proofs of the two theorems are quite different, employing different techniques and illuminating different aspects of the arithmetic of primes. The sentences differ in cognitive significance, in inferential role, and in what one must grasp to understand them, despite sharing truth-conditions. The background cases were simple empirical claims for which truth-conditions seemed sufficient, and the definition mistook this sufficiency for a general principle. The mathematical case reveals that sameness of truth-conditions is too coarse a notion to serve as a criterion of sameness of meaning, for it identifies sentences that differ in everything but their necessary truth.

background_cases: The pronoun 'she' in the sentence 'Mary entered the room and she sat down' refers to Mary, the most recently mentioned female individual in the discourse.
The pronoun 'it' in 'The committee met yesterday; it decided to postpone the vote' refers to the committee, the most salient antecedent in the prior sentence.
The pronoun 'they' in 'The students submitted their essays; they were graded by the tutor' refers to the essays (or perhaps the students), the most salient plural antecedent.
definition: A pronoun P refers to an entity e if and only if (i) e has been explicitly mentioned in the prior discourse, (ii) P agrees with the linguistic expression for e in gender and number, and (iii) e is the most recently mentioned entity satisfying (i) and (ii).
conditions: e has been explicitly mentioned in the prior discourse
P agrees with the linguistic expression for e in gender and number
e is the most recently mentioned entity satisfying the first two conditions
counterexample: Consider two people standing at a window watching a street. A carriage passes, driven recklessly, and narrowly avoids a collision with a pedestrian. One observer turns to the other and says: 'He ought to be more careful.' Neither the driver nor the pedestrian has been mentioned in the prior discourse; there is no linguistic antecedent. The pronoun 'he' refers to the driver — this is immediately clear to both observers — but the reference is achieved not by anaphoric connection to a prior mention but by the perceptual salience of the driver in the shared visual scene. The first condition fails: no entity has been explicitly mentioned. Yet the pronoun refers successfully.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all instances of textual or discourse anaphora, in which a pronoun referred back to a linguistically mentioned antecedent in the prior discourse — Mary, the committee, the students. The definition abstracted prior mention, agreement, and recency as the mechanism of pronominal reference because these features governed every case examined. But the cases were drawn exclusively from written or narrated discourse in which all relevant entities had been introduced linguistically. The case of the carriage driver reveals that a pronoun may refer to an entity that has not been mentioned at all — that pronominal reference may be achieved by perceptual or situational salience rather than by anaphoric connection to a linguistic antecedent. The definition captured the mechanism of discourse anaphora and mistook it for the whole of pronominal reference, when in truth pronouns may draw their referents from the shared perceptual environment, from gesture, from common background, and from a host of extra-linguistic sources.
passage:
Consider three cases of pronominal reference. In the sentence 'Mary entered the room and she sat down,' the pronoun 'she' refers to Mary — the most recently mentioned female individual. In 'The committee met yesterday; it decided to postpone the vote,' the pronoun 'it' refers to the committee, the salient antecedent in the prior sentence. In 'The students submitted their essays; they were graded by the tutor,' the pronoun 'they' refers to the most salient plural antecedent. From these cases one might extract a definition: a pronoun refers to an entity if and only if that entity has been explicitly mentioned in the prior discourse, the pronoun agrees with the expression for the entity in gender and number, and the entity is the most recently mentioned one satisfying these conditions. The background cases all satisfy the conditions, and the definition appears to capture the mechanism of pronominal reference.

But one must consider a rather different situation. Two persons stand at a window, watching a busy street below. A carriage passes at reckless speed, swerving to avoid a pedestrian and narrowly escaping collision. One observer turns to the other and says: 'He ought to be more careful.' The pronoun 'he' refers to the driver of the carriage. Both observers understand this immediately; there is no ambiguity, no hesitation, no request for clarification. Yet the driver has not been mentioned in the prior discourse. There is no linguistic antecedent for the pronoun — no prior sentence in which the driver was introduced by name or description. The first condition of the definition fails entirely: the referent has not been explicitly mentioned. The reference is achieved not by anaphoric connection to a linguistic antecedent but by the perceptual salience of the driver in the shared visual field. Both observers have just watched the carriage pass; the driver is the most salient agent in the scene; and the pronoun 'he' latches onto this salience directly. The background cases were all instances of discourse anaphora — cases in which every referent had been introduced linguistically — and the definition abstracted prior mention as a necessary condition of pronominal reference. The case of the carriage driver reveals that pronouns may refer not only to entities mentioned in discourse but to entities made salient by the shared perceptual environment, and that the mechanism of pronominal reference is broader than any purely anaphoric account can capture.

background_cases: The word 'dog' is meaningful because it can be defined by stating necessary and sufficient conditions: an entity is a dog if and only if it is a domesticated canine of the species Canis familiaris.

The word 'triangle' is meaningful because it can be defined by stating necessary and sufficient conditions: a figure is a triangle if and only if it is a closed plane figure bounded by exactly three straight sides.
The word 'bachelor' is meaningful because it can be defined by stating necessary and sufficient conditions: a person is a bachelor if and only if he is an unmarried adult male.
definition: A word W is meaningful if and only if (i) there exists a set of necessary and sufficient conditions for the correct application of W, (ii) any competent speaker can, upon reflection, state these conditions, and (iii) the meaning of W is exhausted by these conditions.
conditions: there exists a set of necessary and sufficient conditions for the correct application of W
any competent speaker can, upon reflection, state these conditions
the meaning of W is exhausted by these conditions
counterexample: Consider the word 'game.' It is meaningful — children use it, adults use it, philosophers discuss it — yet it resists definition by necessary and sufficient conditions. Is a game necessarily competitive? No, for a child playing with a doll is playing a game without competing. Does a game necessarily have rules? Not in every case, for imaginative play may have no rules at all. Is a game necessarily amusing? Ring-a-ring-o'-roses is a game, but soldiers drilling in formation, which resembles a game in many respects, is not typically amusing. One searches in vain for a set of conditions that are individually necessary and jointly sufficient. Moreover, competent speakers cannot state such conditions upon reflection; they recognise games by a felt similarity to other games rather than by checking conditions. Yet the word is perfectly meaningful.
abductive_insight: The background cases — 'dog,' 'triangle,' 'bachelor' — were all terms whose application is governed by relatively sharp necessary and sufficient conditions, at least on a first approximation. The definition abstracted this feature as essential to meaningfulness because it was present in each case examined. But the sample was drawn from a narrow class of terms: a natural-kind term with a scientific definition, a geometrical term defined by mathematical convention, and a social term defined by a conjunction of simple properties. The word 'game' reveals that many ordinary, perfectly meaningful words do not admit of definition by necessary and sufficient conditions. Their application is governed instead by family resemblances — overlapping networks of similarities that bind instances together without any single feature running through them all. The definition mistook a feature of definable terms for a general requirement of meaningfulness.
passage:
Consider three words whose meaningfulness is beyond dispute. 'Dog' is meaningful because one can state the conditions under which it correctly applies: an entity is a dog if and only if it is a domesticated canine of the species Canis familiaris. 'Triangle' is meaningful because it admits of precise definition: a figure is a triangle if and only if it is a closed plane figure with exactly three straight sides. 'Bachelor' is meaningful because one can specify that a person is a bachelor if and only if he is an unmarried adult male. From these cases one might extract a general account: a word is meaningful if and only if there exist necessary and sufficient conditions for its correct application, any competent speaker can state these conditions, and the meaning of the word is exhausted by them.

But one must consider the word 'game.' It is a perfectly ordinary English word, used with confidence by speakers of every age and education. Children understand it, adults employ it daily, and philosophers have found it a rich subject for reflection. Yet the word resists definition by necessary and sufficient conditions. Is competition a necessary condition of being a game? Board games are competitive, but a child tossing a ball against a wall is playing a game without competing against anyone. Are rules necessary? Card games have elaborate rules, but imaginative play — a child pretending to be a pirate — may proceed without any rules at all. Is amusement necessary? Many games are amusing, but professional chess at the highest level may be more agonising than amusing, and yet it is a game. One can identify features that many games share — competition, rules, amusement, skill, chance — but no single feature is common to all games, and no conjunction of features provides a definition that is both necessary and sufficient. Competent speakers cannot state the conditions that govern the application of the word, for there are no such conditions to state. They recognise games not by checking a list of defining features but by perceiving a resemblance to other things they have called games — a network of overlapping similarities that Wittgenstein termed a family resemblance. The background cases were drawn from terms with sharp boundaries and precise definitions, and the analysis mistook the definability of those terms for a general condition of meaningfulness. The word 'game' is meaningful despite — or perhaps because of — the fact that its application is governed not by necessary and sufficient conditions but by a more fluid and flexible pattern of resemblance.

background_cases: The name 'Walter Scott' refers to a particular individual because there is a causal chain linking present uses of the name to the original bestowal upon the man born in Edinburgh in 1771.
The name 'Isaac Newton' refers to a particular individual because the name was bestowed upon the child born in Woolsthorpe in 1642, and subsequent uses trace back to that bestowal.
The name 'Julius Caesar' refers to a particular individual because the name traces, through an unbroken chain of historical transmission, to the Roman general and dictator.
definition: A proper name N refers to an individual x if and only if (i) N was bestowed upon x in an original act of naming, (ii) the current speaker's use of N is connected to the original act by an unbroken causal chain of communicative uses, and (iii) no link in the chain involves a deliberate change of referent.
conditions: N was bestowed upon x in an original act of naming
the current speaker's use is connected to the original act by an unbroken causal chain
no link in the chain involves a deliberate change of referent
counterexample: Consider the name 'Santa Claus.' The name traces back, through a complex historical chain, to Saint Nicholas of Myra, a fourth-century bishop. But at various points in the transmission of the name, the referent shifted — deliberately or semi-deliberately — from the historical bishop to a mythical figure associated with Christmas gift-giving, flying reindeer, and a workshop at the North Pole. The chain is unbroken, but it involves a radical transformation of the referent: the historical Nicholas was a real person, whilst the Santa Claus of contemporary culture is a fictional construct. One might argue that the change was deliberate — storytellers and folklorists consciously reshaped the figure — in which case the third condition fails. Yet 'Santa Claus' refers, in modern usage, to the fictional character, and this reference is perfectly successful.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all names of real historical individuals whose referents remained stable throughout the causal chain — Scott, Newton, Caesar. The definition abstracted the stability of the referent as a necessary condition of reference because no case in the sample exhibited referential drift. But the sample was drawn from names whose transmission was straightforward and uncontested. The case of 'Santa Claus' reveals that names may undergo radical referential transformation in the course of their transmission, passing from a real individual to a fictional character, and yet continue to refer successfully in their new usage. The definition mistook the stability of reference in the background cases for a necessary condition, when in fact the causal chain may tolerate — and indeed facilitate — deliberate changes of referent that establish new and perfectly functional patterns of reference.
passage:
Consider three names whose reference is well established. 'Walter Scott' refers to the Scottish novelist, the name tracing back through a causal chain to the original bestowal upon the child born in Edinburgh. 'Isaac Newton' refers to the natural philosopher, the name connected by historical transmission to the man born in Woolsthorpe. 'Julius Caesar' refers to the Roman dictator, the name preserved through more than two millennia of continuous usage. From these cases one might extract a definition: a proper name refers to an individual if and only if it was bestowed upon that individual in an original act of naming, the current speaker's use is connected to that act by an unbroken causal chain, and no link in the chain involves a deliberate change of referent. The third condition ensures that the chain preserves the original reference rather than drifting to a new one.

But one must consider the name 'Santa Claus.' The name has a traceable history: it derives, through Dutch 'Sinterklaas,' from 'Saint Nicholas,' and Saint Nicholas was a real person — a bishop of Myra in the fourth century, credited with various acts of generosity and miracle. The causal chain from the historical bishop to the present-day use of 'Santa Claus' is unbroken; at every stage, the name was transmitted from speaker to speaker. Yet at various points in this chain, the referent was deliberately transformed. Storytellers, poets, and commercial illustrators progressively reshaped the figure associated with the name, discarding the historical bishop's attributes and substituting those of a jolly, rotund man in a red suit who travels by reindeer-drawn sleigh and distributes gifts on Christmas Eve. The transformation was not accidental or unconscious; it was a creative reshaping of a traditional figure. The third condition fails — the chain involves deliberate changes of referent at multiple points. Yet 'Santa Claus' refers successfully in modern usage. When a child says 'Santa Claus will come tonight,' every competent speaker understands the reference. The background cases were all names of real individuals whose reference was stable, and the definition abstracted stability as essential. The case of 'Santa Claus' reveals that names may pass through radical referential transformations — from a historical person to a fictional character — and yet function as perfectly successful referring expressions in their new employment.

background_cases: The French sentence 'La neige est blanche' is correctly translated into English as 'Snow is white'; the translation preserves truth-conditions, and the words correspond one-to-one.
The German sentence 'Zwei plus zwei ist vier' is correctly translated as 'Two plus two is four'; the translation preserves truth-conditions, and the syntactic structure is closely parallel.
The Latin sentence 'Omnes homines mortales sunt' is correctly translated as 'All men are mortal'; the translation preserves truth-conditions, and the logical form is maintained.
definition: A translation T of a sentence S from language L1 into language L2 is correct if and only if (i) T preserves the truth-conditions of S, (ii) T preserves the syntactic structure of S as closely as the grammar of L2 allows, and (iii) there is a one-to-one correspondence between the content words of S and the content words of T.
conditions: T preserves the truth-conditions of S
T preserves the syntactic structure of S
there is a one-to-one correspondence between the content words of S and those of T
counterexample: Consider the Japanese sentence 'Yoroshiku onegai shimasu,' used upon meeting someone, upon asking a favour, or upon beginning a collaborative undertaking. No English sentence preserves its truth-conditions exactly, for the sentence does not have truth-conditions in the straightforward sense — it is a performative expression of goodwill and a request for favourable treatment rather than a declarative assertion. No English sentence preserves its syntactic structure, for Japanese syntax differs radically from English. And there is no one-to-one correspondence between its content words and those of any English rendering; the expression may be translated as 'Pleased to meet you,' 'I look forward to working with you,' 'Please treat me well,' or 'I'm counting on you,' depending on context, each of which renders a different aspect of the original. All three conditions fail, yet competent translators produce acceptable translations of the sentence daily.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all simple declarative sentences from European languages with relatively close syntactic and semantic parallels to English — French, German, Latin. The definition abstracted truth-condition preservation, syntactic parallelism, and one-to-one lexical correspondence as the marks of correct translation because all three features were present in the sample. But the sample was drawn from a narrow class of structurally similar languages and a narrow class of simple declarative sentences. The Japanese expression reveals that translation between typologically distant languages, and translation of performative or non-declarative utterances, may require abandoning all three conditions. The definition mistook the features of easy, structure-preserving translation between cognate languages for the general conditions of correct translation.
passage:
Consider three translations that are uncontroversially correct. The French 'La neige est blanche' becomes the English 'Snow is white'; truth-conditions are preserved, the syntactic structure is closely parallel, and there is a neat correspondence between content words. The German 'Zwei plus zwei ist vier' becomes 'Two plus two is four'; again, the translation preserves truth-conditions, maintains syntactic structure, and establishes a word-by-word correspondence. The Latin 'Omnes homines mortales sunt' becomes 'All men are mortal'; the same three features are present. From these cases one might extract a definition: a translation is correct if and only if it preserves truth-conditions, preserves syntactic structure, and establishes a one-to-one correspondence between content words.

But one must consider the Japanese expression 'Yoroshiku onegai shimasu.' This expression is ubiquitous in Japanese social life — it is spoken upon meeting a stranger, upon beginning a collaboration, upon requesting assistance, upon many other occasions. It has no single English equivalent. One might render it as 'Pleased to meet you' in one context, 'I look forward to working with you' in another, 'Please take care of me' in a third, or 'I'm in your hands' in a fourth. None of these translations preserves the truth-conditions of the original, for the original does not have truth-conditions in the ordinary sense; it is not a description of a state of affairs but a performative expression of social goodwill and a request for favourable treatment. None preserves the syntactic structure, for the Japanese sentence is constructed from honorific verb forms and a noun of request that have no structural counterparts in English. And there is no one-to-one correspondence between the content words of the original and those of any rendering, for the Japanese compresses into a single conventional formula what English distributes across several different phrases depending on context. All three conditions of the definition fail. Yet translators produce acceptable renderings of this expression constantly, and Japanese-English communication is not thereby impeded. The background cases were drawn from declarative sentences in structurally similar European languages, and the definition abstracted their features as universal requirements. The Japanese case reveals that translation between typologically distant languages, and translation of performative utterances, may demand a wholesale departure from the conditions that hold for simple declaratives in cognate tongues.

background_cases: A judge says 'I sentence you to five years' imprisonment' in a courtroom, and the defendant is thereby sentenced; the utterance is a performative that accomplishes what it states.
A bride says 'I do' during a marriage ceremony, and the marriage is thereby effected; the utterance performs the act of consenting to marriage.
A sovereign says 'I declare war' before an assembled parliament, and the nation is thereby at war; the utterance constitutes the declaration.
definition: An utterance U is a performative if and only if (i) U is spoken by a person who holds an appropriate institutional authority, (ii) U is uttered in the appropriate institutional setting, and (iii) U employs an explicit performative verb in the first person present indicative — for example, 'I sentence,' 'I promise,' 'I declare.'
conditions: U is spoken by a person holding appropriate institutional authority
U is uttered in the appropriate institutional setting
U employs an explicit performative verb in the first person present indicative
counterexample: Consider the utterance 'You're fired,' spoken by an employer to an employee in a place of work. The utterance is performative — it effects a dismissal rather than merely describing one. The employer holds the requisite authority, and the setting is appropriate. But the utterance does not employ an explicit performative verb in the first person present indicative. The speaker does not say 'I dismiss you' or 'I hereby terminate your employment.' The performative force is carried by a second-person declarative sentence that describes the employee's new state rather than by an explicit first-person performative formula. The third condition fails, yet the utterance is performative.
abductive_insight: The background cases — judicial sentencing, marriage vows, declarations of war — were all highly formalised institutional speech acts performed by figures of authority in ritual settings using explicit performative formulae. The definition abstracted the explicit performative verb in the first person as a necessary feature because every case in the sample exhibited it. But the cases were drawn from the domain of formal, ceremonial, legally constituted speech, where the use of an explicit performative formula is a conventional requirement. The employer's 'You're fired' reveals that performative utterances may operate without any explicit performative verb, relying instead on the conventional understanding of what the utterance accomplishes in its context. The definition mistook the linguistic form characteristic of ceremonial performatives for a general requirement of all performative utterances.
passage:
Consider three utterances that are paradigmatic performatives. A judge in a courtroom says 'I sentence you to five years' imprisonment,' and the defendant is thereby sentenced. A bride at the altar says 'I do,' and the marriage is thereby effected. A sovereign before parliament says 'I declare war,' and the nation is at war. In each case the utterance does not merely describe an action but accomplishes it. From these cases one might extract a definition: an utterance is performative if and only if it is spoken by a person holding appropriate institutional authority, it is uttered in the appropriate setting, and it employs an explicit performative verb in the first person present indicative.

But one must consider the utterance 'You're fired,' spoken by the owner of a business to an employee. The utterance is performative in the fullest sense: it does not describe the employee's dismissal but accomplishes it. Before the utterance, the employee was employed; after it, he is not. The employer holds the appropriate authority — the power to hire and dismiss — and the setting is appropriate, being a place of business in which such authority is exercised. But the utterance does not employ an explicit performative verb in the first person present indicative. The employer does not say 'I hereby dismiss you' or 'I terminate your employment'; she says 'You're fired,' a second-person declarative that describes the employee's new status rather than explicitly performing the act of dismissal. Yet the performative force is unmistakable. The employee does not take the utterance as a description requiring independent verification; he understands immediately that he has been dismissed. The background cases were all drawn from the ceremonial end of the spectrum of speech acts — courtrooms, altars, parliaments — where convention demands an explicit performative formula. The definition abstracted this formula as a necessary condition. The employer's laconic dismissal reveals that performative utterances may operate perfectly well without any explicit performative verb, relying on context, authority, and the conventional understanding of what the words accomplish.

background_cases: In the sentence 'The chairman ploughed through the agenda,' the word 'ploughed' is used metaphorically; the literal meaning of 'ploughed' pertains to agriculture, and the metaphorical meaning is derived by transferring the notion of forceful progress through resistant material to the domain of administrative work.

In the sentence 'He drowned in paperwork,' the word 'drowned' is used metaphorically; the literal meaning pertains to submersion in water, and the metaphorical meaning is derived by transferring the notion of being overwhelmed to the domain of clerical labour.

In the sentence 'Her words cut him deeply,' the word 'cut' is used metaphorically; the literal meaning pertains to physical incision, and the metaphorical meaning is derived by transferring the notion of inflicting a wound to the domain of emotional harm.
definition: A word W is used metaphorically in a sentence S if and only if (i) W has a primary literal meaning pertaining to a concrete, physical domain, (ii) W is transferred from this domain to a different, typically more abstract domain, and (iii) the metaphorical meaning of W can be recovered by identifying the relevant point of similarity between the two domains.
conditions: W has a primary literal meaning pertaining to a concrete, physical domain
W is transferred from the concrete domain to a different, typically more abstract domain
the metaphorical meaning can be recovered by identifying the relevant similarity
counterexample: Consider the sentence 'The atom is a miniature solar system.' Here the metaphor runs from the abstract and theoretical domain of atomic physics to the relatively more concrete domain of planetary motion. The word 'solar system' is not being transferred from a concrete domain to a more abstract one; if anything, the direction of transfer is reversed — a relatively familiar astronomical concept is being applied to the unfamiliar subatomic realm to render it intelligible. Moreover, the similarity between the atom and the solar system, though suggestive, breaks down upon closer examination: electrons do not orbit the nucleus in the way that planets orbit the sun, and the forces involved are different in kind. The metaphor was productive not because it identified a genuine similarity but because it imposed a structure that guided further inquiry, some of which revealed that the similarity was misleading. The first and second conditions are doubtful, and the third condition — that the metaphorical meaning is recovered by identifying a genuine similarity — is undermined by the fact that the similarity is in important respects false.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all instances of a concrete physical term — 'ploughed,' 'drowned,' 'cut' — being applied metaphorically to an abstract domain of human affairs. The definition abstracted this pattern as essential: metaphor is the transfer of a concrete term to an abstract domain, and the metaphorical meaning is recovered by identifying a similarity. But the cases were drawn from a single, very common type of metaphor — the application of physical vocabulary to non-physical situations. The atom-as-solar-system metaphor reveals that metaphors may run in the opposite direction, from the relatively familiar to the unfamiliar, and that the supposed similarity may be misleading or even false, whilst the metaphor remains cognitively productive. The definition mistook the features of one species of metaphor for the genus.
passage:
Consider three sentences in which a word is used metaphorically. 'The chairman ploughed through the agenda': 'ploughed' is transferred from agriculture to administration, and the metaphorical meaning — that the chairman proceeded forcefully and without interruption — is recovered by noting the similarity between forcing a plough through resistant earth and forcing one's way through a lengthy list of business. 'He drowned in paperwork': 'drowned' is transferred from submersion in water to the experience of being overwhelmed by documents. 'Her words cut him deeply': 'cut' is transferred from physical incision to emotional harm. In each case, a word with a concrete, physical meaning is applied to a more abstract domain, and the metaphorical meaning is grasped by identifying a point of similarity. From these cases one might extract a definition: a word is used metaphorically if and only if it has a primary literal meaning in a concrete domain, is transferred to a different and typically more abstract domain, and the metaphorical meaning is recovered by identifying the relevant similarity.

But one must consider the sentence 'The atom is a miniature solar system.' This metaphor, which shaped the early development of atomic physics, does not conform to the pattern abstracted from the background cases. The term 'solar system' is not being transferred from a concrete domain to a more abstract one; if anything, the direction of transfer is the reverse of what the definition predicts. The solar system, though physical, is a macroscopic astronomical structure, and it is being applied to the subatomic realm — a domain that is, in its own way, equally physical but far less accessible to ordinary experience. The metaphor renders the atom intelligible by likening it to something more familiar. Moreover, the similarity between the atom and the solar system, though initially suggestive, is in important respects misleading. Electrons do not orbit the nucleus in well-defined elliptical paths as planets orbit the sun; the forces are electromagnetic rather than gravitational; and the quantum-mechanical behaviour of electrons has no analogue in classical planetary mechanics. Yet the metaphor was enormously productive — it guided Bohr's early model, suggested experimental predictions, and structured a generation of physical thinking. Its productivity did not depend upon the accuracy of the similarity but upon the structure it imposed. The background cases were drawn from a single common pattern of metaphor, and the definition mistook that pattern for the essence of metaphorical language.

background_cases: The sentence 'Snow is white' means that snow is white; its meaning is given by specifying the conditions under which it is true.
The sentence 'Paris is the capital of France' means that Paris is the capital of France; again, its meaning is its truth-conditions.

The sentence 'Water boils at 100 degrees Celsius at standard pressure' means that water boils at 100°C at standard pressure; one grasps its meaning by understanding what the world would have to be like for it to be true.
definition: The meaning of a declarative sentence S is identical with the set of possible worlds in which S is true — that is, S means that p if and only if (i) S is true in exactly those possible worlds in which p obtains, (ii) any two sentences true in exactly the same possible worlds have the same meaning, and (iii) a speaker who grasps the meaning of S thereby grasps which possible worlds are S-worlds and which are not.
conditions: S is true in exactly those possible worlds in which p obtains
Any two sentences true in the same possible worlds have the same meaning
A speaker who grasps the meaning of S thereby grasps which possible worlds are S-worlds
counterexample: Consider the sentences 'All equilateral triangles are equiangular' and 'All equiangular triangles are equilateral.' Both are necessary truths of Euclidean geometry. They are true in every possible world (where Euclidean geometry holds). They are thus true in exactly the same possible worlds, and condition (ii) requires us to say they have the same meaning. But they manifestly do not mean the same thing. The first tells us something about equilateral triangles — that they are equiangular. The second tells us something about equiangular triangles — that they are equilateral. A student might grasp one truth without grasping the other; the two convey different information, make different claims, and could play different roles in an argument.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all contingent sentences — sentences that are true in some possible worlds and false in others — and for such sentences, possible-worlds truth-conditions do a reasonable job of distinguishing meanings. The definition generalised from these contingent cases, but it collapses the distinction between all necessary truths (and all impossible falsehoods), treating them as having the same meaning when they plainly do not.
passage:
Consider three sentences whose meanings seem well captured by their truth-conditions. 'Snow is white' means that snow is white — it is true in exactly those possible worlds in which snow has that colour. 'Paris is the capital of France' means that Paris is the capital of France — it is true in exactly those worlds in which this political fact obtains. 'Water boils at 100°C at standard pressure' means what it says — and one grasps its meaning by understanding which states of affairs would make it true. One might therefore identify meaning with truth-conditions in the possible-worlds sense: the meaning of a sentence is the set of possible worlds in which it is true, and two sentences that are true in exactly the same worlds mean the same thing.

But the identification proves too coarse-grained. Consider the sentences 'All equilateral triangles are equiangular' and 'All equiangular triangles are equilateral.' Both are necessary truths of Euclidean geometry, true in every possible world in which the relevant geometrical axioms hold. They are true in exactly the same possible worlds. The definition therefore requires us to say that they have the same meaning. But they manifestly do not. The first says something about equilateral triangles — that they are equiangular. The second says something about equiangular triangles — that they are equilateral. A student of geometry might prove one and not yet see how to prove the other; the two convey different information, make different claims, and play different roles in mathematical reasoning. The background cases were contingent sentences, for which possible-worlds truth-conditions do a tolerably good job of distinguishing meanings. But the definition was generalised without noticing that it collapses all necessary truths into a single meaning and all impossible falsehoods into another, when in reality they differ as much from one another as any contingent sentences do.

background_cases: The name 'Aristotle' is associated with the description 'the Stagirite philosopher who taught Alexander the Great,' and this description serves to fix the reference of the name.
The name 'Cicero' is associated with the description 'the Roman orator who denounced Catiline,' and this description determines whom the name picks out.
The name 'Homer' is associated with the description 'the blind poet who composed the Iliad and the Odyssey,' and this description fixes the reference.
definition: A proper name N refers to an individual I if and only if (i) speakers associate with N a cluster of descriptions D₁, D₂, ..., Dₙ, (ii) I is the unique individual satisfying a weighted majority of the descriptions in the cluster, and (iii) if no individual satisfies a weighted majority, then N fails to refer.
conditions: Speakers associate with N a cluster of descriptions
I is the unique individual satisfying a weighted majority of the descriptions
If no individual satisfies a weighted majority, N fails to refer
counterexample: Consider the name 'Jonah,' as used by ordinary speakers of English. The descriptions most speakers associate with the name include: 'the prophet who was swallowed by a whale,' 'the man who preached to the Ninevites,' and 'a figure in the Old Testament.' Now suppose — as many historians believe — that there was a historical prophet by that name who preached in Nineveh but who was never swallowed by a whale. The whale story is a later literary embellishment. If 'swallowed by a whale' is the most heavily weighted description in the cluster, then no actual individual satisfies a weighted majority that includes it. Condition (iii) would require us to say that 'Jonah' fails to refer. Yet the name does refer — it refers to the historical prophet, despite the falsity of the most salient description associated with it.
abductive_insight: The background cases — Aristotle, Cicero, Homer — were all instances in which the associated descriptions happened to be largely correct, so the cluster theory appeared to fix reference accurately. But the cases genuinely shared a referential link that survived independently of the correctness of the descriptions, and the descriptions served as heuristic guides rather than as reference-determining conditions.
passage:
Consider three familiar proper names and the descriptions associated with them. 'Aristotle' is associated with 'the Stagirite philosopher who taught Alexander.' 'Cicero' is associated with 'the Roman orator who denounced Catiline.' 'Homer' is associated with 'the blind poet who composed the Iliad and the Odyssey.' In each case, the descriptions serve to fix the reference — they pick out the individual to whom the name refers. One might propose, following a tradition in the philosophy of language, that a name refers to whichever individual satisfies a weighted majority of the descriptions speakers associate with it, and that the name fails to refer if no such individual exists.

But the proposal encounters a difficulty with cases in which the associated descriptions are substantially false. Consider the name 'Jonah.' The descriptions most speakers associate with it include 'the prophet swallowed by a whale,' 'the man who preached to the Ninevites,' and 'a figure in the Old Testament.' Suppose, as many historians believe, that there was a historical prophet of that name who did indeed preach in Nineveh but who was never swallowed by any sea creature — the whale episode being a later literary embellishment. If the whale story is the most heavily weighted description in the cluster, and if the historical Jonah fails to satisfy it, then on the cluster theory no individual satisfies a weighted majority of the descriptions, and condition (iii) dictates that 'Jonah' fails to refer. But this is wrong: the name does refer — it refers to the historical prophet, notwithstanding the falsity of the most salient tale told about him. The background cases were ones in which the descriptions happened to be correct, and the theory appeared to work because reference and description coincided. But the cases genuinely shared a referential link that persists independently of the accuracy of the associated descriptions.

background_cases: The predicate 'bald' is vague: there is no precise number of hairs that separates bald persons from non-bald persons, and persons with an intermediate number of hairs are borderline cases.
The predicate 'heap' is vague: there is no precise number of grains that separates heaps from non-heaps, and collections of an intermediate number of grains are borderline cases.
The predicate 'tall' is vague: there is no precise height that separates tall persons from non-tall persons, and persons of intermediate height are borderline cases.
definition: A predicate F is vague if and only if (i) F is associated with a single underlying dimension along which objects can be ordered (number of hairs, number of grains, height in centimetres), (ii) the application of F is indeterminate for objects in a middle range of that dimension, and (iii) F is not vague in any other respect — its vagueness consists entirely in the absence of a sharp threshold along the single underlying dimension.
conditions: F is associated with a single underlying dimension along which objects can be ordered
The application of F is indeterminate for objects in a middle range of that dimension
F is not vague in any other respect — vagueness consists entirely in the absence of a sharp threshold on the single dimension
counterexample: Consider the predicate 'nice.' It is vague — there are clearly nice persons, clearly not-nice persons, and borderline cases. But its vagueness does not consist in the absence of a sharp threshold along a single underlying dimension. 'Nice' is vague along multiple dimensions simultaneously: a person may be kind but tactless, generous but condescending, warm but unreliable. There is no single scale along which persons can be ordered from most nice to least nice, because niceness is a multi-dimensional quality. Conditions (i) and (iii) fail. The predicate is vague, but its vagueness is not reducible to a single-dimension threshold problem.
abductive_insight: The background cases — 'bald,' 'heap,' 'tall' — were all predicates whose vagueness arises from a single measurable dimension (number of hairs, grains, height), and the definition treated this one-dimensional structure as essential to vagueness. But what the cases genuinely shared was simply the existence of borderline cases and the absence of sharp boundaries, and many vague predicates are vague along multiple dimensions at once, in ways that resist reduction to a single ordered scale.
passage:
Consider three predicates that are paradigmatically vague. 'Bald' is vague because there is no precise number of hairs separating the bald from the non-bald. 'Heap' is vague because there is no precise number of grains separating heaps from non-heaps. 'Tall' is vague because there is no precise height separating the tall from the non-tall. In each case, the vagueness seems to arise from a single underlying dimension — a measurable quantity along which objects can be ordered — and the indeterminacy consists in the absence of a sharp threshold on that dimension. One might therefore propose that vagueness just is this: the association of a predicate with a single ordered dimension on which there is no sharp cut-off.

But the proposal is too restrictive. Consider the predicate 'nice' as applied to persons. It is undoubtedly vague: there are persons who are clearly nice, persons who are clearly not nice, and a great many persons in between about whom competent speakers will disagree. But its vagueness does not consist in the absence of a sharp threshold along a single dimension. There is no single scale of niceness. A person may be kind but tactless, generous but condescending, warm in personal dealings but cold in professional ones. Niceness is a multi-dimensional quality, and the predicate is vague along all of these dimensions simultaneously. One cannot order all persons from most nice to least nice in a way that every competent speaker would accept, because the dimensions of niceness trade off against one another in ways that resist linear ordering. Conditions (i) and (iii) fail: the predicate is not associated with a single dimension, and its vagueness does not consist entirely in a threshold problem. Yet it is vague. The background cases were all single-dimension predicates, and the definition mistook this feature for the essence of vagueness. What the cases genuinely shared was the existence of borderline cases and the absence of sharp boundaries — and these phenomena arise in multi-dimensional predicates as readily as in single-dimensional ones.

background_cases: The sentence 'Snow is white' means that snow is white; its meaning consists in specifying the conditions under which it is true — namely, that the substance denoted by 'snow' has the property denoted by 'white.'
The sentence 'Paris is the capital of France' means that Paris is the capital of France; its meaning is given by the state of affairs that must obtain for the sentence to be true.
The sentence 'Water boils at 100 degrees Celsius' means that water boils at that temperature; to understand the sentence is to know under what conditions it would be true.
definition: The meaning of a declarative sentence S is the set of conditions under which S is true — its truth-conditions — if and only if (i) S expresses a proposition that is determinately true or false, (ii) a competent speaker who understands S is able to specify the conditions under which S would be true, and (iii) two sentences have the same meaning if and only if they have the same truth-conditions.
conditions: S expresses a proposition that is determinately true or false
A competent speaker can specify the truth-conditions of S
Two sentences with the same truth-conditions have the same meaning
counterexample: Consider the sentences 'Hesperus is a planet' and 'Phosphorus is a planet.' Since Hesperus is Phosphorus — both names refer to Venus — these sentences have the same truth-conditions: they are true in precisely the same possible circumstances, namely those in which Venus is a planet. By condition (iii), they have the same meaning. Yet the two sentences differ in meaning in a way that is philosophically important: a competent speaker might understand both sentences and assent to one while withholding assent from the other, if the speaker does not know that Hesperus is Phosphorus. The sentences differ in what one might call cognitive significance, even though their truth-conditions are identical.
abductive_insight: The background cases — snow being white, Paris being the capital of France, water boiling at 100 degrees — all involved sentences whose meaning seemed fully captured by their truth-conditions, because the terms involved did not raise questions of cognitive significance. But what made those cases amenable to a truth-conditional treatment was not that meaning is nothing more than truth-conditions but that the particular sentences chosen did not exhibit the gap between truth-conditions and cognitive significance that arises when co-referential but cognitively distinct terms are involved. The definition has generalised from cases in which truth-conditions suffice to determine meaning to the claim that they always suffice — a generalisation that the Hesperus-Phosphorus case reveals to be unwarranted.
passage:
The truth-conditional theory of meaning has considerable intuitive support. When one grasps the meaning of 'Snow is white,' one knows under what conditions the sentence is true — namely, when snow is in fact white. When one understands 'Paris is the capital of France,' one knows what state of affairs must obtain for the sentence to be true. From such cases one might extract the general principle: the meaning of a declarative sentence is given by its truth-conditions, and two sentences that have the same truth-conditions have the same meaning.

Yet this principle, plausible as it seems, encounters a difficulty that the background cases did not exhibit. Consider the sentences 'Hesperus is a planet' and 'Phosphorus is a planet.' The names 'Hesperus' and 'Phosphorus' both refer to the planet Venus: Hesperus is the evening star, Phosphorus is the morning star, and they are one and the same celestial body. The two sentences, therefore, have exactly the same truth-conditions: they are true in precisely those possible circumstances in which Venus is a planet, and false in precisely those circumstances in which it is not. By the truth-conditional criterion, they have the same meaning. But a speaker who does not know that Hesperus is Phosphorus — who has not learned the astronomical discovery that the evening star and the morning star are identical — might assent to one sentence and withhold assent from the other. The sentences differ in what might be called their cognitive significance: they present the same state of affairs under different aspects, and a speaker's relation to the two sentences may be different even though the truth-conditions are the same. The background cases — snow being white, Paris being a capital, water boiling — all involved terms that did not raise this difficulty, because the terms employed did not admit of cognitively distinct but co-referential alternatives. The definition has generalised from a class of easy cases to a universal principle that the harder cases reveal to be insufficient. What is needed is an account of meaning that accommodates the difference between truth-conditions and cognitive significance.

background_cases: The name 'Aristotle' refers to the ancient philosopher because speakers who use the name associate with it a cluster of descriptions — the teacher of Alexander, the author of the Nicomachean Ethics, the pupil of Plato — and the name refers to whichever individual satisfies the greatest number of these descriptions.

The name 'Homer' refers to the poet because speakers associate with it certain descriptions — the author of the Iliad, the blind bard of Ionia — and the name picks out whoever satisfies these descriptions, if anyone does.

The name 'Cicero' refers to the Roman orator because speakers associate with the name a cluster of descriptions — the denouncer of Catiline, the author of the Tusculan Disputations — and the name refers to the individual who fits the cluster.
definition: A proper name N refers to the individual who satisfies the descriptions that speakers associate with N — that is, (i) speakers who use N associate with it a cluster of descriptions D1, D2, ..., Dk, (ii) N refers to the unique individual who satisfies a sufficient number of the descriptions in the cluster, and (iii) if no individual satisfies a sufficient number of the descriptions, N does not refer to anything.
conditions: Speakers associate a cluster of descriptions with N
N refers to the individual satisfying sufficiently many descriptions
If no individual satisfies enough descriptions, N fails to refer
counterexample: Consider the name 'Jonah,' as used by ordinary speakers. The descriptions most commonly associated with 'Jonah' are: the prophet who was swallowed by a great fish, who preached to the people of Nineveh, and who was disgorged alive after three days. Suppose — as many biblical scholars hold — that no individual in history satisfies any of these descriptions: there was no person swallowed by a fish, and the story is a literary parable. By condition (iii), 'Jonah' fails to refer. Yet ordinary speakers use the name 'Jonah' as though it refers to someone — a particular figure in the biblical narrative — and they would be startled to be told that their name does not refer. Moreover, if a historical person named Jonah existed but was never swallowed by a fish and never preached to Nineveh, the description theory would yield the result that 'Jonah' does not refer to that person, since the individual does not satisfy the associated descriptions. The name would fail to connect with the actual historical figure, if there was one.
abductive_insight: The background cases — Aristotle, Homer, Cicero — all involved names for which the associated descriptions happened to pick out a real and identifiable individual. The description theory worked because the descriptions were, by and large, true of the intended referent. But the feature that makes a name refer to a particular individual is not the accuracy of the associated descriptions but the historical and linguistic chain that connects the use of the name to the individual. The definition has generalised from cases in which descriptions and reference happened to align to a theory in which descriptions determine reference — a theory that fails when the descriptions are inaccurate or mythological.
passage:
It is natural to suppose that a proper name refers to the individual who satisfies the descriptions that speakers associate with the name. When speakers use the name 'Aristotle,' they associate with it a cluster of descriptions — the teacher of Alexander, the author of the Nicomachean Ethics, the pupil of Plato — and the name refers to whoever satisfies these descriptions. Similarly for 'Homer' — the blind poet, the author of the Iliad — and for 'Cicero' — the orator, the denouncer of Catiline. From these cases one might extract the general theory: a name refers to the individual who fits the associated descriptions, and if no one fits, the name fails to refer.

Yet this theory, plausible for the well-documented figures of intellectual history, encounters difficulties when applied to names whose associated descriptions may be largely or entirely false. Consider the name 'Jonah.' The descriptions commonly associated with it are vivid: the prophet swallowed by a great fish, who preached to Nineveh, who was disgorged alive after three days in the belly of the creature. Suppose, as many scholars hold, that no historical individual satisfies any of these descriptions — that the story of Jonah is a parable, a work of religious literature rather than a record of fact. The theory yields the result that 'Jonah' does not refer to anyone. Yet speakers use the name as though it refers; they speak of Jonah as a figure with a story, a character with attributes. And if — as is also possible — there was a historical figure named Jonah who bore no resemblance to the biblical character, the theory would fail to connect the name to that figure, since the descriptions do not fit him. The background cases succeeded because the associated descriptions happened to be true of the intended referent. The theory has generalised from these fortunate cases to a universal principle, but the principle breaks down when the descriptions are inaccurate, mythologised, or entirely fictitious. What is needed is an account of naming that does not make reference hostage to the accuracy of speakers' beliefs about the referent.

background_cases: The French sentence 'La neige est blanche' is translated into English as 'Snow is white,' and the translation is regarded as adequate because the two sentences have the same truth-conditions and the same propositional content — both are true if and only if snow is white.
The German sentence 'Der Morgenstern ist ein Planet' is translated into English as 'The morning star is a planet,' and the translation preserves the propositional content exactly.
The Latin sentence 'Caesar Galliam vicit' is translated as 'Caesar conquered Gaul,' and the translation is adequate because it preserves the same factual claim about the same historical events.
definition: A translation of a sentence S from language L1 into a sentence T in language L2 is correct if and only if (i) S and T have the same truth-conditions, (ii) the logical structure of T mirrors the logical structure of S, and (iii) for each content word in S, T contains a synonym in L2.
conditions: S and T have the same truth-conditions
The logical structure of T mirrors that of S
Each content word in S has a synonym in T
counterexample: Consider the German word 'Schadenfreude,' which denotes the pleasure one takes in another person's misfortune. A translator rendering a German sentence containing 'Schadenfreude' into English cannot find a single English word that is synonymous with it; the concept must be rendered by a phrase or a paraphrase. Condition (iii) — that each content word in the original have a synonym in the target language — cannot be met. Yet adequate translations of sentences containing 'Schadenfreude' are produced every day, using paraphrases, circumlocutions, or the borrowed word itself. The translation may preserve the truth-conditions and the logical structure, but it does not do so by providing word-for-word synonyms, because the required synonyms do not exist.
abductive_insight: The background cases — French to English, German to English, Latin to English — all involved sentences whose content words happened to have close equivalents in the target language. The word-for-word synonym condition worked because the particular languages and sentences chosen were amenable to it. But what made the translations adequate was the preservation of propositional content, not the availability of word-for-word correspondences. The definition has generalised from cases of easy translatability to a universal requirement that fails when languages carve experience differently.
passage:
The simplest cases of translation seem to support a straightforward theory: a translation is correct when it preserves truth-conditions, mirrors logical structure, and replaces each content word with a synonym in the target language. 'La neige est blanche' becomes 'Snow is white'; 'Der Morgenstern ist ein Planet' becomes 'The morning star is a planet'; 'Caesar Galliam vicit' becomes 'Caesar conquered Gaul.' In each case, the truth-conditions are the same, the logical structure is preserved, and each word finds a natural counterpart. From these cases one might extract the definition: correct translation requires sameness of truth-conditions, parallel logical structure, and word-for-word synonymy.

Yet this definition, shaped by cases of easy translatability between closely related languages, encounters a fundamental difficulty when the languages in question carve experience differently. Consider the German word 'Schadenfreude' — the pleasure derived from another's misfortune. English has no single word for this concept. A translator rendering a German sentence that contains 'Schadenfreude' cannot satisfy condition (iii): there is no English synonym for the word, because the English lexicon does not contain a term with the same meaning. The translator must resort to a phrase — 'pleasure in another's misfortune' — or to a paraphrase, or to the expedient of borrowing the German word itself. The resulting translation may preserve the truth-conditions perfectly and maintain the logical structure of the original, but it does not do so by providing a synonym for each content word. The background cases worked because the particular languages and sentences chosen happened to have closely matching vocabularies. The definition has generalised from this convenient alignment to a universal requirement — word-for-word synonymy — that fails whenever the lexical resources of the two languages diverge. What is needed is a theory of translation that identifies the preservation of propositional content as the central requirement, without demanding that this preservation be achieved by word-level correspondences that may not exist.

background_cases: The sentence 'Snow is white' means that snow is white: the sentence is true if and only if snow is white, and competent English speakers use the sentence to assert this and no other proposition.

The sentence 'Paris is the capital of France' means that Paris is the capital of France: its truth-conditions are determinate, and competent speakers employ it to express a definite proposition about the political geography of France.

The sentence 'Water boils at 100 degrees Celsius' means that water boils at 100 degrees Celsius: it states a determinate condition whose truth or falsity can be ascertained by observation, and competent speakers use it to assert just this.
definition: The meaning of a declarative sentence S is given by its truth-conditions if and only if (i) there is a determinate set of conditions under which S is true, (ii) a competent speaker of the language can state what those conditions are, and (iii) any two sentences with the same truth-conditions express the same proposition.
conditions: There is a determinate set of conditions under which S is true
A competent speaker of the language can state what those conditions are
Any two sentences with the same truth-conditions express the same proposition
counterexample: Consider the sentences 'Hesperus is a planet' and 'Phosphorus is a planet.' Since Hesperus and Phosphorus are the same celestial body (Venus), the two sentences have exactly the same truth-conditions: both are true if and only if Venus is a planet. Condition (iii) therefore requires that they express the same proposition. Yet a competent speaker may believe that Hesperus is a planet while doubting whether Phosphorus is, which strongly suggests that the two sentences express different propositions.
abductive_insight: The background cases involve sentences whose key terms have transparent referents — 'snow,' 'Paris,' 'water' — and so the identity of truth-conditions reliably tracks the identity of propositional content. But when coreferential terms with different senses are involved, truth-conditions can coincide while propositional content diverges. The definition's third condition assumes that truth-conditions exhaust propositional content, overlooking the contribution of sense or mode of presentation.
passage:
The three background cases — 'Snow is white,' 'Paris is the capital of France,' and 'Water boils at 100 degrees Celsius' — conform beautifully to a truth-conditional account of meaning. Each sentence has determinate truth-conditions, a competent speaker can state them, and there is no temptation to suppose that any other sentence with the same truth-conditions might express a different proposition. One might therefore confidently propose that meaning is exhausted by truth-conditions, and that identity of truth-conditions guarantees identity of propositional content.

Yet this proposal encounters a difficulty that the background cases, in their transparency, concealed. Consider the sentences 'Hesperus is a planet' and 'Phosphorus is a planet.' Since Hesperus and Phosphorus are the same celestial body — the planet Venus — these sentences share truth-conditions: both are true if and only if Venus is a planet. On the proposed account, they express the same proposition. But a competent speaker of English who does not know that Hesperus is Phosphorus may sincerely assent to the one while withholding assent from the other. This strongly suggests that the two sentences differ in propositional content despite sharing truth-conditions. The background cases all involved terms whose referents were transparent, so that identity of truth-conditions coincided with identity of the way the referent was presented. The Hesperus/Phosphorus case demonstrates that when terms with different senses corefer, truth-conditions alone cannot distinguish the propositions expressed. The definition must be supplemented to account for the contribution that sense or mode of presentation makes to propositional content.

background_cases: When a minister says 'I now pronounce you husband and wife' in the course of a wedding ceremony, the utterance constitutes the act of marrying the couple: the speaker has the requisite authority, the conventional procedure is followed, and the circumstances are appropriate.

When a judge says 'I sentence you to five years' imprisonment' in a court of law, the utterance constitutes the act of sentencing: the speaker has the authority to impose sentences, the procedure is followed, and the circumstances are those of a trial.

When a chairperson says 'The meeting is adjourned' at the end of a committee session, the utterance constitutes the act of adjourning: the speaker has the authority to close the meeting, the conventional formula is employed, and the circumstances are appropriate.
definition: An utterance U constitutes a performative act if and only if (i) U is produced by a speaker who possesses the requisite institutional authority, (ii) U employs the conventional formula associated with the act in question, and (iii) the circumstances of utterance are those conventionally required for the performance of the act.
conditions: U is produced by a speaker who possesses the requisite institutional authority
U employs the conventional formula associated with the act in question
The circumstances of utterance are those conventionally required for the performance of the act
counterexample: Consider a person who says 'I promise to return your book by Friday' in an ordinary conversation. The utterance constitutes a promise — a performative act. Yet the speaker possesses no special institutional authority; promising is not an act that requires authority conferred by an institution. The utterance employs a conventional formula ('I promise'), and the circumstances are appropriate. But condition (i) fails, since no institutional authority is needed. Nevertheless, the promise is successfully made.
abductive_insight: The background cases are all institutional performatives — acts that require the backing of an institution (the church, the court, the committee). This led the definition to treat institutional authority as a necessary condition. But promising, betting, apologising, and thanking are performative acts that require no institutional authority whatever. The definition has been constructed from too narrow a base of cases, all of which happened to be institutional, and thereby excludes the large class of performatives that operate through interpersonal convention rather than institutional mandate.
passage:
The wedding ceremony, the judicial sentencing, and the committee adjournment share a common structure: in each, a person vested with institutional authority produces an utterance in the appropriate conventional form and circumstances, and the utterance thereby constitutes the performance of a particular act. One might therefore suppose that performative utterances in general require institutional authority, a conventional formula, and appropriate circumstances.

Yet this analysis is too narrow, as a simple case reveals. When a person says to a friend, 'I promise to return your book by Friday,' the utterance constitutes a promise. It is a performative act in the fullest sense: by saying 'I promise,' the speaker does not describe a pre-existing state of affairs but brings a new obligation into existence. Yet the speaker possesses no institutional authority. Promising is not an act that requires the backing of a church, a court, or a committee. Anyone possessed of the relevant communicative capacities can make a promise, in any circumstances where the utterance is seriously intended. The background cases, being uniformly institutional, led the definition to treat institutional authority as essential to performative speech. But the case of promising — and one might add betting, apologising, thanking, and numerous other performative acts — demonstrates that many performatives operate through interpersonal convention rather than institutional mandate. The definition must be revised to accommodate performative acts that do not presuppose institutional authority, perhaps by replacing the authority condition with a broader requirement concerning the speaker's standing to perform the act in question.

background_cases: The predicate 'is tall' is vague: there is no precise height at which a person becomes tall, and for any proposed boundary one can construct a Sorites series — a sequence of persons differing by one millimetre in height — that makes the boundary appear arbitrary.

The predicate 'is bald' is vague: there is no precise number of hairs at which a person ceases to be bald, and a Sorites series of persons differing by one hair reveals the impossibility of drawing a sharp line.

The predicate 'is a heap' is vague: there is no precise number of grains at which a collection becomes a heap, and the classical Sorites argument exploits this absence of a sharp boundary to derive an absurd conclusion.
definition: A predicate F is vague if and only if (i) F gives rise to a Sorites series — a sequence of cases differing by imperceptibly small increments along a single dimension, such that adjacent cases cannot be distinguished with respect to the applicability of F, (ii) the endpoints of the series clearly differ in the applicability of F, and (iii) there is no sharp boundary within the series at which F ceases to apply.
conditions: F gives rise to a Sorites series along a single dimension
The endpoints of the series clearly differ in the applicability of F
There is no sharp boundary within the series at which F ceases to apply
counterexample: Consider the predicate 'is a religion.' It is vague — there are borderline cases such as Confucianism, certain forms of secular Buddhism, and various new-age movements about which competent speakers disagree as to whether they constitute religions. Yet this vagueness does not arise from a Sorites series along a single dimension. There is no single parameter — number of rituals, degree of supernaturalism, size of congregation — such that a Sorites series along that parameter generates the characteristic difficulty. The predicate is vague because the concept of a religion is multi-dimensional and its boundaries are indeterminate in a way that is not reducible to incremental variation along any one axis. Condition (i) fails, yet the predicate is plainly vague.
abductive_insight: The background cases — 'tall,' 'bald,' 'heap' — are all vague along a single measurable dimension (height, number of hairs, number of grains), which made the Sorites-series condition seem essential to vagueness. But 'is a religion' is vague without being Sorites-susceptible along any single dimension. Its vagueness is multi-dimensional and combinatorial. The definition has been constructed from a class of one-dimensional predicates and thereby fails to capture the broader phenomenon of vagueness, which can arise from the indeterminacy of multi-dimensional concepts.
passage:
The predicates 'tall,' 'bald,' and 'heap' are paradigmatic instances of vagueness, and they share a common structural feature: each gives rise to a Sorites series along a single measurable dimension — height, number of hairs, number of grains — such that adjacent members of the series are indistinguishable with respect to the predicate's applicability, even though the endpoints differ clearly. One might therefore define vagueness in terms of susceptibility to Sorites reasoning: a predicate is vague if and only if it gives rise to such a series, the endpoints clearly differ, and no sharp boundary can be drawn.

Yet this analysis proves too narrow. Consider the predicate 'is a religion.' Competent speakers disagree about whether Confucianism, certain forms of secular Buddhism, or various new-age spiritual movements are properly called religions. The predicate has borderline cases and lacks a sharp boundary — it is, by any reasonable pre-theoretic standard, vague. But this vagueness does not arise from a Sorites series along a single dimension. There is no single parameter — degree of supernaturalism, number of rituals, size of the community of adherents — along which one could arrange a sequence of cases that generates the characteristic Sorites difficulty. The vagueness of 'is a religion' is multi-dimensional: it arises from the interaction of several criteria, none of which is individually decisive, and the boundary of the concept is indeterminate in a way that cannot be reduced to incremental variation along any one axis. The background cases, all involving one-dimensional predicates, suggested that Sorites-susceptibility is the essence of vagueness. The case of 'is a religion' reveals that vagueness is a broader phenomenon than the Sorites structure alone can capture, and the definition must be revised accordingly.

background_cases: The sentence 'Snow is white' means that snow is white; its meaning is given by the conditions under which it is true — namely, the condition that the stuff denoted by 'snow' bears the property denoted by 'white.'
The sentence 'Paris is the capital of France' means that Paris is the capital of France; its meaning is fully captured by specifying the conditions that must obtain for the sentence to be true.

The sentence 'Water boils at 100 degrees Celsius at standard atmospheric pressure' means what it does because its truth-conditions are perfectly determinate: there is a precise set of physical circumstances under which the sentence is true.
definition: The meaning of a declarative sentence S is given by its truth-conditions if and only if (i) S expresses a determinate proposition, (ii) the truth-conditions of S are specifiable in a metalanguage, and (iii) grasping the meaning of S consists in knowing its truth-conditions.
conditions: S expresses a determinate proposition
The truth-conditions are specifiable in a metalanguage
Grasping the meaning consists in knowing the truth-conditions
counterexample: Consider the sentence 'That was a generous remark,' uttered with heavy irony. The truth-conditions of the sentence, taken literally, are that a certain remark was generous. These conditions are specifiable in a metalanguage. Yet grasping the meaning of the utterance does not consist in knowing these truth-conditions; the speaker means the opposite of what the sentence literally says. The meaning of the utterance is that the remark was mean-spirited or ungracious, and no specification of the literal truth-conditions captures this.
