The third condition fails because the meaning of the utterance diverges from the truth-conditions of the sentence.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve sentences used literally and sincerely, where the meaning of the utterance coincides with the truth-conditions of the sentence. The definition abstracts from these cases the principle that meaning is truth-conditions. But irony demonstrates that the meaning of an utterance may systematically diverge from the truth-conditions of the sentence uttered, revealing that the truth-conditional account captures sentence meaning in the literal case but fails to accommodate the broader phenomenon of speaker meaning in non-literal uses.
passage:
The truth-conditional account of meaning draws its support from cases in which the meaning of a sentence is transparently captured by specifying the conditions under which it is true. The sentence 'Snow is white' means that snow is white; there is nothing more to its meaning than its truth-conditions. 'Paris is the capital of France' means that Paris is the capital of France — again, the truth-conditions exhaust the meaning. 'Water boils at 100 degrees Celsius at standard pressure' says precisely what its truth-conditions specify. In each case, to grasp the meaning is to know the conditions under which the sentence is true.

One might therefore define meaning as truth-conditions: the meaning of a declarative sentence is given by specifying, in a metalanguage, the conditions under which the sentence is true, and grasping the meaning consists in knowing those conditions. This definition captures the paradigm cases with admirable economy. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises from the pervasive phenomenon of irony. Consider a speaker who, upon hearing a colleague's cutting and dismissive remark, says with heavy irony, 'That was a generous remark.' The truth-conditions of the sentence, taken literally, are that the remark in question was generous. These conditions are perfectly specifiable in a metalanguage. But the meaning of the utterance is precisely the opposite: the speaker means that the remark was ungracious and mean-spirited. To grasp the meaning is not to know the truth-conditions of the sentence but to recognise that the speaker is inverting them. The third condition of the definition is not met — grasping the meaning of the ironic utterance does not consist in knowing its literal truth-conditions. The background cases all involved literal, sincere assertion, and the truth-conditional account was abstracted from such cases. Irony reveals that the meaning of an utterance may systematically diverge from the truth-conditions of the sentence employed, and that a complete account of meaning must accommodate speaker meaning alongside sentence meaning.

background_cases: The name 'Aristotle' refers to a particular individual because there is a causal-historical chain linking current uses of the name back through a succession of speakers to an original act of naming in which the name was bestowed upon the infant Aristotle.

The name 'London' refers to a particular city because current uses of the name are connected, through a continuous chain of communicative acts, to an original christening of the settlement by its founders or early inhabitants.

The name 'Halley's Comet' refers to a particular astronomical body because the name was introduced in connection with a specific comet observed by Edmond Halley, and subsequent uses inherit their reference from this initial baptismal event.
definition: A proper name N refers to an individual O if and only if (i) there is a causal-historical chain of communication linking the current use of N back to an initial act of naming in which N was bestowed upon O, (ii) each link in the chain preserves the reference of N by the speaker's intention to use N with the same reference as the person from whom she acquired it, and (iii) the chain is unbroken — there is no point at which the reference shifts to a different individual.
conditions: There is a causal-historical chain from current use back to an initial naming of O
Each link preserves reference through the speaker's intention to co-refer
The chain is unbroken — reference never shifts
counterexample: Consider the name 'Madagascar.' According to historical accounts, Marco Polo used the name to refer to a portion of the African mainland — perhaps the region around Mogadishu. Through a series of miscommunications and misidentifications, the name came to be applied to the large island off the south-eastern coast of Africa. The causal-historical chain is intact, and at each link the speaker intended to use the name with the same reference as the previous speaker. Yet the reference shifted: 'Madagascar' now refers to the island, not to the mainland region to which it was originally applied. The third condition requires an unbroken chain with no reference shift, yet the chain is unbroken in terms of communicative links and co-referential intentions, even though the reference has shifted.
abductive_insight: The background cases — Aristotle, London, Halley's Comet — all involve causal-historical chains in which reference is preserved faithfully from the initial naming to the present day. The definition abstracts from these cases the requirement that the chain be unbroken and reference-preserving. But the Madagascar case reveals that a chain may be causally and intentionally continuous and yet produce a reference shift through accumulated error or confusion. Each speaker intended to co-refer with her predecessors, and the chain is unbroken, but the cumulative effect of small misidentifications resulted in a wholesale change of referent. The definition cannot accommodate such cases without either declaring the chain broken — which it is not, in terms of communicative links — or acknowledging that reference can shift even within a continuous chain.
passage:
The causal-historical theory of reference draws its plausibility from cases in which a name's reference is transmitted faithfully through a chain of communicative acts stretching back to an original baptism. The name 'Aristotle' reaches us through an unbroken succession of speakers, each of whom intended to use the name with the same reference as the person from whom she acquired it, and the chain terminates in an act of naming directed at a particular infant in Stagira. The name 'London' is similarly connected to an original christening of a settlement. 'Halley's Comet' inherits its reference from Halley's identification of a particular astronomical body. In each case, the chain is faithful: reference is preserved at every link.

One might therefore define reference as requiring a causal-historical chain of communication, reference-preserving intentions at each link, and continuity — the stipulation that the reference never shifts. This definition captures the paradigm cases admirably. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is provided by the history of the name 'Madagascar.' According to the best available historical evidence, the name was originally applied by Marco Polo — or by his sources — to a portion of the African mainland, perhaps the region around Mogadishu. Through a sequence of miscommunications, the name migrated to the large island off Africa's south-eastern coast. At each link in the chain, the speaker intended to use 'Madagascar' with the same reference as the person from whom she learned it. The chain is causally and intentionally continuous. Yet the reference shifted: the name now denotes the island, not the mainland. The definition requires that the chain be unbroken and reference-preserving, and in a sense it is — the intentional links are all intact. But the cumulative effect of small errors of identification produced a wholesale change of referent. The definition cannot explain how a chain that satisfies the co-reference condition at every individual link can nevertheless produce a global reference shift, suggesting that the preservation of reference requires more than local co-referential intentions.

background_cases: When a judge in a courtroom says 'I sentence you to five years,' the utterance constitutes a sentencing because the speaker occupies the appropriate institutional role, the utterance follows the prescribed procedural form, and the institutional setting is one in which such utterances have legal force.

When a minister at a wedding says 'I now pronounce you husband and wife,' the utterance constitutes a marriage because the speaker is authorised by law, the ceremony follows the required procedure, and the institutional context validates the performative.

When a presiding officer says 'The meeting is adjourned,' the utterance constitutes an adjournment because the speaker holds the appropriate office, the procedural conditions are satisfied, and the institutional framework recognises the utterance as effecting the adjournment.
definition: An utterance U constitutes a performative speech act if and only if (i) U is uttered by a speaker who occupies an institutional role that authorises the performance, (ii) U follows the conventional form prescribed for the act in question, and (iii) the institutional context in which U is uttered is one that recognises U as having the appropriate force.
conditions: The speaker occupies an authorising institutional role
U follows the prescribed conventional form
The institutional context recognises U as having force
counterexample: Consider a person who says 'I bet you five pounds it will rain tomorrow' to a friend during a casual conversation. She occupies no institutional role; there is no prescribed form for making a bet; and there is no institutional context — no bookmaker's office, no formal wagering framework — that recognises the utterance. Yet a bet has been made. The friend accepts, and both parties regard themselves as bound. The performative succeeds entirely outside any institutional framework, through nothing more than the mutual understanding of the speakers. None of the three conditions is met.
abductive_insight: The background cases — sentencing, marriage, adjournment — all involve performatives that succeed within institutional frameworks: a courtroom, a wedding ceremony, a formal meeting. The definition abstracts from these cases the requirements of institutional role, conventional form, and institutional recognition. But the casual bet reveals that performative speech acts can succeed through informal mutual agreement, without any institutional scaffolding. The definition mistakes the institutional character of its paradigm cases for a necessary feature of performative speech acts in general, overlooking the large class of everyday performatives — bets, promises, dares, apologies — that operate through conversational norms rather than institutional authority.
passage:
The performative speech acts most frequently analysed in the philosophical literature occur within well-defined institutional contexts. A judge's sentencing, a minister's pronouncement of marriage, a chairperson's adjournment of a meeting — each succeeds because the speaker occupies an authorising role, the utterance follows a prescribed form, and the institutional setting recognises the utterance as having the relevant force. The institutional machinery, so to speak, converts the utterance into the act.

One might therefore define a performative speech act as an utterance made by a speaker occupying an appropriate institutional role, following the prescribed conventional form, within an institutional context that recognises the utterance as efficacious. This definition captures the paradigm cases with precision. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises in the most ordinary of settings. Suppose two friends are walking together and one says, 'I bet you five pounds it will rain tomorrow.' The other replies, 'Done.' A bet has been made. Both parties regard themselves as bound, and if it rains, the friend who took the bet will expect to collect her five pounds. Yet no institutional role authorises the speaker — she is not a bookmaker, nor does she hold any office. There is no prescribed conventional form for making a casual bet — the words might just as easily have been 'I wager' or 'Five pounds says it rains.' And there is no institutional context — no betting shop, no formal wagering licence, no regulatory body — that recognises the utterance. The performative succeeds entirely through the mutual understanding and conversational cooperation of the two speakers. The definition, abstracted from the institutional cases, cannot accommodate this everyday performative, revealing that institutional authority is a sufficient but not necessary condition for the success of a performative speech act.

background_cases: The word 'bank' is ambiguous between a financial institution and the side of a river — the two senses are unrelated, and a hearer must rely upon context to determine which sense is intended; the ambiguity arises from the accidental convergence of two distinct words upon a single phonetic form.

The word 'bat' is ambiguous between a flying mammal and a piece of sporting equipment — again, the two senses are historically unrelated, and context resolves the ambiguity without difficulty in normal conversation.

The word 'crane' is ambiguous between a large bird and a lifting machine — the senses may have a distant etymological connection, but in contemporary usage they function as distinct meanings, and a hearer selects the appropriate sense by attending to the surrounding discourse.
definition: A sentence S is ambiguous if and only if (i) S has two or more distinct meanings, (ii) the distinct meanings arise from the presence of a word or phrase in S that has two or more unrelated senses, and (iii) a competent hearer cannot determine which meaning is intended without additional context.
conditions: S has two or more distinct meanings
The meanings arise from a word with unrelated senses
A hearer needs context to determine the intended meaning
counterexample: Consider the sentence 'Visiting relatives can be tiresome.' This sentence is ambiguous — it may mean that the act of visiting one's relatives is tiresome, or that relatives who are visiting are tiresome. But the ambiguity does not arise from any word having two unrelated senses. Every word in the sentence has a single, unambiguous meaning. The ambiguity arises from the syntactic structure: 'visiting' may be parsed as a gerund (the act of visiting) or as a participial adjective (relatives who are visiting). The two readings assign different grammatical roles to the same word without invoking different senses. Condition (ii) is not satisfied, yet the sentence is genuinely ambiguous.
abductive_insight: The background cases — 'bank,' 'bat,' 'crane' — all involve lexical ambiguity, in which a single word happens to bear two unrelated meanings. The definition captures this mechanism faithfully. But 'Visiting relatives can be tiresome' is ambiguous for a wholly different reason — the syntactic structure admits of two parsings — revealing that the definition has identified one source of ambiguity (lexical) and mistaken it for the only source, neglecting structural or syntactic ambiguity entirely.
passage:
The most familiar examples of ambiguity in natural language involve words that bear two or more unrelated senses. The word 'bank' may denote a financial institution or the side of a river; the word 'bat' may denote a flying mammal or a piece of sporting equipment; the word 'crane' may denote a large wading bird or a machine for lifting heavy objects. In each case, the ambiguity arises because a single phonetic form has come to carry two historically unrelated meanings, and a hearer must rely upon context — the surrounding words, the topic of conversation, the shared situation — to determine which sense is intended.

One might codify these observations by defining ambiguity as the property of a sentence that has two or more distinct meanings arising from a word or phrase with two or more unrelated senses, such that context is required to resolve the indeterminacy. This definition accommodates the paradigm cases of lexical ambiguity with considerable precision. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is the sentence 'Visiting relatives can be tiresome.' This sentence is genuinely ambiguous — it may mean either that the activity of visiting one's relatives is tiresome or that relatives who come to visit are tiresome. But the ambiguity does not arise from any word having two unrelated senses. The word 'visiting' has a single meaning; 'relatives' has a single meaning; 'tiresome' has a single meaning. The ambiguity arises entirely from the syntactic structure of the sentence: 'visiting' may be parsed as a gerund modifying the activity or as a participial adjective modifying the relatives. The two readings result not from lexical multiplicity but from structural indeterminacy. Condition (ii) is not satisfied. The background cases all involved ambiguities generated by lexical polysemy, and the definition captures that mechanism faithfully. But the phenomenon of syntactic ambiguity — in which the structure of a sentence admits of more than one parsing, even though every word has a single determinate sense — lies entirely beyond its reach.

background_cases: The name 'London' denotes the city of London — the name stands in a direct relation to a particular city, and the denotation is secured by the existence of the city and the convention of using the name to refer to it.
The definite description 'the highest mountain in the world' denotes Mount Everest — the description picks out a particular mountain by specifying a property that it uniquely satisfies.
The numeral '7' denotes the number seven — the symbol stands in a direct relation to an abstract mathematical object, and the denotation is secured by the conventions of mathematical notation.
definition: An expression E denotes an entity X if and only if (i) E stands in a conventional relation of reference to X, (ii) X exists, and (iii) the denotation of E is determined by the meaning of E together with the way the world is.
conditions: E stands in a conventional referential relation to X
X exists
Denotation is determined by meaning plus the state of the world
counterexample: Consider the expression 'the present king of France,' uttered in 1905 or today. The expression has a determinate meaning — it purports to refer to whoever is presently king of France. But France is a republic, and no such person exists. Condition (ii) is violated. Yet the expression is meaningful: the sentence 'The present king of France is bald' is grammatically well-formed, semantically intelligible, and — one might argue — either true or false (or neither). The expression has meaning and appears to denote, yet there is no entity for it to denote. The definition cannot accommodate meaningful expressions that fail to denote because their purported referent does not exist.
abductive_insight: The background cases — 'London,' 'the highest mountain,' '7' — all involve expressions whose referents exist. The definition captures the logic of successful denotation but presupposes the existence of the referent. When the referent does not exist, the definition either declares the expression meaningless — which is plainly wrong — or declares that it does not denote — which raises the question of how a meaningful description can fail to denote while remaining intelligible. The definition lacks the resources to handle empty denotation, which is a central problem in the analysis of meaning.
passage:
The expressions most straightforwardly analysed in terms of denotation are those whose referents plainly exist. The name 'London' denotes the city of London — the city exists, the convention is in place, and the name refers. The description 'the highest mountain in the world' denotes Mount Everest — the mountain exists and uniquely satisfies the description. The numeral '7' denotes the number seven — the abstract object exists (by the lights of standard mathematical practice) and the convention of notation is fixed. In each case, the expression, the convention, and the existence of the referent jointly secure the denotation.

One might codify these observations by defining denotation as a relation in which an expression stands in a conventional referential relation to an existing entity, with the denotation determined by meaning and the state of the world. This definition accommodates the paradigm cases cleanly. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is the definite description 'the present king of France.' The expression has a perfectly determinate meaning — it purports to pick out whoever presently occupies the French throne. The meaning is composed, in the ordinary way, from the meanings of its parts and their syntactic arrangement. But France has been a republic for over a century, and no such person exists. Condition (ii) is violated. The definition must then say either that the expression does not denote or that it is meaningless. The latter is plainly wrong — the sentence 'The present king of France is bald' is perfectly intelligible. The former raises a question the definition cannot answer: how is it that a meaningful, well-formed expression can fail to denote while remaining fully intelligible? The background cases all involved expressions with existing referents, and the definition captures the logic of successful denotation. But the phenomenon of meaningful expressions with empty denotation — a phenomenon that arises whenever descriptions outrun the world — lies beyond its reach.

background_cases: The sentence 'Snow is white' is true if and only if snow is white — the meaning of the sentence is given by specifying the conditions under which it is true, and understanding the sentence consists in knowing what state of affairs would make it true.

The sentence 'Paris is the capital of France' is true if and only if Paris is the capital of France — again, meaning is identified with truth-conditions, and understanding consists in grasping what would make the sentence true.

The sentence 'Water boils at 100 degrees Celsius at standard pressure' is true if and only if water boils at 100 degrees Celsius at standard pressure — the truth-conditions specify the state of affairs that must obtain for the sentence to be true.
definition: The meaning of a declarative sentence S is its truth-conditions — that is, S means what it means if and only if (i) there is a specifiable state of affairs that must obtain for S to be true, (ii) a competent speaker who understands S can recognise whether the state of affairs obtains, and (iii) two sentences have the same meaning if and only if they have the same truth-conditions.
conditions: There is a specifiable state of affairs for S's truth
A competent speaker can recognise whether it obtains
Same truth-conditions entail same meaning
counterexample: Consider the sentences 'Hesperus is a planet' and 'Phosphorus is a planet.' Since Hesperus is Phosphorus — both are the planet Venus — these sentences have exactly the same truth-conditions: both are true if and only if Venus is a planet. Condition (iii) would therefore require that they have the same meaning. Yet the two sentences differ in meaning — an ancient astronomer who understood both names might assent to one sentence while dissenting from the other, precisely because he did not know that the two names refer to the same celestial body. Sentences may share truth-conditions while differing in meaning.
abductive_insight: The background cases — 'Snow is white,' 'Paris is the capital of France,' 'Water boils at 100 degrees' — all involve sentences whose truth-conditions are transparently related to their meaning. The definition captures this transparent relationship. But when sentences contain co-referential expressions that differ in cognitive significance — as 'Hesperus' and 'Phosphorus' do — sameness of truth-conditions fails to guarantee sameness of meaning. The definition identifies meaning with truth-conditions, but meaning includes a dimension of cognitive significance or mode of presentation that truth-conditions alone do not capture.
passage:
The sentences that provide the most natural illustrations of the truth-conditional theory of meaning are those in which the relation between meaning and truth-conditions is maximally transparent. 'Snow is white' is true if and only if snow is white — the truth-conditions simply mirror the content of the sentence. 'Paris is the capital of France' is true if and only if Paris is the capital of France. 'Water boils at 100 degrees Celsius at standard pressure' is true if and only if the relevant physical condition obtains. In each case, specifying the truth-conditions seems to specify the meaning, and understanding the sentence seems to consist in knowing what state of affairs would make it true.

One might codify these observations by defining the meaning of a declarative sentence as its truth-conditions, adding that a competent speaker can recognise whether the relevant state of affairs obtains and that sentences with the same truth-conditions have the same meaning. This definition captures the paradigm cases with elegant simplicity. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises from the phenomenon of co-referential names with different cognitive significance. The sentences 'Hesperus is a planet' and 'Phosphorus is a planet' have exactly the same truth-conditions, since Hesperus and Phosphorus are the same celestial body — Venus. Both sentences are true if and only if Venus is a planet. Condition (iii) therefore requires that they have the same meaning. But the sentences plainly differ in meaning: an ancient astronomer who understood both names might believe that Hesperus is a planet while denying that Phosphorus is, because he did not know that the bright object visible in the evening and the bright object visible in the morning are one and the same. The two names present their common referent under different aspects, and this difference in mode of presentation constitutes a difference in meaning that the truth-conditional account cannot capture. The background cases all involved sentences whose meaning was exhausted by their truth-conditions, but the Hesperus-Phosphorus case reveals a dimension of meaning — cognitive significance — that lies beyond the reach of truth-conditions alone.

background_cases: The word 'triangle' means a three-sided closed plane figure; a competent speaker applies the word to objects possessing this property and withholds it from objects lacking it, and the word means what it does because it is conventionally associated with this defining property.

The word 'bachelor' means an unmarried adult male; competent speakers apply it on the basis of the satisfaction of these defining conditions, and the meaning of the word is given by the conditions that must be met for its correct application.

The word 'vixen' means a female fox; the meaning is constituted by the conjunction of the properties of being female and being a fox, and competent speakers master the word by learning these defining conditions.
definition: A word W means a property or complex of properties F if and only if (i) competent speakers apply W to objects possessing F and withhold it from objects lacking F, (ii) the property F constitutes a set of necessary and sufficient conditions for the application of W, and (iii) any competent speaker who fully understands W can state the conditions that constitute F.
conditions: Competent speakers apply W to objects possessing F and withhold it from objects lacking F
F constitutes necessary and sufficient conditions for the application of W
Any competent speaker who fully understands W can state the conditions constituting F
counterexample: Consider the word 'game.' Competent speakers apply it readily and with high agreement to a wide range of activities — chess, football, ring-around-the-roses, solitaire. Yet one might observe that there appears to be no property or complex of properties common to all games that constitutes a set of necessary and sufficient conditions. Some games involve competition and others do not. Some require skill and others depend on chance. Some are played for amusement and others for high stakes. Moreover, although competent speakers use the word with great facility, they are unable to state a set of conditions that is both necessary and sufficient. The word 'game' means something perfectly definite to every English speaker, but its meaning does not take the form the definition requires.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve words whose meanings can be stated as a set of necessary and sufficient conditions that any competent speaker can articulate — 'triangle,' 'bachelor,' 'vixen.' The definition codifies this as the general structure of word-meaning. Yet the case of 'game' reveals that many perfectly meaningful words resist analysis into necessary and sufficient conditions. Their application is governed not by a defining set of properties but by a network of overlapping resemblances — what one might call a family-resemblance structure — that defies the definitional format the background cases exemplify. The definition mistakes one particularly tidy species of meaning for the genus.
passage:
The words whose meanings are most readily analysed tend to be those for which a set of defining conditions can be stated. 'Triangle' means three-sided closed plane figure. 'Bachelor' means unmarried adult male. 'Vixen' means female fox. In each case, the meaning is constituted by a set of necessary and sufficient conditions that any competent speaker can articulate, and the word is correctly applied to all and only those objects that satisfy the conditions.

One might codify this pattern by the following definition: a word means a property or complex of properties if and only if competent speakers apply it on the basis of that property, the property constitutes necessary and sufficient conditions for application, and any competent speaker can state these conditions. This definition captures the structure of meaning as it appears in the background cases. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises with the word 'game.' Competent English speakers apply this word readily and with high agreement to an enormous range of activities — chess, football, ring-around-the-roses, patience, war-games, word-games. Yet one might observe that there appears to be no property common to all these activities that constitutes a necessary and sufficient condition for being a game. Some games involve competition, but solitaire does not. Some require skill, but games of pure chance do not. Some are played for amusement, but professional sports are played for livelihood. Moreover, although competent speakers use the word with perfect facility, they find themselves unable to state a set of conditions that captures all games and excludes all non-games. The meaning of 'game' appears to be constituted not by a defining set of properties but by a network of overlapping resemblances among the activities to which the word is applied. The definition, by requiring necessary and sufficient conditions, fails to accommodate this pervasive and important species of meaning.

background_cases: The name 'Hesperus' refers to Venus because it is associated with the description 'the bright celestial body visible in the evening sky,' and Venus is the object that uniquely satisfies this description; the reference is mediated by the descriptive content that speakers associate with the name.

The name 'Cicero' refers to the Roman orator because speakers associate with it such descriptions as 'the author of the Catilinarian orations' and 'the consul who suppressed Catiline's conspiracy,' and Marcus Tullius Cicero is the individual who uniquely satisfies these descriptions.

The name 'Homer' refers to the poet of the Iliad and the Odyssey because speakers associate the name with the description 'the author of the Iliad and the Odyssey,' and the reference of the name is determined by whoever uniquely satisfies this description.
definition: A proper name N refers to an individual x if and only if (i) speakers who use N associate with it a definite description or set of descriptions D, (ii) x is the unique individual that satisfies D, and (iii) the reference of N is determined by D — that is, N refers to x because x satisfies D.
conditions: Speakers associate with N a definite description or set of descriptions D
x uniquely satisfies D
The reference of N is determined by D
counterexample: Consider the name 'Madagascar.' European sailors originally acquired this name from Marco Polo, who used it to refer to a portion of the African mainland. Through a geographical error, the name came to be applied to the large island off the southeast coast of Africa. The descriptions that current speakers associate with 'Madagascar' — 'the large island off southeast Africa,' 'the home of lemurs' — are satisfied by the island. But the name was originally introduced to refer to a part of the mainland, and the island was not what Marco Polo intended to designate. The reference has shifted: 'Madagascar' now refers to the island, not to the mainland region that Marco Polo described. The current reference is not determined by the original descriptive content associated with the name but by the community's subsequent use.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve names whose reference appears to be fixed by the descriptions speakers associate with them — 'the evening star,' 'the author of the Catilinarian orations,' 'the poet of the Iliad.' The definition codifies this descriptive mediation as the mechanism of reference. Yet the Madagascar case reveals that the reference of a name can shift through communicative practice, detaching from the original descriptive content and attaching to a new referent via the community's evolving usage. This suggests that reference is sustained not solely by descriptive content but by the causal-historical chain of communicative acts through which the name is transmitted and, on occasion, redirected.

The names whose reference is most transparent tend to be those for which speakers can articulate a description that picks out a unique individual. 'Hesperus' refers to Venus because it is associated with 'the bright body visible in the evening sky.' 'Cicero' refers to the Roman orator because it is associated with descriptions of his consular and literary activities. 'Homer' refers to the author of the Iliad and the Odyssey because this is the description speakers associate with the name. In each case, the reference appears to be mediated by the descriptive content that speakers attach to the name.

One might codify this pattern by the following definition: a proper name refers to an individual if and only if speakers associate a definite description with the name, the individual uniquely satisfies that description, and the reference is determined by the satisfaction of the description. This definition captures the apparent mechanism of the background cases. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises with the name 'Madagascar.' Marco Polo used this name to refer to a portion of the African mainland. Through a geographical misunderstanding, European sailors applied it to the large island off the southeast coast of Africa. The descriptions that current English speakers associate with 'Madagascar' — the large island, the home of lemurs, the former French colony — are satisfied by the island, not by the mainland. The reference has shifted from the original referent to a new one. The original descriptive content no longer determines the reference; instead, the community's subsequent communicative practice has redirected the name to a different object. One might observe that this case reveals a mechanism of reference that the background cases do not make visible: the causal-historical chain of transmission through which a name passes from speaker to speaker, and through which the reference may, on occasion, be redirected by collective usage rather than by the satisfaction of a description.

background_cases: When a clergyman says 'I now pronounce you husband and wife' during a wedding ceremony conducted in accordance with the relevant legal and ecclesiastical requirements, the utterance constitutes the act of marrying the couple; the words do not describe a pre-existing state of affairs but bring a new state of affairs into being.

When a judge says 'I sentence you to five years' imprisonment' in the course of a properly conducted trial, the utterance constitutes the act of sentencing; the words do not report a sentence that has already been imposed but impose it.

When a person says 'I promise to repay you by Friday' in the context of a loan between friends, the utterance constitutes the act of promising; the words do not describe a promise that has already been made but make it.
definition: An utterance U is performative if and only if (i) U is cast in the first person present indicative, (ii) U does not describe a state of affairs but brings one about, and (iii) the felicity of U depends upon the speaker's occupying an appropriate institutional or social role.
conditions: U is cast in the first person present indicative
U does not describe a state of affairs but brings one about
The felicity of U depends upon the speaker's occupying an appropriate institutional or social role
counterexample: Consider a situation in which a group of neighbours agrees informally that whenever anyone waves a white flag from their doorstep, this gesture constitutes a request for assistance. The wave of the white flag is not cast in the first person present indicative — it is not cast in any grammatical form at all, being a non-verbal act. Yet it functions performatively: it does not describe a request but makes one. Its felicity depends upon the shared understanding of the community. The gesture brings about a new state of affairs — the existence of a request — just as surely as the clergyman's words bring about a marriage.
abductive_insight: The background cases are all verbal performatives cast in the first person present indicative — 'I pronounce,' 'I sentence,' 'I promise.' The definition codifies this grammatical form as a necessary condition. Yet the white-flag case reveals that performative force may attach to non-verbal acts — gestures, signals, conventional actions — that bring about new states of affairs through shared social understanding rather than through grammatical form. This suggests that the essential feature of performatives is not their grammatical form but their capacity, within a framework of shared conventions, to constitute rather than describe the states of affairs they concern.
passage:
The utterances most readily identified as performative share a distinctive grammatical form. The clergyman says 'I pronounce you husband and wife.' The judge says 'I sentence you to five years.' The borrower says 'I promise to repay you by Friday.' In each case, the utterance is cast in the first person present indicative, and the words do not describe a pre-existing state of affairs but bring a new one into being. The marriage, the sentence, and the promise come into existence through the utterance itself.

One might codify this pattern by the following definition: an utterance is performative if and only if it is cast in the first person present indicative, it does not describe but brings about a state of affairs, and its felicity depends upon the speaker's occupying an appropriate institutional or social role. This definition captures the shared structure of the background cases. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when one considers non-verbal performatives. Suppose a community of neighbours agrees informally that the wave of a white flag from one's doorstep constitutes a request for assistance. The flag is waved, and a request comes into being — not a request that existed before the wave and is merely reported by it, but one that is constituted by the wave itself. The gesture is not cast in the first person present indicative or in any grammatical form at all. It is a non-verbal act. Yet it functions performatively in precisely the way that the clergyman's words function: it brings about a state of affairs — the existence of a binding request — through a conventional act performed within a shared framework of understanding. One might observe that the definition, by tying performative force to a specific grammatical form, fails to recognise that the capacity to constitute rather than describe may attach to non-verbal conventional acts as well as to verbal utterances.

background_cases: The sentence 'The cat is on the mat' means that the cat is on the mat: speakers use it to describe a particular spatial relation, hearers understand it accordingly, and its truth-conditions are determined compositionally by the meanings of its constituent words and their syntactic arrangement.

The sentence 'Water boils at one hundred degrees Celsius' means that water boils at one hundred degrees Celsius: its meaning is determined by the meanings of its parts, its truth-conditions are publicly accessible, and competent speakers agree on the circumstances in which it is true.

The sentence 'Bachelors are unmarried men' means that bachelors are unmarried men: its meaning is determined by the definitions of its constituent terms, and any competent speaker can verify its truth by reflection on the meanings of the words alone.
definition: A sentence S means that P if and only if (i) the meaning of S is determined compositionally by the meanings of its constituent expressions and their mode of combination, (ii) the truth-conditions of S — the circumstances under which S is true — are fixed by this compositional process, and (iii) any competent speaker of the language is in a position to grasp the truth-conditions of S on the basis of linguistic competence alone.
conditions: The meaning of S is determined compositionally by the meanings of its constituent expressions and their mode of combination
The truth-conditions of S — the circumstances under which S is true — are fixed by this compositional process
Any competent speaker of the language is in a position to grasp the truth-conditions of S on the basis of linguistic competence alone
counterexample: Consider the sentence 'That is expensive.' Its meaning is compositionally determined and its truth-conditions are in principle fixed. Yet a competent speaker cannot determine the truth-conditions of this sentence on the basis of linguistic competence alone, for the sentence contains an indexical ('that') whose reference depends upon the context of utterance, and a context-sensitive predicate ('expensive') whose standard varies with the comparison class. Without knowing what 'that' refers to and what the relevant standard of expense is, the competent speaker cannot grasp the truth-conditions. Conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied, but condition (iii) is not.
abductive_insight: The background cases share a feature that the indexical sentence lacks: each is a sentence whose truth-conditions are fully determined by linguistic meaning without supplementation from the context of utterance. 'The cat is on the mat,' 'Water boils at one hundred degrees Celsius,' and 'Bachelors are unmarried men' are all context-independent in the relevant sense. The sentence 'That is expensive' reveals that truth-conditions may depend upon features of the context — the identity of the referent, the operative standard of comparison — that are not part of the sentence's linguistic meaning. The definition captures the truth-conditions of context-independent sentences but cannot accommodate the pervasive phenomenon of context-sensitivity, in which meaning underdetermines truth-conditions.
passage:
The sentences most commonly used to illustrate compositional semantics share a notable feature: their truth-conditions are fully determined by the meanings of their constituent words and their syntactic arrangement, without supplementation from the context of utterance. 'The cat is on the mat' describes a spatial relation determinately. 'Water boils at one hundred degrees Celsius' states a physical fact whose truth-conditions are fixed by the meanings of the terms. 'Bachelors are unmarried men' is true by definition. In each case, a competent speaker can grasp the truth-conditions on the basis of linguistic competence alone.

One might accordingly define sentential meaning as the compositional determination of truth-conditions, accessible to any competent speaker through linguistic competence. This definition captures the paradigmatic cases and secures a tight connection between meaning and truth-conditions. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises with context-sensitive sentences. Consider the utterance 'That is expensive.' The sentence is compositionally structured; its truth-conditions are in principle fixed. But a competent speaker, encountering this sentence in isolation, cannot determine its truth-conditions. The demonstrative 'that' requires a contextually supplied referent. The predicate 'expensive' requires a contextually supplied standard of comparison — expensive relative to what? Without these contextual inputs, linguistic competence does not suffice to fix truth-conditions. The background cases obscure this difficulty because they are context-independent. It appears that the definition captures the relationship between meaning and truth-conditions for a restricted class of sentences but cannot accommodate the pervasive phenomenon of context-sensitivity, in which compositional meaning determines truth-conditions only in conjunction with features of the context of utterance.

background_cases: When a judge says 'I sentence you to five years in prison,' the utterance constitutes the act of sentencing: the judge has the institutional authority, the setting is appropriate (a courtroom), and the conventional procedure is followed; the words do not merely describe the sentencing but perform it.

When a minister says 'I now pronounce you husband and wife,' the utterance constitutes the act of marrying: the minister has the relevant authority, the setting is appropriate (a ceremony), and the conventional procedures — witnesses, vows — have been observed; the words perform the union.

When an umpire says 'You're out,' the utterance constitutes the act of declaring the batter out: the umpire has the institutional authority, the setting is a baseball game, and the conventional procedure — observing the play and making a ruling — has been followed; the utterance does not describe a pre-existing fact but creates one.
definition: An utterance U is a performative act if and only if (i) U is issued by a speaker with the appropriate institutional authority, (ii) U is issued in the appropriate institutional setting, and (iii) U is issued in accordance with the conventional procedure governing acts of that type.
conditions: U is issued by a speaker with the appropriate institutional authority
U is issued in the appropriate institutional setting
U is issued in accordance with the conventional procedure governing acts of that type
counterexample: Consider a person who, upon seeing a ship on a slipway and feeling that the occasion demands a christening, picks up a bottle of champagne and says, 'I name this ship the Generalissimo Stalin.' The speaker has no institutional authority to name ships. The setting is not an official christening ceremony. No conventional procedure has been followed. All three conditions are violated. Yet the question arises: has the speaker performed a performative act? Intuitively, the answer is no — the ship has not been named. But now consider a case in which a friend says to another, 'I promise to return your book tomorrow.' The friend has no institutional authority (promising requires no institutional authority), the setting is informal (a conversation in a garden), and there is no conventional procedure beyond the utterance of the words 'I promise.' All three conditions appear unsatisfied, yet a promise has plainly been made.
abductive_insight: The background cases — sentencing, marrying, declaring out — share a feature that the promise case lacks: each is embedded in a formal institution with codified authority, designated settings, and explicit procedures. The definition captures institutional performatives perfectly. But promising, apologising, thanking, and many other speech acts are performed without institutional authority, outside institutional settings, and without adherence to any codified procedure. They require nothing more than the speaker's sincerity and the utterance of the appropriate words. The definition conflates the institutional conditions sufficient for a subset of performatives with the conditions necessary for all performatives, and thereby cannot accommodate the large class of non-institutional speech acts.
passage:
The most vivid instances of performative utterances are embedded in institutional frameworks. A judge sentences; the act is performed by virtue of the judge's institutional authority, the courtroom setting, and the conventional procedures of criminal law. A minister pronounces a couple married; the act depends upon the minister's authority, the ceremonial setting, and the observance of prescribed forms. An umpire declares a batter out; the act presupposes the umpire's authority, the baseball game as setting, and the conventional procedures of the sport. In each case, three elements converge: authority, setting, and procedure.

One might accordingly define a performative act as an utterance issued by a speaker with appropriate institutional authority, in an appropriate institutional setting, in accordance with the relevant conventional procedure. This definition captures the institutional performatives with precision. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises with non-institutional speech acts. Consider a friend who says, 'I promise to return your book tomorrow.' The friend has no institutional authority — promising is not an institutional act requiring credentials. The setting is informal — a garden, a kitchen, a street corner. There is no conventional procedure beyond the utterance of the words 'I promise.' All three conditions are violated or inapplicable. Yet a promise has plainly been made, and the friend is bound by it. The background cases suggest that performative force depends upon institutional embedding, but the promise case reveals that many of the most important speech acts operate entirely outside institutions. It appears that the definition captures a species of performative — the institutional — but cannot accommodate the broader genus, which includes acts performed by the mere utterance of the appropriate words, backed by sincerity rather than authority.

background_cases: The predicate 'tall' is vague: there is no precise height at which a person becomes tall, and a series of persons differing by one millimetre can be constructed from a clear case of tallness to a clear case of non-tallness, with no discernible boundary between them.

The predicate 'red' is vague: there is no precise wavelength at which red ends and orange begins, and a series of colour patches with imperceptibly small differences in hue can be arranged from a clear case of red to a clear case of orange, with no sharp boundary.

The predicate 'heap' is vague: there is no precise number of grains at which a collection becomes a heap, and grains can be removed one at a time from a heap without any single removal being identifiable as the one that destroys the heap.
definition: A predicate F is vague if and only if (i) F has borderline cases — objects to which F neither clearly applies nor clearly fails to apply, (ii) the borderline region of F is itself vague — there is no sharp boundary between the clear cases and the borderline cases, and (iii) F is tolerant: sufficiently small differences in the relevant respect never take one from a case to which F applies to a case to which F does not apply.
conditions: F has borderline cases — objects to which F neither clearly applies nor clearly fails to apply
The borderline region of F is itself vague — there is no sharp boundary between the clear cases and the borderline cases
F is tolerant: sufficiently small differences in the relevant respect never take one from a case to which F applies to a case to which F does not apply
counterexample: Consider the predicate 'is a child.' There are borderline cases — a fourteen-year-old might be neither clearly a child nor clearly not a child. The borderline region is itself vague — there is no sharp boundary between the clear children and the borderline cases. But tolerance appears to fail: a difference of one day is sufficiently small, yet there seem to be pairs of consecutive days — say, the day before and the day after a legal threshold — at which the predicate shifts. The legal threshold creates a sharp boundary within what is otherwise a vague predicate. Conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied, but (iii) is violated by the imposition of a legal convention upon a predicate that is semantically vague.
abductive_insight: The background cases — 'tall,' 'red,' 'heap' — share a feature that 'is a child' partially lacks: they are purely semantic predicates unencumbered by legal or institutional conventions that impose artificial boundaries. Their tolerance holds because nothing external to their meaning introduces a sharp cutoff. 'Is a child' is vague in its semantic core but subject to legal stipulation that introduces a discontinuity into what would otherwise be a tolerant predicate. The definition captures the vagueness of predicates whose application is governed entirely by semantic considerations but cannot accommodate predicates in which semantic vagueness coexists with institutionally imposed precision — a hybrid condition more common than the pure cases might suggest.
passage:
The predicates most frequently cited as paradigms of vagueness — 'tall,' 'red,' 'heap' — share three features: they have borderline cases, the borderline region is itself vaguely bounded, and they are tolerant in the sense that a sufficiently small difference in the relevant respect never shifts one from a clear case to a clear non-case. A person one millimetre shorter than a tall person is tall. A colour patch imperceptibly different from a red patch is red. Removing one grain from a heap leaves a heap. The tolerance of these predicates is what generates the sorites paradox: iterated application of the tolerance principle carries one from a clear case to a clear non-case.

One might accordingly define vagueness as the conjunction of borderline cases, higher-order vagueness, and tolerance. This definition captures the three canonical predicates with precision. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises with predicates that are semantically vague but institutionally regulated. Consider 'is a child.' There are borderline cases: a fourteen-year-old may be neither clearly a child nor clearly not a child. The borderline region is itself vague. But in many legal systems, a precise threshold — say, the eighteenth birthday — creates a sharp boundary. On the day before that birthday, the person is legally a child; on the day after, the person is not. The tolerance principle is violated by institutional fiat. The background cases suggest that vagueness and tolerance go together, but 'is a child' reveals that semantic vagueness and institutional precision can coexist in a single predicate. It appears that the definition captures pure semantic vagueness but cannot accommodate the common phenomenon of predicates in which vague meaning is overlaid by conventional stipulation.

background_cases: The sentence 'The cat is on the mat' means that the cat is on the mat: competent speakers use the sentence to convey this state of affairs, hearers recover this content, and the meaning is stable across contexts because the constituent words have fixed conventional meanings that combine according to the rules of English grammar.

The sentence 'Water is a liquid at room temperature' means that water is a liquid at room temperature: the meaning is determined by the conventional meanings of the words and the compositional rules of the language, and it is the same for every competent speaker regardless of context.

The sentence 'Three is a prime number' means that three is a prime number: the meaning is fixed by the conventional meanings of the mathematical and logical terms and their mode of combination, and no contextual information is needed to recover it.
definition: A sentence S means M if and only if (i) competent speakers of the language use S to convey M, (ii) a hearer who understands the language can recover M from S without recourse to information beyond the conventional meanings of S's constituent expressions and their mode of combination, and (iii) the meaning M is the same for every competent speaker in every context of utterance.
conditions: Competent speakers use S to convey M
A hearer can recover M from S using only the conventional meanings of S's parts and their mode of combination
The meaning M is the same for every competent speaker in every context of utterance
counterexample: Consider the sentence 'I am tired.' Competent speakers use it to convey that the speaker is tired. But the hearer cannot recover the full meaning — the identity of the person who is tired — from the conventional meanings of the words alone. The word 'I' has a fixed linguistic meaning (it refers to the speaker), but which person it picks out varies with who utters the sentence. The meaning is not the same for every speaker in every context: when Alice says 'I am tired,' the sentence means that Alice is tired; when Bob says it, it means that Bob is tired. Conditions (ii) and (iii) both fail.
abductive_insight: The background cases share a feature that the indexical sentence lacks: the sentences 'The cat is on the mat,' 'Water is a liquid at room temperature,' and 'Three is a prime number' contain no expressions whose reference shifts with the context of utterance. Their meanings are, as it were, self-contained — recoverable from the words and their combination alone. The sentence 'I am tired' contains an indexical whose reference must be supplied by the context. The definition, drawn from cases in which meaning is context-invariant, cannot accommodate the pervasive phenomenon of context-dependence. What the paradigmatic cases suggest is that the definition captures a special class of sentences — those whose meaning is fully determined by linguistic convention — but not the general case, in which contextual information is essential to recovering what is communicated.

The sentences whose meanings are most easily characterised share a distinctive feature: their content can be recovered from the conventional meanings of their constituent expressions and the rules by which those expressions are combined, without recourse to any information about the context of utterance. 'The cat is on the mat' means that the cat is on the mat, for any competent speaker in any context. 'Water is a liquid at room temperature' means what it means regardless of who utters it. 'Three is a prime number' is the same mathematical claim in every mouth.

One might accordingly propose that a sentence means M if and only if competent speakers use it to convey M, a hearer can recover M from the conventional meanings of the parts and their mode of combination, and the meaning is context-invariant. This proposal captures the paradigmatic cases with admirable economy. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises as soon as one considers sentences containing indexical expressions. The sentence 'I am tired' is among the simplest in the language, and competent speakers use it routinely to convey that they are tired. But the hearer cannot recover the full content — the identity of the tired person — from the conventional meanings of the words alone. The word 'I' has a fixed linguistic rule (it refers to the speaker), but which individual it picks out varies with the context. When Alice utters the sentence, it means that Alice is tired; when Bob utters it, it means that Bob is tired. Conditions (ii) and (iii) both fail. One might observe that the paradigmatic cases, from which the definition was drawn, all involve sentences free of indexicals, demonstratives, and other context-sensitive expressions. The definition captures this special class but cannot accommodate the vast majority of actual utterances, in which contextual information is essential to determining what is meant.

background_cases: The name 'Aristotle' refers to Aristotle: speakers use the name to pick out a particular individual — the philosopher born in Stagira who studied under Plato and wrote the Metaphysics — and the reference is stable across different speakers and different occasions of use.

The name 'London' refers to London: speakers use it to pick out a particular city, and the reference is stable despite the fact that different speakers associate different descriptions with the city (some think of it as the capital of England, others as the city on the Thames, others as the seat of Parliament).

The name 'Everest' refers to Mount Everest: speakers use the name to pick out a particular mountain, and the reference is stable even though the descriptions associated with the name have changed over the course of its history (it was once called 'Peak XV' and described simply as the highest peak in the Himalayas).
definition: A proper name N refers to an individual O if and only if (i) speakers associate with N a cluster of descriptions that uniquely identify O, (ii) O satisfies a sufficient number of the descriptions in the cluster, and (iii) no other individual satisfies as many of the descriptions in the cluster as O does.
conditions: Speakers associate with N a cluster of descriptions that uniquely identify O
O satisfies a sufficient number of the descriptions in the cluster
No other individual satisfies as many of the descriptions in the cluster as O does
counterexample: Suppose that through a series of historical errors, the descriptions commonly associated with the name 'Pythagoras' — discoverer of the theorem bearing his name, founder of a mystical brotherhood, believer in the transmigration of souls — are all in fact true of a different, lesser-known figure, while the historical Pythagoras himself satisfies none of them. The lesser-known figure satisfies all the descriptions in the cluster; Pythagoras satisfies none. On the proposed definition, 'Pythagoras' refers to the lesser-known figure, not to Pythagoras. Yet this seems plainly wrong: when historians speak of Pythagoras, they are speaking of a particular individual, not of whoever happens to fit the associated descriptions.
abductive_insight: The background cases share a feature that the Pythagoras case brings into relief: in each, the reference of the name is in fact secured not by the accuracy of associated descriptions but by a chain of usage stretching back to an original act of naming. 'Aristotle' refers to Aristotle because the name was given to him and has been transmitted through a continuous tradition of usage. 'London' refers to London because the name was assigned to the city and has been passed from speaker to speaker. The descriptions speakers associate with the name are often inaccurate, incomplete, or variable, yet the reference remains stable. The Pythagoras case reveals that when all the associated descriptions are false of the intended referent, the name still refers to him — suggesting that descriptions play a secondary role and that reference is secured by the causal-historical chain of transmission rather than by descriptive fit.

The proper names whose reference is most securely established share a striking feature: their reference remains stable even when the descriptions speakers associate with them are incomplete, variable, or partially incorrect. 'Aristotle' refers to the philosopher born in Stagira, regardless of whether individual speakers think of him as the teacher of Alexander, the author of the Metaphysics, or the founder of the Lyceum. 'London' refers to the city on the Thames, regardless of whether a given speaker associates it with Parliament, with the Great Fire, or with the Tower. 'Everest' refers to the mountain, regardless of the changing descriptions attached to it over the course of its naming history.

One might accordingly propose that a proper name refers to an individual in virtue of the individual's satisfying a sufficient number of the descriptions in a cluster that speakers associate with the name, where no other individual satisfies as many. This proposal captures the intuition that names are connected to their referents through the information speakers possess about them. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when we consider the possibility that all the associated descriptions might be false of the intended referent. Suppose that the descriptions commonly associated with the name 'Pythagoras' — discoverer of a famous geometrical theorem, founder of a mystical brotherhood, advocate of the transmigration of souls — are all in fact true of a different, lesser-known figure, whilst the historical Pythagoras himself satisfies none of them. The lesser-known figure satisfies every description in the cluster; Pythagoras satisfies none. On the proposed definition, the name 'Pythagoras' refers to the lesser-known figure. But this seems plainly wrong: when historians speak of Pythagoras, they mean a particular man, not whoever happens to fit the received descriptions. One might observe that the paradigmatic cases suggest why: the reference of 'Aristotle,' 'London,' and 'Everest' is secured not by descriptive accuracy but by a chain of usage stretching back to an initial act of naming. The Pythagoras case reveals that when descriptions and referent come apart entirely, it is the chain of usage that preserves reference, not the accuracy of the descriptions.

background_cases: When a judge pronounces a defendant guilty, the utterance does not merely describe a state of affairs but brings one about: the defendant is guilty in the eyes of the law because the judge has said so, in the appropriate institutional context, following the appropriate procedures.

When a minister says 'I now pronounce you husband and wife,' the utterance does not report a pre-existing marriage but constitutes the marriage: the couple is married because the minister has said so, in the appropriate institutional context, with the appropriate authority.

When a monarch says 'I dub thee knight,' the utterance does not describe a knighthood but confers it: the person is a knight because the monarch has said so, in the appropriate ceremonial context, with the appropriate authority.
definition: An utterance U is performative if and only if (i) U is not a description of a state of affairs but an act that brings about or constitutes a new state of affairs, (ii) the speaker S has the authority or standing required to perform the act in question, and (iii) the utterance is made in the appropriate conventional or institutional context.
conditions: U is not a description but an act that brings about or constitutes a new state of affairs
The speaker has the requisite authority or standing
The utterance is made in the appropriate conventional or institutional context
counterexample: Suppose a group of children playing a game agree that whoever says 'I claim this territory' while standing on a patch of ground has thereby claimed it for their team. A child stands on a patch of ground and says 'I claim this territory.' The utterance is not a description but an act that brings about a new state of affairs within the game — the territory is now claimed. The child has the requisite standing within the game's rules. The utterance is made in the appropriate context — during the game, on the relevant ground. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet we should hesitate to assimilate this case to the judge's verdict or the minister's pronouncement, for the child's utterance derives its force from a temporary, informal agreement among children rather than from a genuine institution. The definition cannot distinguish performatives backed by genuine institutional authority from those sustained by ad hoc conventions.
abductive_insight: The background cases share a feature that the children's game lacks: in each, the performative utterance derives its force from a stable, publicly recognised institution — the legal system, the institution of marriage, the system of royal honours. These institutions exist independently of the particular participants, are sustained by the wider society, and confer upon the speaker's words a binding force that persists beyond the occasion of utterance. The child's utterance, by contrast, derives its force from a temporary agreement that exists only for the duration of the game and that is binding only upon the children who have agreed to it. The definition, by requiring only 'appropriate conventional or institutional context,' does not distinguish between stable institutions and ad hoc arrangements, and thereby assimilates paradigmatic performatives to cases that lack the characteristic weight and permanence of genuine institutional speech acts.

The utterances we most readily classify as performative share a distinctive feature: they derive their force from stable, publicly recognised institutions that confer upon the speaker's words a binding authority. A judge's verdict derives its force from the legal system. A minister's pronouncement of marriage derives its force from the institution of marriage as recognised by law and custom. A monarch's dubbing derives its force from the system of royal honours. In each case, the utterance does not describe a state of affairs but brings one about, and it does so because the speaker occupies an authorised role within an enduring institution.

One might accordingly propose that an utterance is performative if and only if it constitutes rather than describes a state of affairs, the speaker has the requisite authority, and the utterance is made in the appropriate conventional or institutional context. This proposal captures the shared structure of the paradigmatic cases. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when we consider utterances that satisfy these conditions in the context of temporary, informal arrangements rather than enduring institutions. Suppose a group of children agree upon the rules of a game: whoever says 'I claim this territory' while standing on a patch of ground has thereby claimed it for their team. A child performs the utterance in the appropriate context. It constitutes a new state of affairs within the game. The child has the requisite standing. All three conditions are met. Yet one might observe that the child's utterance lacks the weight and permanence characteristic of the paradigmatic performatives. The judge's verdict alters the defendant's legal status in a way that persists beyond the courtroom and is recognised by the whole of society. The child's claim is binding only upon the participants in the game and only for its duration. The definition, by specifying 'appropriate conventional or institutional context' without distinguishing between enduring institutions and ad hoc agreements, cannot capture the difference between the two cases. The paradigmatic cases suggest that genuine performatives require not merely any convention but the backing of a stable institution with publicly recognised authority — a requirement the definition does not state.

background_cases: When Augustine described learning language as a process of ostension — elders pointing to objects and naming them, the child associating names with the objects pointed at — the account seemed to capture the fundamental mechanism by which words acquire meaning: meaning is the object to which the word is attached.

When Mill distinguished connotation from denotation, he refined the ostensive picture by noting that a name like 'white' not only denotes white objects but connotes the attribute of whiteness; meaning was still tied to a relation between words and features of the world.

When Russell analysed definite descriptions as quantified propositions, he showed that the apparent reference of 'the present King of France' could be dissolved into a complex of existential and uniqueness claims; meaning was ultimately a matter of the proposition's truth-conditions, which are determined by the world.
definition: A word W is meaningful if and only if (i) W stands for or designates an object, property, or relation in the world, (ii) the designation relation between W and its referent is established by convention or stipulation, and (iii) understanding W consists in grasping what W designates.
conditions: W stands for or designates an object, property, or relation in the world
The designation relation between W and its referent is established by convention or stipulation
Understanding W consists in grasping what W designates
counterexample: Consider the word 'but.' It is meaningful — substituting 'but' for 'and' in 'She was tired but she continued' alters the meaning of the sentence. Yet 'but' does not designate any object, property, or relation in the world. It does not name anything. Its contribution to meaning is functional: it signals a contrast between what precedes and what follows. Condition (i) fails entirely, yet 'but' is a meaningful word whose meaning any competent speaker grasps.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that each analyses a word or phrase that functions referentially — a name, a description, a predicate that applies to objects — and the designation model succeeds precisely because these words do refer; but language contains an entire class of words — connectives, prepositions, articles, interjections — whose meaning is constituted by their functional role in sentences rather than by any relation of designation.
passage:
The philosophical analysis of meaning has long been guided by a referential model. Augustine's ostensive picture, Mill's account of connotation and denotation, Russell's theory of descriptions — each takes as its starting point the relation between a linguistic expression and something in the world. A name means its bearer; a predicate means the property it ascribes; a description means whatever uniquely satisfies it. Understanding consists in grasping what the expression designates. This model has produced impressive results: it illuminates how proper names function, how predicates classify, and how descriptions identify.

Yet one might observe that the referential model, successful as it is for names, predicates, and descriptions, cannot accommodate an entire class of words that are undeniably meaningful but that designate nothing. Consider the word 'but.' It is a word that every competent speaker of English understands. Its presence in a sentence makes a difference to what the sentence communicates: 'She was tired and she continued' and 'She was tired but she continued' do not say the same thing. The second sentence, unlike the first, signals that what follows contrasts with what might be expected given what precedes. 'But' is meaningful; its meaning can be taught, learned, and consistently applied. But it does not stand for any object, property, or relation in the world. There is no thing — no entity, no feature, no connection among entities — that 'but' designates. Its contribution to the sentence is functional rather than referential: it indicates a logical relationship between clauses without naming that relationship. The background cases share the feature that each analyses an expression that functions referentially, and the designation model succeeds for precisely this reason. But language is not composed exclusively of referential expressions. Connectives, prepositions, articles, and a host of other functional words constitute the very fabric of discourse, and their meaning is not captured by the designation relation. The definition, by requiring that every meaningful word designate something in the world, cannot account for the meaning of the words that hold our sentences together.

background_cases: When Frege analysed the reference of 'the morning star' and 'the evening star,' he noted that both expressions refer to the same celestial body — Venus — but differ in sense: the mode of presentation associated with each expression is different, and this difference in sense explains how the identity 'the morning star is the evening star' can be informative.

When Russell argued that ordinary proper names are disguised definite descriptions, he maintained that the reference of a name like 'Scott' is secured by an associated description — 'the author of Waverley' — and that understanding the name consists in grasping this descriptive content.

When Strawson argued that the reference of 'the present King of France' presupposes the existence of such a person, he maintained that the sentence 'The present King of France is bald' is neither true nor false when the presupposition fails — that reference, unlike description, carries an existential commitment that must be satisfied for the sentence to have a truth-value.
definition: A singular term T refers to an object O if and only if (i) T is associated in the mind of the competent speaker with a description or set of descriptions that uniquely identify O, (ii) T refers to whatever object, if any, satisfies the associated description, and (iii) if no object satisfies the description, T fails to refer.
conditions: T is associated in the mind of the competent speaker with a description or set of descriptions that uniquely identify O
T refers to whatever object, if any, satisfies the associated description
If no object satisfies the description, T fails to refer
counterexample: Consider a young child who has learned the name 'Einstein' from a picture book. The child associates no uniquely identifying description with the name — she does not possess the description 'the author of the special theory of relativity' or 'the physicist who derived E=mc².' She might associate with 'Einstein' only the vague impression 'the man with wild hair from the picture book.' This description does not uniquely identify Einstein — many people have wild hair. Condition (i) fails. Yet the child's use of 'Einstein' successfully refers to Einstein, because her use is linked, through a chain of communication stretching back to Einstein himself, to the original bearer of the name.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is the assumption that reference is mediated by descriptive content in the mind of the speaker, but the case of the child reveals that reference can be secured by a historical chain of transmission from speaker to speaker, independently of the descriptive content any individual speaker associates with the name — that reference is a social and historical phenomenon, not a purely mental one.
passage:
The descriptive theory of reference, developed through the work of Frege, Russell, and their successors, holds that a name refers to whatever satisfies the description the speaker associates with it. This theory has considerable explanatory power.
