It explains how identity statements can be informative — 'The morning star is the evening star' is informative because the two descriptions present Venus under different modes. It explains how names can fail to refer — 'Vulcan' fails to refer because no planet satisfies the associated description 'the planet between Mercury and the Sun.' And it locates the mechanism of reference in a transparent mental act: the speaker grasps a description and the name refers to whatever fits it.

Yet one might observe that the theory's reliance on descriptive content in the speaker's mind produces difficulties when applied to cases of impoverished or erroneous description. Consider a young child who has encountered the name 'Einstein' in a picture book. The child has no access to any description that uniquely identifies Einstein. She does not grasp that Einstein developed the special theory of relativity, or that he explained the photoelectric effect, or that he was born in Ulm in 1879. She associates with the name only a vague impression — 'the man with funny hair from the book.' This description does not uniquely identify Einstein; it applies to many individuals. On the descriptive theory, the child's use of 'Einstein' either fails to refer or refers to anyone who fits the vague description — neither of which is correct. In fact, the child's use of 'Einstein' refers to Einstein. It does so because the child acquired the name from the picture book, which acquired it from a prior source, which acquired it from a prior source, in a chain extending back to Einstein himself. The reference is transmitted through a historical chain of communication, not through descriptive content. What the background cases share is the assumption that reference is a mental phenomenon — a matter of what descriptions the speaker grasps. The child's case reveals that reference is, at least in part, a social and historical phenomenon: a matter of how the name has been passed from speaker to speaker.

background_cases: When Tarski developed his semantic theory of truth for formalised languages, the truth-conditions of each sentence were specified recursively: the truth of a complex sentence was determined by the truth-values of its components and the logical connectives joining them; the meaning of each sentence was thereby identified with its truth-conditions.

When Davidson proposed a truth-conditional theory of meaning for natural language, he argued that a theory of meaning for a language L takes the form of a Tarskian truth theory: to give the meaning of a sentence is to state the conditions under which it is true, and two sentences with the same truth-conditions have the same meaning.

When logical positivists proposed the verification principle, they maintained that the meaning of a sentence consists in its method of verification — the conditions under which it would be confirmed or disconfirmed; meaning was tied to truth-conditions via the intermediary of observational evidence.
definition: The meaning of a sentence S is its truth-conditions — that is, S means what it does if and only if (i) there is a specifiable set of conditions under which S is true, (ii) understanding S consists in grasping these truth-conditions, and (iii) two sentences with identical truth-conditions are synonymous.
conditions: There is a specifiable set of conditions under which S is true
Understanding S consists in grasping these truth-conditions
Two sentences with identical truth-conditions are synonymous
counterexample: Consider the sentences 'It is raining' and 'It is raining, and either leprechauns exist or leprechauns do not exist.' The second sentence conjoins a tautology to the first. Since a tautology is true in all circumstances, the conjunction is true under exactly the same conditions as the first sentence alone: both are true if and only if it is raining. Their truth-conditions are identical, and condition (iii) entails that they are synonymous. But they are not synonymous: the second sentence makes reference to leprechauns, introduces a disjunction that has no relevance to the weather, and would strike any competent speaker as saying something different — something odd and gratuitous — that the first does not say.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is the assumption that truth-conditions exhaust the meaning of a sentence, but the case of tautological conjunction reveals that two sentences may be true under exactly the same conditions yet differ in what they communicate — in the concepts they invoke, the associations they trigger, and the conversational purposes they serve — suggesting that meaning has dimensions that truth-conditions alone do not capture.
passage:
The truth-conditional approach to meaning has deep roots in the analytic tradition. Tarski showed how to specify truth-conditions recursively for formalised languages. Davidson extended the programme to natural language, arguing that a theory of meaning takes the form of a Tarskian truth theory. The verification principle, in its various formulations, tied meaning to the empirical conditions under which a sentence would be confirmed. These developments converge on a common thesis: that the meaning of a sentence consists in its truth-conditions, and that sentences with identical truth-conditions are synonymous.

Yet one might observe that this thesis yields implausible results when applied to sentences that differ in content but agree in truth-conditions. Consider two sentences: 'It is raining' and 'It is raining, and either leprechauns exist or leprechauns do not exist.' The second sentence appends a tautology — a disjunction that is true regardless of the state of the world. Since the appended conjunct is true under all conditions, its conjunction with the first sentence is true under exactly the same conditions as the first sentence alone. The truth-conditions are identical. Condition (iii) therefore entails that the two sentences are synonymous. But no competent speaker would regard them as synonymous. The second sentence introduces the concept of leprechauns, a concept wholly irrelevant to the meteorological state of affairs. It would be bizarre to respond to the question 'What is the weather?' with 'It is raining, and either leprechauns exist or leprechauns do not exist.' The sentences communicate different things, invoke different concepts, and serve different conversational purposes, despite being true under identical conditions. What the background cases reveal, upon reflection, is not that truth-conditions are irrelevant to meaning but that they do not exhaust it. The meaning of a sentence includes not only the conditions under which it is true but the manner in which it presents those conditions — the concepts it deploys, the structure it exhibits, and the communicative work it performs.

background_cases: The word 'water' means a certain colourless, odourless liquid found in rivers, lakes, and rain — and its meaning can be given by specifying the substance to which competent speakers apply the term; the meaning is, as it were, the thing or kind of thing the word picks out.
The word 'gold' means a certain yellowish metallic element — and its meaning can be given by identifying the substance that competent speakers call 'gold'; the meaning is fixed by the referent.
The word 'tiger' means a certain large striped feline — and its meaning can be specified by pointing to the animals that competent speakers identify as tigers; the referent determines the meaning.
definition: A word W is meaningful if and only if (i) W has a determinate referent — an object, substance, or kind in the world that W picks out, (ii) competent speakers can identify instances of the referent, and (iii) the meaning of W is exhausted by its referent.
conditions: W has a determinate referent — an object, substance, or kind in the world that W picks out
Competent speakers can identify instances of the referent
The meaning of W is exhausted by its referent
counterexample: Consider the word 'but.' It is meaningful — no competent English speaker would deny this. Yet 'but' has no referent: it does not pick out any object, substance, or kind in the world. Competent speakers cannot identify instances of 'but' in the way they identify instances of water or gold. And whatever meaning 'but' has, it is not exhausted by a referent it does not possess. The word's meaning is functional: it marks a contrast between two conjoined propositions.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that they are all natural-kind terms — words that function by picking out substances or species in the world — but the definition generalises from this particular class to all meaningful words, thereby excluding logical connectives, prepositions, conjunctions, and other functional expressions whose meaning is not referential but operational or structural.
passage:
The words 'water,' 'gold,' and 'tiger' each have a clear referent in the natural world, and their meaning can be given by identifying the substance or kind to which competent speakers apply the term. These cases suggest a general theory: a word is meaningful when it has a determinate referent, competent speakers can identify instances, and meaning is exhausted by reference.

Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this referential account may be drawn from the most basic elements of language. Consider the word 'but.' It is as meaningful as any word in English — its absence would impoverish the language considerably. Every competent speaker understands what 'but' contributes to a sentence: it conjoins two propositions whilst marking a contrast or unexpected relation between them. Yet 'but' has no referent. It does not pick out any object, substance, or kind in the world. One cannot point to an instance of 'but' as one might point to an instance of water or a specimen of tiger. Whatever meaning 'but' possesses, it is not exhausted by — nor even partially constituted by — a referent it does not have. The meaning of 'but' is functional and contrastive. One might observe that the background cases — water, gold, tiger — are all natural-kind terms, a particular class of words for which referential theories work well. The definition generalises from this class to all meaningful words and thereby excludes the vast domain of logical connectives, prepositions, conjunctions, and other expressions whose meaning is structural rather than referential.

background_cases: The name 'Aristotle' refers to the ancient Greek philosopher because speakers have inherited the name through a continuous chain of communication stretching back to the original naming — each speaker learned the name from a predecessor who used it to refer to the same individual.

The name 'Shakespeare' refers to the Elizabethan playwright because the name has been transmitted through generations of speakers, each intending to use it as their predecessors did, maintaining a referential chain that links present usage to the original bearer.

The name 'Napoleon' refers to the French emperor because the name has been passed from speaker to speaker through a causal-historical chain originating in the baptismal event or initial dubbing of the individual.
definition: A proper name N refers to an individual I if and only if (i) there is a causal-historical chain of communication linking the current use of N to an initial dubbing of I, (ii) each speaker in the chain intends to use N to refer to the same individual as the speaker from whom they acquired the name, and (iii) no speaker in the chain deliberately redirects N to refer to a different individual.
conditions: There is a causal-historical chain of communication linking the current use of N to an initial dubbing of I
Each speaker in the chain intends to use N to refer to the same individual as the speaker from whom they acquired the name
No speaker in the chain deliberately redirects N to refer to a different individual
counterexample: Consider the name 'Madagascar.' Marco Polo reportedly acquired this name from East African informants who used it to refer to a portion of the mainland coast. Through a communicative error, Marco Polo applied the name to the large island off the coast, and subsequent European speakers inherited his usage. No speaker deliberately redirected the name — the shift was accidental. Yet 'Madagascar' now refers to the island, not to the mainland region to which the original dubbing applied. The causal chain links present usage to a dubbing of the mainland, but the name now refers to the island.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that the causal chain transmitted the reference without distortion — the name arrived at the present speaker with the same referent it had at the origin. But the definition assumes that causal chains always preserve reference faithfully, overlooking cases in which accidental shifts, misunderstandings, or gradual semantic drift redirect a name to a different referent without any speaker intending the change.
passage:
The names 'Aristotle,' 'Shakespeare,' and 'Napoleon' each refer to a specific individual through a causal-historical chain of communication stretching from the present speaker back to the original dubbing. In each case, successive speakers intended to refer to the same individual as the speaker from whom they learned the name, and no deliberate redirection occurred. These cases suggest a causal-historical theory: a name refers to whoever was dubbed at the origin of the chain, provided the chain is unbroken and no speaker has deliberately redirected the name.

Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be drawn from the history of geographical naming. The name 'Madagascar' was reportedly acquired by Marco Polo from East African informants who used it to refer to a portion of the mainland coast — perhaps the region around Mogadishu, or a district further south. Through a communicative error, Marco Polo applied the name to the large island lying off the coast, and subsequent European speakers inherited his usage without correction. No speaker in the chain deliberately redirected the name; the shift was the product of accident and misunderstanding. Yet 'Madagascar' now refers unambiguously to the island, not to the mainland region that was the referent of the original dubbing. The causal chain is intact, no intentional redirection has occurred, but the reference has shifted nonetheless. It appears that the definition, by assuming that causal chains preserve reference faithfully so long as no deliberate redirection occurs, overlooks the possibility of accidental referential drift — cases in which a name comes to refer to something other than what was originally dubbed without any speaker intending the change.

background_cases: The French sentence 'Il pleut' is correctly translated as 'It is raining' in English because the two sentences have the same truth-conditions: both are true just in case rain is falling at the relevant time and place. The translation succeeds by preserving what the sentence says about the world.

The German sentence 'Schnee ist weiss' is correctly translated as 'Snow is white' in English because both sentences are true under exactly the same circumstances — namely, when snow is white. Truth-conditional equivalence secures the adequacy of the translation.

The Spanish sentence 'El gato está en la alfombra' is correctly translated as 'The cat is on the mat' in English because both sentences describe the same state of affairs and are true under exactly the same conditions.
definition: A translation of a sentence S₁ in language L₁ into a sentence S₂ in language L₂ is correct if and only if (i) S₁ and S₂ have the same truth-conditions, (ii) S₂ is a well-formed sentence of L₂, and (iii) S₂ preserves the logical structure of S₁.
conditions: S₁ and S₂ have the same truth-conditions
S₂ is a well-formed sentence of L₂
S₂ preserves the logical structure of S₁
counterexample: Consider the Japanese sentence 'Itadakimasu,' spoken before a meal. It has no precise truth-conditions in the way that 'It is raining' does — it functions as a performative expression of gratitude and social acknowledgement. No English sentence with the same truth-conditions exists, because the original sentence does not straightforwardly describe a state of affairs. Yet the expression is translatable in the sense that competent translators produce adequate renderings — 'I humbly receive this meal,' 'Let us eat,' or a contextual paraphrase. The translation proceeds not by matching truth-conditions but by conveying the pragmatic and social function of the utterance.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that they involve declarative, truth-evaluable sentences describing states of affairs — but the definition generalises from this particular class of sentence to all translation, thereby excluding the translation of performatives, expressives, imperatives, and other utterances whose function is not to describe the world but to do things with words.
passage:
The translations of 'Il pleut,' 'Schnee ist weiss,' and 'El gato está en la alfombra' succeed by preserving truth-conditions: each pair of sentences is true under exactly the same circumstances. These cases suggest a truth-conditional theory of translation: a translation is correct when it preserves truth-conditions, is well-formed, and maintains logical structure.

Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be drawn from the translation of performative expressions. Consider the Japanese utterance 'Itadakimasu,' spoken before a meal. This expression does not straightforwardly describe a state of affairs; it functions as a ritualised expression of gratitude, humility, and social acknowledgement. It has no precise truth-conditions in the way that 'Snow is white' does — one cannot readily specify the circumstances under which 'Itadakimasu' is true or false. Yet the expression is translatable. Competent translators produce adequate renderings — 'I humbly receive this meal,' 'Let us eat,' or a contextual paraphrase adapted to the social situation. The translation proceeds not by matching truth-conditions but by conveying the pragmatic and social function of the utterance. It appears that the background cases involved declarative sentences whose primary function is to describe states of affairs, and that the definition, by generalising from this class, cannot accommodate the vast domain of performative, expressive, and imperative utterances whose translation requires attention to function, context, and social convention rather than to truth-conditions alone.

background_cases: When one understands the meaning of the word 'red,' the understanding consists not merely in an ability to apply the word to red objects, but in grasping its systematic connections to other colour terms — knowing that red excludes blue, that scarlet is a shade of red, that pink results from mixing red and white — so that the meaning of 'red' is constituted in part by its place in a network of related concepts.

When one understands the meaning of the word 'uncle,' the understanding consists not merely in the ability to identify uncles, but in grasping the term's relation to 'aunt,' 'nephew,' 'parent,' and 'sibling' — so that one could not fully grasp what 'uncle' means without understanding the kinship system in which it is embedded.

When one understands the meaning of the word 'cause,' the understanding involves grasping its connections to 'effect,' 'explanation,' 'necessity,' and 'law' — so that the concept of causation is not intelligible in isolation but only as part of a network of related notions that mutually illuminate one another.
definition: A speaker S understands the meaning of a word W if and only if (i) S can correctly apply W to objects or situations in which W is appropriate, (ii) S can provide a verbal definition of W, and (iii) S can distinguish correct from incorrect uses of W.
conditions: S can correctly apply W to objects or situations in which W is appropriate
S can provide a verbal definition of W
S can distinguish correct from incorrect uses of W
counterexample: Consider a child who has been taught to use the word 'triangle' correctly. The child can apply the word to triangular shapes, can define a triangle as 'a shape with three sides,' and can reliably distinguish triangles from non-triangles. All three conditions are met. Yet suppose the child has no grasp of the relationships between 'triangle' and related geometrical concepts — 'side,' 'angle,' 'polygon,' 'square' — and cannot answer questions such as whether a triangle is a kind of polygon, whether a square is a kind of triangle, or what distinguishes a triangle from other three-sided figures that might have curved sides. The child can apply and define the word but does not understand it in the full sense, for the concept remains isolated from the network of related concepts that give it its full significance.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share, and what the definition fails to capture, is the requirement that understanding a word involves grasping its systematic connections to other related words and concepts — that the speaker locate the word within the network of semantic and conceptual relations that constitute its full meaning, rather than merely possessing the ability to apply and define it in isolation.
passage:
The cases in which we are most confident that a speaker understands the meaning of a word reveal a structural feature that simple characterisations tend to overlook. When one understands the word 'red,' the understanding involves grasping its systematic connections to other colour terms — knowing that red excludes blue, that scarlet is a shade of red, that pink results from mixing red and white. When one understands 'uncle,' the understanding requires grasping the term's place in the kinship system — its relations to 'aunt,' 'nephew,' 'parent,' and 'sibling.' When one understands 'cause,' one grasps its connections to 'effect,' 'explanation,' 'necessity,' and 'law.'

One might attempt to capture understanding by requiring that the speaker can correctly apply the word, provide a verbal definition, and distinguish correct from incorrect uses. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the education of children. Suppose a child has been taught to use the word 'triangle' correctly. The child applies it reliably to triangular shapes, defines a triangle as 'a shape with three sides,' and distinguishes triangles from non-triangles without error. Every condition is satisfied. Yet suppose the child has no grasp of the relationships between 'triangle' and related geometrical concepts. The child cannot say whether a triangle is a kind of polygon, whether a square is a kind of triangle, or what distinguishes a triangle from a figure with three curved sides. The concept of 'triangle' remains isolated in the child's mind, disconnected from the network of geometrical relations that give it its full significance. One might observe that what the background cases share — and what the definition fails to require — is that understanding a word involves grasping its systematic connections to other concepts, locating it within the network of semantic relations that constitute its full meaning.

background_cases: When parents name a child at a christening, the act of naming succeeds not merely because a word is uttered with the intention of designating the child, but because the utterance occurs within a recognised social institution — the ceremony of baptism, the registration of births — that confers upon the utterance the power to establish a lasting designation, so that the name is not merely a sound associated with the child but a socially ratified designation with normative force.

When a botanist assigns a binomial name to a newly discovered species, the naming succeeds not merely because the botanist utters a Latin binomial in the presence of a specimen, but because the utterance conforms to the rules of the International Code of Nomenclature, which confers upon the designation its authority and permanence within the scientific community.

When a sovereign state officially names a newly constructed bridge, the naming succeeds not merely because an official utters a name at the dedication ceremony, but because the utterance is performed by a person vested with the appropriate authority and occurs within the institutional framework of public works administration, so that the name becomes the legally recognised designation of the structure.
definition: An act of naming succeeds in assigning a name N to an object O if and only if (i) a speaker utters N with the intention of designating O, (ii) the speaker is in perceptual contact with O at the time of the utterance, and (iii) the speaker has the concept of naming — that is, the speaker understands that the utterance is meant to establish a lasting designation.
conditions: A speaker utters N with the intention of designating O
The speaker is in perceptual contact with O at the time of utterance
The speaker has the concept of naming and understands the utterance establishes a lasting designation
counterexample: Suppose a child, playing alone in a garden, picks up an unusual stone and declares, 'I name you Rupert.' The child utters the name with the genuine intention of designating the stone. The child is in perceptual contact with the stone. And the child possesses the concept of naming — the child understands that the utterance is meant to establish a lasting designation, having observed christenings and the naming of pets. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet if no one else ever learns of or recognises this designation, the name 'Rupert' for this particular stone has no social currency, no normative force, and no permanence beyond the child's own memory. We should hesitate to regard this as a successful act of naming in the full sense.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share, and what the definition fails to capture, is the requirement that the act of naming occur within or be ratified by a recognised social or institutional framework that confers normative force upon the designation — so that the name is not merely a private stipulation but a socially authoritative designation that can be transmitted, recognised, and enforced by others.
passage:
The acts of naming that we most readily recognise as successful share a structural feature that simple accounts tend to overlook. When parents name a child at a christening, the success of the naming depends not merely upon the utterance of a word with the intention of designating the child, but upon the social and institutional framework within which the utterance occurs — the ceremony, the registration of births, the community's recognition of the name. When a botanist assigns a binomial name to a new species, the naming succeeds because it conforms to the rules of the International Code of Nomenclature, which confers authority and permanence upon the designation. When a sovereign state names a bridge at a dedication ceremony, the success depends upon the authority vested in the official who performs the naming and the institutional framework of public administration.

One might attempt to capture this by defining successful naming as an utterance made with the intention of designation, in perceptual contact with the object, by a speaker who possesses the concept of naming. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from childhood play. Suppose a child, alone in a garden, picks up an unusual stone and declares with full solemnity, 'I name you Rupert.' The child utters the name with genuine designative intention. The child holds the stone and is in perceptual contact with it. The child understands what naming is, having observed christenings and the naming of family pets. Every condition is met. Yet if no one else ever learns of or acknowledges this designation, the name 'Rupert' has no social currency, no normative force, and no permanence beyond the child's private memory. One might observe that what the background cases share — and what the definition fails to require — is that the act of naming be embedded in or ratified by a recognised social or institutional framework, so that the designation carries normative force and can be transmitted, recognised, and upheld by others.

background_cases: When FitzGerald translated the Rubáiyát of Omar Khayyám into English, the translation was regarded as a triumph not merely because it conveyed the propositional content of the Persian quatrains, but because FitzGerald found English metres, images, and cadences that reproduced the emotional and aesthetic effect of the original — the haunting melancholy, the carpe diem urgency, the sensuous imagery — so that an English reader could experience something analogous to what a Persian reader would experience.

When Constance Garnett translated Tolstoy's novels into English, the translations succeeded not merely because the plots and characters were faithfully reproduced, but because Garnett captured the moral seriousness, the psychological interiority, and the distinctive rhythm of Tolstoy's prose, enabling English readers to encounter the works with something of the force and depth available to readers of Russian.

When Arthur Waley translated classical Chinese poetry into English, the translations were celebrated not merely for their accuracy of content, but for Waley's ability to find English equivalents for the compression, allusiveness, and imagistic precision of the Chinese originals, so that the translations communicated not only what the poems said but how they achieved their effects.
definition: A translation T of a text X from language L1 into language L2 is successful if and only if (i) T conveys the same propositional content as X, (ii) T is fluent and idiomatic in L2, and (iii) T preserves the referential structure of X — that is, the same objects, persons, and events are referred to.
conditions: T conveys the same propositional content as X
T is fluent and idiomatic in L2
T preserves the referential structure of X
counterexample: Suppose a translator renders a celebrated sonnet from Italian into English. The translation conveys the same propositional content — the same declarations of love, the same descriptions of the beloved — and preserves the referential structure, referring to the same persons and events. The English prose is fluent and idiomatic. All three conditions are met. Yet the translation renders the sonnet as a prose paragraph, stripping away the metre, the rhyme scheme, the enjambments, and the volta that constitute the poetic form of the original. What remains is propositionally accurate but aesthetically inert. The original's power derived not merely from what it said but from how the formal structure enacted and intensified the emotional content. The translation, though meeting every stated condition, fails to be successful in any adequate sense.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share, and what the definition fails to capture, is the requirement that a successful translation reproduce not only the propositional and referential content of the original but also its aesthetic, formal, and affective dimensions — that T communicate something of how the original achieves its effects, not merely what it states, so that the reader of the translation can experience an analogous engagement to that of the reader of the original.
passage:
The translations that have been most celebrated in literary history share a feature that purely content-based accounts of translation fail to capture. When FitzGerald translated the Rubáiyát of Omar Khayyám, the triumph lay not merely in conveying the propositional content of the Persian quatrains but in finding English metres, images, and cadences that reproduced the emotional and aesthetic effect of the original. When Constance Garnett translated Tolstoy, the success consisted not merely in faithful reproduction of plot and character but in capturing the moral seriousness, the psychological depth, and the distinctive rhythm of Tolstoy's prose. When Arthur Waley translated classical Chinese poetry, the achievement was to find English equivalents for the compression, allusiveness, and imagistic precision of the originals.

One might attempt to capture this by defining a successful translation as one that conveys the same propositional content, is fluent and idiomatic in the target language, and preserves the referential structure. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the translation of poetry. Suppose a translator renders a celebrated Italian sonnet into fluent, idiomatic English prose that conveys the same propositional content and preserves the referential structure. The same declarations of love are stated, the same persons and events are referred to, and the English is graceful. Every condition is met. Yet the translation renders the sonnet as a prose paragraph, stripping away the metre, the rhyme scheme, the enjambments, and the volta. What remains is propositionally accurate but aesthetically inert. The original's power derived from the way its formal structure enacted and intensified the emotional content — a dimension entirely absent from the prose rendering. One might observe that what the background cases share — and what the definition fails to require — is that a successful translation reproduce not only what the original states but something of how it achieves its effects, so that the reader may experience an engagement analogous to that of the original's audience.

background_cases: When a parent names a child at a christening ceremony, the name becomes attached to the child through a public act witnessed by the community. Thereafter, the name is transmitted from person to person, each user intending to refer to the same child. The name functions as a rigid designator: it picks out that individual in all circumstances of evaluation.

When a geographer names a newly discovered mountain, the name is introduced by an act of baptism — a public declaration associating the name with the peak. Subsequent users of the name learn it from others who learnt it from the original namer, and each intends to refer to the same mountain. The name designates the mountain rigidly, regardless of how the mountain might change over time.

When a scientist names a newly discovered chemical element, the name is introduced by stipulation and ratified by the scientific community. The name is transmitted through the literature, each user intending to co-refer with the original stipulation. The name picks out the same substance in every possible circumstance.
definition: A proper name N designates an individual I if and only if (i) N was introduced by a public act of baptism or stipulation directed at I, (ii) N has been transmitted through a causal chain of communication from the original act to the present user, and (iii) each user in the chain intends to use N to refer to the same individual as the person from whom he received the name.
conditions: N was introduced by a public act of baptism or stipulation directed at I
N has been transmitted through a causal chain of communication from the original act to the present user
Each user in the chain intends to use N to refer to the same individual as the person from whom he received the name
counterexample: Suppose a mediaeval village has two blacksmiths, one named 'Edmund' and the other named 'Aldric.' Over the course of several generations, through a series of small confusions in oral transmission, the stories and accomplishments of Edmund come to be attributed to Aldric, and vice versa. Each user in the chain intends to refer to the same person as the previous user, but the referential shifts accumulate until the present-day villagers use 'Edmund' to refer to the individual who was originally baptised 'Aldric,' and 'Aldric' to refer to the individual originally baptised 'Edmund.' All three conditions of the definition are nominally satisfied at each link in the chain, yet the name has shifted its reference entirely.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the christening, the geographic naming, the scientific stipulation — all involve clear, well-documented acts of baptism followed by relatively stable chains of transmission. This naturally suggests that public baptism, causal transmission, and co-referential intention suffice for naming. Yet the case of the two blacksmiths reveals that a chain of individually well-intentioned transmissions can, through cumulative small confusions, produce a wholesale shift in reference. Each link in the chain satisfies the conditions, but the chain as a whole drifts from the original referent. The definition cannot detect this drift because it examines each link in isolation and assumes that individually correct intentions guarantee a globally correct outcome.
passage:
The most familiar examples of naming involve clear acts of baptism followed by stable chains of linguistic transmission. When a parent names a child at a christening, the name becomes attached to the child through a public act, and thereafter each person who uses the name intends to refer to the same child. When a geographer names a mountain, the name is transmitted through the cartographic literature, each user intending to co-refer. When a scientist names a chemical element, the stipulation is ratified by the community and preserved through the scientific literature. In each case, a public act of baptism, a causal chain of communication, and co-referential intention at each link appear to suffice for naming.

These cases invite a general characterisation: a name designates an individual when it was introduced by a public act directed at that individual, transmitted through a causal chain, and used at each stage with co-referential intention. Yet a case which tells against this formulation may be drawn from the history of oral traditions. Suppose a mediaeval village has two blacksmiths, Edmund and Aldric. Over several generations, through a series of small confusions in the retelling of stories, the accomplishments of Edmund come to be attributed to Aldric, and those of Aldric to Edmund. Each storyteller intends to refer to the same person as the previous teller, and each link in the chain satisfies the conditions of the definition. Yet the cumulative effect of many small misattributions is that the present-day villagers use 'Edmund' to refer to the individual originally baptised 'Aldric,' and 'Aldric' to refer to the individual originally baptised 'Edmund.' The name has shifted its reference entirely, despite every individual link in the causal chain satisfying the stated conditions. What the background cases share — and what the definition takes for granted — is that the chains of transmission are stable and well-documented, so that small errors do not accumulate. The definition cannot accommodate the phenomenon of gradual referential drift, because it examines each link in isolation and cannot detect a global shift produced by the accumulation of individually imperceptible confusions.

background_cases: When Shakespeare writes 'Juliet is the sun,' the metaphor is understood by recognising a similarity between Juliet and the sun: both are sources of light, warmth, and life. The hearer grasps the intended meaning by mapping features of the sun onto Juliet, guided by the context of the play.

When one says 'The world is a stage,' the metaphor is understood through the recognition that life, like a theatrical performance, involves roles, entrances, exits, and an audience. The hearer identifies the relevant respects of similarity and sets aside the irrelevant ones.

When a critic writes 'The novel is a mirror held up to society,' the metaphor is grasped by recognising that a novel, like a mirror, reflects the features of what is placed before it — in this case, the social world. The hearer understands the metaphor by identifying the respects in which the novel resembles a mirror.
definition: An utterance is metaphorical if and only if (i) it asserts an identity or predication that is literally false, (ii) the hearer can identify respects of similarity between the literal subject and the metaphorical predicate, and (iii) the intended meaning can be paraphrased by a literal statement of the relevant similarities.
conditions: The utterance asserts an identity or predication that is literally false
The hearer can identify respects of similarity between the literal subject and the metaphorical predicate
The intended meaning can be paraphrased by a literal statement of the relevant similarities
counterexample: Suppose a poet writes 'Grief is a cathedral of bone.' The utterance is literally false. But the hearer cannot identify a pre-existing set of respects in which grief resembles a cathedral of bone in the way that Juliet resembles the sun or a novel resembles a mirror. The metaphor does not invite the hearer to map known features from one domain to another; rather, it generates a new way of apprehending grief — as something vast, skeletal, architectural, hollow, sacred — that did not exist before the metaphor was encountered. Moreover, the intended meaning resists paraphrase: any literal restatement of the similarities ('grief is vast, structured, sacred, hollow') fails to capture the particular imaginative fusion that the metaphor achieves. Condition (iii) fails, and arguably condition (ii) is satisfied only after the metaphor has done its creative work, not before.
abductive_insight: The background cases — Juliet as the sun, the world as a stage, the novel as a mirror — are all conventional metaphors whose interpretation proceeds by mapping pre-existing similarities from one familiar domain to another. This naturally suggests that metaphor operates by exploiting recognised similarities and that the metaphorical meaning can be paraphrased literally. Yet 'Grief is a cathedral of bone' reveals that the most creative metaphors do not exploit pre-existing similarities but generate new ones, and their meaning resists literal paraphrase. The inductive generalisation from conventional, easily paraphrasable metaphors to a universal definition of metaphor fails because it mistakes a feature of familiar, domesticated metaphors for a necessary condition of metaphor as such.
passage:
The most commonly discussed examples of metaphor involve utterances whose figurative meaning is recovered by identifying pre-existing similarities between two familiar domains. When Shakespeare says 'Juliet is the sun,' the hearer maps the sun's qualities — radiance, warmth, centrality — onto Juliet. When one says 'The world is a stage,' the hearer recognises that life involves roles, entrances, and exits. When a critic calls a novel 'a mirror held up to society,' the hearer identifies the reflective function that novel and mirror share. In each case, the metaphorical meaning can be paraphrased by a literal statement of the relevant similarities.

These cases invite a general characterisation: an utterance is metaphorical when it is literally false, the hearer can identify similarities between the subjects, and the meaning is paraphrasable in literal terms. Yet a case which tells against this formulation may be drawn from contemporary poetry. Suppose a poet writes 'Grief is a cathedral of bone.' The utterance is literally false, satisfying condition (i). But the hearer cannot identify, in advance of encountering the metaphor, a set of respects in which grief resembles a cathedral of bone in the way that Juliet resembles the sun. The metaphor does not invite the hearer to map known features from one domain to another; rather, it generates a new mode of apprehending grief — as something vast, skeletal, architectural, hollow, and sacred — associations that did not exist before the encounter. Moreover, any attempt to paraphrase the metaphor literally — 'grief is vast, structured, sacred, and hollow' — fails to capture the particular imaginative fusion that the original metaphor achieves. The paraphrase is a diminished substitute, not an adequate restatement. What the background cases share is their domesticated, conventional character: they exploit well-trodden similarities and yield readily to paraphrase. The definition, built upon these cases, fails to accommodate the most creative metaphors, which generate new similarities rather than exploiting existing ones and resist literal restatement precisely because their cognitive contribution is irreducibly metaphorical.

background_cases: The predicate 'tall' applied to adult human beings exhibits vagueness in a characteristic way: there are clear cases (a person of seven feet is tall), clear non-cases (a person of four feet is not tall), and borderline cases in between about which competent speakers disagree. The vagueness admits of degree: a person of six feet two inches is taller, and more clearly tall, than a person of five feet ten inches.

The predicate 'red' applied to colour samples exhibits a similar pattern: a pure scarlet sample is clearly red, a pure blue sample is clearly not red, and there are borderline orange-red samples about which competent speakers hesitate. The vagueness is associated with a continuous spectrum along which no sharp boundary separates the red from the non-red.

The predicate 'bald' applied to human heads follows the same pattern: a person with no hair at all is clearly bald, a person with a full head of hair is clearly not bald, and there are borderline cases in between. The vagueness arises from the absence of a precise threshold in the continuous dimension of hair density.
definition: A predicate F is vague if and only if (i) there exist borderline cases to which neither F nor not-F determinately applies, (ii) the borderline cases arise along a continuous dimension of variation, and (iii) competent speakers of the language disagree about the application of F to the borderline cases.
conditions: There exist borderline cases to which neither F nor not-F determinately applies
The borderline cases arise along a continuous dimension of variation
Competent speakers of the language disagree about the application of F to the borderline cases
counterexample: Consider the predicate 'is a religion' applied to various systems of belief and practice. There are clear cases: Christianity, Islam, and Buddhism are religions. There are clear non-cases: stamp collecting and football are not religions. And there are borderline cases — Confucianism, certain forms of secular Buddhism, various New Age movements — about which competent speakers disagree. Conditions (i) and (iii) are satisfied. Yet the borderline cases do not arise along any single continuous dimension of variation. The dimensions relevant to the application of 'religion' — the presence of a deity, ritual practice, a moral code, a community of adherents, a metaphysical worldview — are multiple, discrete, and not reducible to a single continuum. Condition (ii) fails. Yet the predicate is plainly vague.
abductive_insight: The background cases — 'tall,' 'red,' 'bald' — are all predicates whose vagueness arises along a single continuous physical dimension (height, wavelength, hair density), and this naturally suggests that vagueness requires a continuous dimension of variation. Yet 'is a religion' reveals that vagueness can arise in multi-dimensional and non-continuous domains where the borderline cases are generated by the complex interaction of several discrete criteria rather than by position along a single continuum. The inductive generalisation from paradigmatic sorites-susceptible predicates to a universal definition of vagueness fails because it mistakes a feature of the simplest cases for a necessary condition of vagueness as such.
passage:
The most frequently discussed examples of vague predicates share a common structural feature: their borderline cases arise along a single continuous dimension of variation. The predicate 'tall' has borderline cases arrayed along the continuum of human height. The predicate 'red' has borderline cases distributed along the continuous spectrum of visible light. The predicate 'bald' has borderline cases arranged along the continuum of hair density. In each case, the vagueness is intimately connected with a continuous physical dimension, and the absence of a sharp boundary along that dimension is what generates the borderline cases and the disagreement among competent speakers.

These cases invite a general characterisation: a predicate is vague when it has borderline cases arising along a continuous dimension of variation about which competent speakers disagree. Yet a case which tells against this formulation may be drawn from the philosophy of social classification. Consider the predicate 'is a religion.' There are clear cases — Christianity, Islam, Buddhism — and clear non-cases — stamp collecting, football. And there are borderline cases: Confucianism, certain forms of secular Buddhism, various New Age movements. Competent speakers disagree about whether these are religions. Conditions (i) and (iii) are satisfied. Yet the borderline cases do not arise along any single continuous dimension. The criteria relevant to the application of 'religion' — the presence of a deity, ritual practice, a moral code, a community of adherents, a metaphysical worldview — are multiple, qualitatively distinct, and not reducible to a single continuum. The predicate is vague not because of a missing boundary along a continuous dimension but because of the complex, multi-dimensional character of the concept it expresses. What the background cases share is the coincidence of vagueness with continuity, and the definition mistakes this coincidence for a necessary connection. The predicate 'is a religion' demonstrates that vagueness can arise in domains that are fundamentally non-continuous, where the indeterminacy is generated by the interaction of several discrete criteria rather than by position along a single spectrum.

background_cases: When a speaker utters the sentence 'Snow is white,' the meaning of the sentence appears to be determined by the meanings of its constituent words and their syntactic arrangement. 'Snow' refers to snow, 'is' ascribes a property, and 'white' denotes a colour. The meaning of the whole is a function of the meanings of the parts.

When a speaker utters 'The cat sat on the mat,' the meaning of the sentence is compositionally determined: one grasps the meaning of 'the cat,' of 'sat on,' and of 'the mat,' and the syntactic structure fixes how these meanings combine to yield the meaning of the whole.

When a speaker utters 'Every student passed the examination,' the meaning is determined by the meanings of 'every,' 'student,' 'passed,' and 'the examination,' together with the syntactic relations among them. The quantifier 'every' combines with 'student' to form a quantified noun phrase, and the meaning of the whole follows from the rule for combining a quantified subject with a predicate.
definition: The meaning of any sentence S in a natural language is determined entirely by (i) the meanings of the individual words composing S, (ii) the syntactic rules governing their combination, and (iii) nothing beyond these two factors contributes to the meaning of S.
conditions: The meanings of the individual words composing S determine the meaning of S
The syntactic rules governing word combination determine the meaning of S
Nothing beyond word meanings and syntax contributes to sentence meaning
counterexample: Suppose a speaker, at a dinner party, utters the sentence 'It is a bit warm in here.' The meanings of the individual words and the syntactic structure yield the literal meaning: the temperature of the room is somewhat elevated. But in context, the speaker means something quite different: she is requesting that someone open a window. The sentence's communicative meaning, what the speaker uses it to convey, is not determined by word meanings and syntax alone but depends upon the conversational context, the speaker's intention, and the conventions of indirect communication. Word meanings and syntax deliver only the literal meaning; the full meaning of the utterance requires contextual and pragmatic information.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that they are all examples of literal, context-independent assertions in which what the speaker means coincides with what the sentence literally says. The inductive generalisation that meaning is entirely compositional is an artefact of restricting attention to such cases. In the vast domain of indirect speech, irony, implicature, and context-sensitive communication, the meaning of an utterance exceeds what word meanings and syntax alone can determine, and a purely compositional account of meaning is incomplete.
passage:
The most transparent cases of linguistic meaning appear to support a purely compositional account. When a speaker says 'Snow is white,' the meaning is fixed by the meanings of the constituent words and their syntactic arrangement. When a speaker says 'The cat sat on the mat,' the meaning is similarly determined by the parts and their combination. When a speaker says 'Every student passed the examination,' the quantificational structure and the lexical meanings suffice to fix the meaning of the whole. These cases invite the generalisation that the meaning of any sentence is determined entirely by the meanings of its words and the syntactic rules governing their combination, with nothing further contributing.

Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from everyday conversational practice. Suppose a speaker at a dinner party utters the sentence 'It is a bit warm in here.' The meanings of the individual words and the syntactic structure yield the literal meaning: the temperature of the room is somewhat elevated. But in context, the speaker means something quite different. She is requesting that someone open a window. The communicative meaning of the utterance, what the speaker uses the sentence to convey, is not determined by word meanings and syntax alone but depends upon the conversational context, the speaker's intention, and the conventions of indirect communication. The background cases, by featuring literal, context-independent assertions, encouraged a generalisation that omits the role of pragmatic and contextual factors. In the vast domain of indirect speech, implicature, and context-dependent communication, the meaning of an utterance exceeds what a purely compositional account can deliver.

background_cases: When parents name a child at a christening, the name is introduced by a public ceremonial act in which the child is present and the name is bestowed in the presence of witnesses. The connexion between name and bearer is established by the social act of naming and is thereafter maintained by the community's continued use of the name to refer to that individual.

When a scientist names a newly discovered species, the name is introduced in a publication that describes a type specimen. The connexion between the name and the species is established by the formal act of designation and is maintained by the taxonomic community's adherence to the rules of nomenclature.

When a geographical feature is named, the name is introduced by an act of designation, often inscribed on a map, that associates the name with a particular location. Subsequent users of the name refer to that location by virtue of a chain of transmission stretching back to the original act of designation.
definition: A name N names an object O if and only if (i) N was introduced by a public act of designation that associated N with O, (ii) there exists a continuous social chain of transmission from the original designation to present uses of N, and (iii) present users of N intend to refer to the same object that was designated in the original act.
conditions: N was introduced by a public act of designation associating N with O
A continuous social chain of transmission connects the original designation to present uses
Present users intend to refer to the same object designated originally
counterexample: Suppose that a name was originally introduced for a particular river by early settlers, and the name was transmitted faithfully through generations, with each generation intending to refer to the same geographical feature. Yet over centuries, the river changed its course so dramatically that the body of water presently bearing the name occupies an entirely different valley from the one the settlers named. Present speakers, using the name, intend to refer to the river they see before them, which is the successor of the original river by geographical continuity but occupies a different location and carries different water. All three conditions are satisfied: there was a public act of designation, a continuous chain of transmission, and present speakers intend to refer to the same object. Yet whether the name refers to the original river or to its geographical successor is genuinely indeterminate, and the definition provides no resources for resolving the question.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that the objects named, a child, a species, a geographical feature, remained sufficiently stable over time for the chain of transmission to maintain a determinate reference. The inductive generalisation to a continuous chain as sufficient for reference assumes the stability of the named object. When the object undergoes radical change, the chain of transmission may preserve the name without preserving determinate reference, and the definition fails to specify what degree of continuity in the object is required for the name to track it successfully.
passage:
The most familiar cases of naming share a reassuring structure: a name is introduced by a public act, transmitted through a social chain, and present speakers intend to refer to the original object. A child is named at a christening, a species is designated in a publication, a geographical feature is inscribed on a map. In each case, the object remains sufficiently stable for the chain of transmission to maintain a determinate reference. These cases invite the generalisation that naming consists in a public designation, a continuous chain of transmission, and the intention to refer to the same object.

Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the naming of natural features that undergo radical change. Suppose a name was introduced for a particular river by early settlers, and the name was transmitted faithfully through generations, each generation intending to refer to the same geographical feature. But over centuries, the river changed its course so dramatically that the body of water now bearing the name occupies an entirely different valley, carries different water, and shares with the original river only a tenuous geographical continuity. Present speakers, using the name, intend to refer to the river before them. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied: there was a public act of designation, a continuous chain of transmission exists, and speakers intend to refer to the original object. Yet whether the name refers to the original river or to its present geographical successor is genuinely indeterminate. The background cases, by featuring stable objects, encouraged the assumption that a continuous chain of transmission is sufficient for determinate reference. When the object changes radically, the definition provides no resources for resolving the indeterminacy.

background_cases: When a judge says 'I sentence you to five years in prison,' the utterance is not a description of an action but the performance of one. The words themselves, spoken by the appropriate person in the appropriate institutional setting, constitute the act of sentencing.

When a bride says 'I do' at a wedding ceremony, the utterance is not a report of a mental state but the performance of the act of marrying. The words, spoken by the appropriate person in the appropriate ceremonial context, constitute the act.

When a monarch says 'I hereby dissolve parliament,' the utterance constitutes the act of dissolution. No further action is needed beyond the speaking of the words by the person with the requisite authority in the requisite institutional setting.
definition: An utterance U constitutes a performative speech act if and only if (i) U is spoken by a person with the requisite institutional authority, (ii) U is spoken in the institutional setting appropriate to the act in question, and (iii) the speaking of U itself constitutes the performance of the act rather than being a description of it.
conditions: U is spoken by a person with the requisite institutional authority
U is spoken in the appropriate institutional setting
The speaking of U itself constitutes the performance of the act
counterexample: Suppose two friends are walking together in a park, with no institutional setting and no special authority vested in either party. One says to the other, 'I promise to help you move house next Saturday.' The utterance constitutes a genuine promise: a normative obligation is created by the very act of speaking. The speaking of the words constitutes the performance of the act of promising rather than being a description of it. The third condition is satisfied. Yet neither the first nor the second condition is met: no institutional authority is required to make a promise, and no institutional setting is necessary. The promise is made in an ordinary, non-institutional conversational context by a person with no special authority. Nevertheless, the utterance is a paradigmatic performative speech act.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that they all involve institutionally embedded performatives: acts of sentencing, marrying, and dissolving parliament that require specific authorities and settings. The inductive generalisation to institutional authority and setting as necessary conditions of performative speech is an artefact of selecting exclusively institutional examples. Many performative speech acts, such as promising, apologising, or thanking, require no institutional authority and no special setting, being accomplished by ordinary speakers in ordinary conversational contexts.
passage:
The most striking examples of performative speech acts are embedded in institutional life. When a judge sentences a defendant, the utterance constitutes the act of sentencing. When a bride says 'I do,' the utterance constitutes the act of marrying. When a monarch dissolves parliament, the words themselves perform the act. In each case, the utterance is spoken by a person with the requisite institutional authority in the appropriate institutional setting, and the speaking of the words constitutes the performance of the act rather than a description of it. These cases invite the generalisation that a performative speech act requires institutional authority, an appropriate institutional setting, and the constitutive relation between utterance and act.

Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the most ordinary conversational exchanges. Suppose two friends are walking in a park, with no institutional setting and no special authority vested in either party. One says to the other, 'I promise to help you move house next Saturday.' The utterance constitutes a genuine promise: a normative obligation is created by the very act of speaking the words. The third condition is satisfied, for the speaking of the utterance constitutes the performance of the act of promising rather than a description of it. Yet the first and second conditions are not met: no institutional authority is required, and no institutional setting is necessary. The promise is made by an ordinary person in an ordinary context. The background cases, by featuring exclusively institutional performatives, encouraged a generalisation that is too narrow. Promising, apologising, thanking, and many other performative acts require no institutional authority and no special setting, being accomplished by ordinary speakers in the course of everyday life.

background_cases: The sentence 'Snow is white' means that snow is white: its meaning is given by the conditions under which it is true, and competent speakers grasp those conditions.
The sentence 'Paris is the capital of France' means that Paris is the capital of France: one understands the sentence by knowing the circumstances that would make it true.
The sentence 'Water boils at one hundred degrees Celsius at standard pressure' means what it does because competent speakers can specify the conditions under which it holds.
definition: The meaning of a declarative sentence is its truth-conditions if and only if the sentence expresses a determinate proposition, competent speakers can specify the conditions under which it is true, and the sentence contains no indexical or demonstrative elements.
conditions: The sentence expresses a determinate proposition
Competent speakers can specify the conditions under which it is true
The sentence contains no indexical or demonstrative elements
counterexample: Consider the sentence 'I am tired.' This sentence contains an indexical element — the first-person pronoun 'I' — and so the third condition is not satisfied. Yet it appears that the meaning of this sentence is perfectly well captured by its truth-conditions, once the referent of 'I' is fixed by context. Competent speakers understand the sentence by grasping the conditions under which it would be true of the speaker. The truth-conditional account of meaning applies to this sentence without difficulty. The exclusion of sentences containing indexicals is therefore too restrictive and reflects a limitation of the background cases, all of which happen to be free of context-sensitive elements.
abductive_insight: The background cases — 'Snow is white,' 'Paris is the capital of France,' and the sentence about water — are all eternal sentences lacking indexical or demonstrative expressions. The abductive generalisation treats this absence as a necessary feature. But the indexical sentence 'I am tired' shows that truth-conditions can capture meaning even when context-sensitive elements are present, provided context fixes their referents, revealing that the sample was biased toward context-free sentences.
passage:
From a consideration of sentences such as 'Snow is white,' 'Paris is the capital of France,' and 'Water boils at one hundred degrees Celsius at standard pressure,' one might infer that the meaning of a declarative sentence is its truth-conditions, subject to three requirements: that the sentence express a determinate proposition, that competent speakers be able to specify the conditions under which it is true, and that the sentence contain no indexical or demonstrative elements. Each of the background sentences satisfies these conditions. Each expresses a determinate proposition, its truth-conditions are readily specifiable, and none contains indexical or demonstrative vocabulary. The inference to a general account of meaning as truth-conditions, so constrained, appears well supported.

Yet one should hesitate to endorse the third condition. Consider the sentence 'I am tired.' It contains the indexical 'I,' and so the condition is not satisfied. But the meaning of this sentence is, one may argue, perfectly well given by its truth-conditions: it is true just in case the speaker, at the time of utterance, is tired. Competent speakers understand the sentence by grasping these conditions, with context supplying the referent of the pronoun. The truth-conditional approach to meaning applies here as naturally as it does to the background cases. The exclusion of indexical sentences reflects not a genuine limitation of the truth-conditional approach but rather a contingent feature of the sample from which the generalisation was drawn. All three background sentences happen to be free of context-sensitive elements, and the abductive inference has mistakenly treated this as a necessary condition. The definition must be revised to accommodate the pervasive role of indexicality in natural language.

background_cases: A host says to a guest 'There is coffee in the kitchen,' thereby informing the guest and performing an act of assertion; the utterance is sincere, the speaker believes what she says, and the hearer takes it as a report of fact.
A witness in court says 'I saw the defendant enter the building at nine o'clock,' performing an assertion; she believes her claim, intends it as a factual report, and the court treats it as such.
A meteorologist says 'It will rain tomorrow,' asserting a prediction she sincerely believes; the audience accepts the utterance as an expression of her considered judgement.
definition: An utterance constitutes an assertion if and only if the speaker believes the content of the utterance, the speaker intends the hearer to accept the content as true, and the utterance is made in a context where the speaker is accountable for its truth.
conditions: The speaker believes the content of the utterance
The speaker intends the hearer to accept the content as true
The utterance is made in a context where the speaker is accountable for its truth
counterexample: Suppose that a diplomat, in the course of a delicate negotiation, states 'My government is prepared to make concessions on this point.' She does not believe this — she knows her government has no intention of conceding — but she says it to advance the negotiation. She intends the other party to accept the content as true, and the diplomatic context is one in which she is, at least formally, accountable for the truth of her statements. The second and third conditions are met, but the first is not. Yet the utterance functions as an assertion in every public and institutional sense: it is entered into the record, it shapes the negotiations, and the diplomat may be held to it. The case reveals that sincere belief is not necessary for the performance of an assertion, and that the generalisation from sincere background cases has produced too restrictive a definition.
abductive_insight: The background cases — a host, a witness, a meteorologist — all involve sincere speakers, and the abductive inference treats sincerity as necessary. The diplomatic case reveals that assertions may be performed insincerely while retaining their assertoric force, suggesting that the social and institutional function of assertion is independent of the speaker's private belief.

From a survey of paradigmatic assertions — a host informing a guest, a witness testifying in court, a meteorologist issuing a forecast — one may infer that assertion requires the speaker to believe what she says, to intend the hearer to accept the content as true, and to speak in a context of accountability for truth. In each background case, the speaker sincerely holds the belief expressed, aims at the hearer's acceptance, and occupies a role — host, witness, expert — in which she may be called to account for the accuracy of her claims. The generalisation to a definition of assertion seems natural.

Yet one might construct a case which reveals that the first condition is not necessary. Suppose a diplomat, engaged in a delicate negotiation, states that her government is prepared to make concessions on a disputed point. She does not believe this. She knows, in fact, that her government has no such intention. But she speaks strategically, aiming to advance the negotiation, and she intends the other party to accept her statement as true. The diplomatic context is one in which she is formally accountable for the truth of her pronouncements — should the statement prove false, there will be consequences for her credibility and her government's reputation. The second and third conditions are satisfied. The first is not: she does not believe what she says. Yet the utterance functions as an assertion in every public and institutional sense. It is entered into the diplomatic record, it shapes the course of the negotiation, and the diplomat may be held to it in subsequent discussions. We are compelled to recognise that the definition, drawn from cases of sincere assertion, has treated the speaker's belief as necessary when in fact the assertoric character of an utterance depends upon its public and institutional role rather than upon the speaker's private mental state.

background_cases: The predicate 'tall' admits of borderline cases: a man of middling height is neither clearly tall nor clearly not tall, and competent speakers disagree about whether to apply the predicate.
The predicate 'bald' admits of borderline cases: a man with a moderate amount of hair is neither clearly bald nor clearly not bald, and speakers vary in their willingness to apply the term.
The predicate 'rich' admits of borderline cases: a person of moderate wealth may or may not be called rich, and there is no sharp boundary between the rich and the not-rich.
definition: A predicate is vague if and only if it admits of borderline cases, there is no sharp boundary between its positive and negative extensions, and it applies to observable or perceptible features of objects.
conditions: It admits of borderline cases
There is no sharp boundary between its positive and negative extensions
It applies to observable or perceptible features of objects
counterexample: Consider the predicate 'is a democracy.' This predicate admits of borderline cases — certain political regimes are neither clearly democracies nor clearly not — and there is no sharp boundary between democratic and non-democratic states, for the transition from one to the other admits of many intermediate gradations. The first two conditions are met. Yet the predicate does not apply to observable or perceptible features of objects in any straightforward sense. Whether a state is a democracy depends on its institutions, laws, practices, and political culture — features that are not directly perceptible but must be inferred through analysis. The third condition fails. Nevertheless, the predicate is plainly vague. The generalisation from 'tall,' 'bald,' and 'rich' has imposed a perceptibility requirement that is an artefact of the background cases rather than a genuine feature of vagueness.
abductive_insight: The background predicates — 'tall,' 'bald,' 'rich' — all concern features that are, at least in part, directly observable. The abductive inference treats this as necessary. The predicate 'is a democracy' demonstrates that vagueness arises equally in predicates whose application requires complex inferential judgement rather than direct perception, revealing the evidential base to be too narrow.
