From an examination of vague predicates such as 'tall,' 'bald,' and 'rich,' one might infer that vagueness consists in the admission of borderline cases, the absence of a sharp boundary between the positive and negative extensions, and application to observable or perceptible features. Each of these predicates concerns a feature one can, at least in part, perceive directly: one can see whether a person is tall or bald and can observe the outward signs of wealth. The inference that vagueness is tied to perceptibility may seem natural.

Yet this inference proves too restrictive. Consider the predicate 'is a democracy.' This predicate plainly admits of borderline cases. Certain political regimes occupy an intermediate zone: they hold elections but restrict the franchise, permit opposition parties but harass their leaders, maintain a free press in some domains while censoring it in others. There is no sharp boundary between the democratic and the non-democratic; the transition between them is gradual and admits of many intermediate gradations. The first two conditions are satisfied. But the predicate does not apply to observable or perceptible features in the relevant sense. Whether a state is a democracy depends upon its constitution, its legal framework, its institutional practices, and the complex interplay of political forces — none of which is directly perceptible. The third condition fails. Yet the predicate is manifestly vague. We are thus led to recognise that the generalisation from paradigmatically perceptual predicates has produced a definition too narrow to capture the full range of vagueness. The perceptibility requirement is an artefact of the background sample and not a genuine condition of vagueness.

background_cases: When one says 'Juliet is the sun,' one does not literally believe Juliet to be a celestial body; the utterance invites the hearer to perceive a similarity — warmth, radiance, centrality — between Juliet and the sun.

When one says 'The world is a stage,' one does not literally believe the world to be a theatrical platform; the utterance invites the perception of a similarity between human life and theatrical performance.
When one says 'Time is money,' one does not literally equate temporal duration with currency; the utterance highlights a similarity between the two with respect to their value and expendability.
definition: An utterance is metaphorical if and only if the speaker does not literally believe the propositional content of the utterance, the utterance highlights a similarity between two distinct domains, and the hearer is expected to interpret the utterance by mapping features from one domain onto another.
conditions: The speaker does not literally believe the propositional content
The utterance highlights a similarity between two distinct domains
The hearer is expected to interpret the utterance by mapping features from one domain onto another
counterexample: Suppose a speaker says 'The stone is alive with colour.' She does not literally believe the stone to be a living organism. The hearer is expected to interpret the utterance by understanding that the vibrancy typically associated with living things is being attributed to the stone's appearance. Yet the utterance does not straightforwardly highlight a similarity between two distinct domains — the domain of living organisms and the domain of coloured stones. Rather, it attributes a quality (vivacity, dynamism) to the stone that, strictly speaking, belongs to animate beings, without inviting the hearer to construct an extended mapping between the two domains. The metaphor works by transferring a single salient quality rather than by establishing a structural correspondence between domains. The second and third conditions, as stated, are not satisfied in their full generality, yet the utterance is plainly metaphorical.
abductive_insight: The background cases — 'Juliet is the sun,' 'the world is a stage,' 'time is money' — are all extended structural metaphors inviting systematic cross-domain mappings. The abductive inference treats this as essential. The case of 'the stone is alive with colour' reveals that metaphor may operate through the transfer of a single quality rather than through a structural mapping between domains, showing that the generalisation was drawn from a sample biased toward one particular type of metaphor.
passage:
From such celebrated cases as 'Juliet is the sun,' 'The world is a stage,' and 'Time is money,' one might infer that metaphorical utterances share three features: the speaker does not literally believe the propositional content, the utterance highlights a similarity between two distinct domains, and the hearer is expected to interpret the utterance by mapping features from one domain onto another. Each of these cases involves an explicit identification of two things drawn from different spheres — a person and a celestial body, the world and a stage, temporal duration and currency — and invites the hearer to trace structural correspondences between them.

Yet one should hesitate to treat this as the complete account. Consider the utterance 'The stone is alive with colour.' The speaker does not literally believe the stone to be a living organism; the first condition is satisfied. But the utterance does not straightforwardly highlight a similarity between two distinct domains in the manner of the background cases. It does not invite the hearer to construct an extended mapping between the domain of living organisms and the domain of coloured stones. Rather, it transfers a single salient quality — vivacity, dynamism — from one category to another, attributing to the stone a liveliness that strictly belongs to animate beings. The metaphor operates by the transfer of an isolated quality rather than by the establishment of structural parallels. The second and third conditions, understood as requiring cross-domain similarity and systematic feature-mapping, are not satisfied in their full generality. Yet the utterance is as plainly metaphorical as any in the language. We are led to recognise that the generalisation from celebrated structural metaphors has produced a definition too narrow to accommodate the many metaphors that work through the transfer of a single quality rather than through an elaborate mapping of domains.

background_cases: The French sentence 'La neige est blanche' is adequately translated as 'Snow is white': the truth-conditions are preserved, the grammar is correct, and the translated sentence is readily understood by English speakers.

The German sentence 'Der Himmel ist blau' is adequately translated as 'The sky is blue': truth-conditions are maintained, the English sentence is grammatical, and the translation captures the content without loss.

The Italian sentence 'L'acqua bolle a cento gradi' is adequately translated as 'Water boils at one hundred degrees': the factual content is preserved, the English is well-formed, and the audience grasps the same proposition.
definition: A translation from one language into another is adequate if and only if it preserves the truth-conditions of the original sentence, the result is grammatical in the target language, and the content is accessible to a monolingual speaker of the target language.
conditions: It preserves the truth-conditions of the original sentence
The result is grammatical in the target language
The content is accessible to a monolingual speaker of the target language
counterexample: Suppose one translates the Japanese sentence 'Mono no aware wo kanjiru' as 'One feels the pathos of things.' The English sentence is grammatical; a monolingual English speaker can parse it and grasp something of its content. But the truth-conditions of the original are not straightforwardly preserved, for 'mono no aware' denotes a particular aesthetic and emotional sensibility — an awareness of impermanence and the bittersweet beauty of transient things — that is not captured by the English word 'pathos.' The first condition is, at best, imperfectly satisfied. Yet the translation is widely regarded as the best available rendering and is used by scholars and translators with full awareness of its limitations. It is adequate in the sense that it conveys as much of the original as the target language permits. The definition, by requiring strict preservation of truth-conditions, fails to account for cases in which no perfect truth-conditional match is possible and the best available approximation is the appropriate standard of adequacy.
abductive_insight: The background cases involve simple factual sentences whose truth-conditions are straightforwardly preserved across languages. The abductive generalisation treats strict truth-conditional preservation as necessary. The Japanese case reveals that for sentences expressing culturally specific concepts, no translation can fully preserve truth-conditions, and that adequacy must be understood as the best available approximation rather than as exact preservation — a possibility the background sample, consisting of simple and culturally neutral sentences, fails to anticipate.
passage:
If one considers a range of successful translations — 'La neige est blanche' rendered as 'Snow is white,' 'Der Himmel ist blau' as 'The sky is blue,' 'L'acqua bolle a cento gradi' as 'Water boils at one hundred degrees' — one may infer that adequate translation requires the preservation of truth-conditions, grammaticality in the target language, and accessibility to a monolingual speaker. These cases suggest that translation is, in essence, the transfer of propositional content from one linguistic vessel to another, with truth-conditions serving as the measure of fidelity.

Yet one might construct a case which reveals the insufficiency of this generalisation. Consider the Japanese expression 'mono no aware,' which denotes a distinctive aesthetic and emotional sensibility — an awareness of the impermanence of things and the bittersweet beauty that attends their passing. Suppose one translates a sentence containing this expression into English as 'One feels the pathos of things.' The English sentence is grammatical and accessible to a monolingual English speaker. But the truth-conditions of the original are not straightforwardly preserved: 'pathos' does not capture the full range of feeling encompassed by 'mono no aware,' which includes not merely sympathy or sadness but a refined consciousness of transience as a source of beauty. The first condition of the definition is, at best, only partially satisfied. Yet the translation is widely used and is regarded by scholars as the best available rendering. It conveys as much of the original content as the resources of English permit. We are led to recognise that the definition, inferred from simple factual sentences whose content transfers without loss across languages, has imposed a standard of strict truth-conditional preservation that is unattainable for sentences expressing culturally embedded concepts. Adequacy, in such cases, must be understood as the best available approximation rather than as exact preservation.
